options.B: Errors accumulated with each copy, making diagrams inaccurate
options.C: They were deliberately altered by religious authorities
options.D: They could not include any illustrations or diagrams

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说手抄时每抄一次就会引入错误，到第十份副本时图表可能严重失真。A项（只用拉丁文书写）讲座完全没有提到，属于topic_adjacent。C项（被宗教当局故意篡改）讲座说的是当局压制传播，而非篡改手稿内容，属于wrong_detail。D项（不能包含任何插图）与事实相反——讲座说手抄本里有人体图和星图，只是不准确，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor say allowed astronomers in different countries to build on each other's work?

options.A: They began traveling between Italy and England to meet in person
options.B: Universities started teaching a single standardized curriculum
options.C: They could examine identical printed diagrams with the same numbers
options.D: Governments funded international scientific organizations

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说当意大利和英格兰的天文学家能看到完全相同的印刷图表和相同的数字时，他们才能比较结果、互相借鉴。A项（亲自旅行会面）讲座没有提及，属于topic_adjacent。B项（大学统一课程）讲座未提到课程标准化，属于topic_adjacent。D项（政府资助国际科学组织）讲座完全没有涉及，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Andreas Vesalius and his 1543 anatomy text?

options.A: To argue that Vesalius was the most important scientist of the era
options.B: To explain why medical training improved across all of Europe
options.C: To note that Vesalius published his work in the year 1543
options.D: To give a concrete example of reproducible printed illustrations

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授举维萨里的例子是为了具体说明印刷术带来的可复制插图——让各地医生研究完全相同的解剖图像。A项（维萨里是那个时代最重要的科学家）夸大了教授的意图，教授并未做此评价，属于over_inference。B项（解释欧洲医学训练普遍提升）超出了教授的本意，他只是举例说明可复制插图，并非论证医学训练改善，属于wrong_purpose。C项（指出他在1543年出版）只是复述了讲座中说的内容，而非解释为何提及他，属于literal_restatement。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the spacing effect in long-term memory retention
items.transcript: So, think about how you studied for your last big exam. If you're like most students, you crammed—hours of review packed into one long night. It feels productive. But decades of research in cognitive psychology show there's a far better way, and it has to do with something called the spacing effect. The spacing effect is the finding that we remember information much better over the long term when we spread our study sessions out over time, rather than massing them together in one sitting. The principle goes back to Hermann Ebbinghaus, a German psychologist who, in the 1880s, ran memory experiments on himself using nonsense syllables. He noticed that repetitions spaced across days produced far more durable memories than repetitions crammed into a single session. Here's why it seems to work. Each time you retrieve a memory after some forgetting has begun, your brain has to work harder to pull it back up. That effort actually strengthens the memory. When you cram, there's no forgetting in between, so there's no effortful retrieval—the information feels easy, but it fades quickly. A classic study had two groups learn the same material. One studied in a single block; the other spread the same total time across several days. On a test weeks later, the spaced group dramatically outperformed the crammers. Now, this has huge implications for how we should design courses and study schedules. And next time, we'll look at a related technique—retrieval practice—and how testing yourself, rather than rereading, can make these spaced sessions even more powerful.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why cramming the night before an exam feels productive to students
options.B: A memory principle showing spaced study beats massed study
options.C: The biography of the psychologist Hermann Ebbinghaus
options.D: How nonsense syllables are used in modern memory research

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。讲座主旨是间隔效应——把学习分散到不同时间比集中一次性学习能更好地长期记忆。A项（临时抱佛脚为何感觉有效）只是开头引入的对比，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。C项（艾宾浩斯的生平传记）只是提到他做实验，讲座并非讲他的人生，属于too_narrow。D项（无意义音节如何用于现代记忆研究）误用了讲座中艾宾浩斯实验的细节，且讲座说的是1880年代而非现代，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Hermann Ebbinghaus discover in his experiments?

options.A: That memorizing real words is easier than nonsense syllables
options.B: That memories fade completely within a single day
options.C: That repetitions spread across days produced more durable memories
options.D: That studying with a partner improves memory more than studying alone

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说艾宾浩斯发现跨天间隔的重复比集中在一次的重复产生更持久的记忆。A项（真实单词比无意义音节更易记）讲座没有做这个比较，属于topic_adjacent。B项（记忆在一天内完全消退）夸大且讲座未这样说，属于wrong_detail。D项（与同伴一起学习效果更好）讲座完全没提到合作学习，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does spacing strengthen memory?

options.A: Because studying in one block keeps the information fresh and easy
options.B: Because spreading sessions out reduces the total study time needed
options.C: Because longer study sessions allow more material to be covered
options.D: Because retrieving a partly forgotten memory takes effort that strengthens it

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授解释每次在开始遗忘后再提取记忆，大脑要更费力，这种努力强化了记忆。A项（集中学习让信息保持新鲜简单）实际是教授描述的临时抱佛脚的缺点——信息感觉简单但很快消退，属于reversed_logic。B项（分散学习减少所需总学习时间）讲座说的是同样的总时间，并未说减少时间，属于wrong_detail。C项（更长的学习时段能覆盖更多材料）讲座没有提及覆盖材料量的问题，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: Retrieval practice and how self-testing strengthens spaced study
options.B: How to redesign university courses around the spacing effect
options.C: The details of Ebbinghaus's nonsense-syllable experiments
options.D: Why cramming remains popular despite its known weaknesses

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授在结尾明确预告下次要讲一个相关技术——提取练习（retrieval practice），以及自我测试如何让间隔学习更有效。B项（围绕间隔效应重新设计大学课程）虽然讲座提到了对课程设计的启示，但那是本次讲座的内容，不是预告的下一主题，属于too_narrow。C项（艾宾浩斯实验的细节）是本次讲座已讲过的内容，不是下一主题，属于too_narrow。D项（临时抱佛脚为何仍流行）是本次讲座的引子主题，且歪曲了预告内容，属于reversed_logic。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: how ancient amphitheaters were designed for natural acoustics
items.transcript: Imagine sitting in the very back row of an open-air theater, thousands of years ago, with no microphones, no speakers—and yet you can hear an actor speaking clearly all the way down on the stage. How? That's the remarkable acoustic design of ancient Greek and Roman amphitheaters. Take the theater at Epidaurus in Greece, built in the fourth century BCE. It seats around fourteen thousand people, and even from the topmost row, a whisper or the drop of a coin on the stage can be heard. For a long time, people weren't sure exactly why. One big factor is the shape. These theaters were carved into hillsides in a semicircle, with rows of stone seats rising steeply. That steep, curved slope helps direct sound up toward the audience instead of letting it scatter sideways. But researchers studying Epidaurus found something more surprising. The limestone seats themselves act as an acoustic filter. The rows of stone naturally dampen low-frequency background noise—like the murmur of the crowd or wind—while letting the higher frequencies of a human voice carry through. So the audience hears the speaker, but not the rumble. The builders probably discovered this effect through trial and error, not physics equations. They couldn't have measured sound frequencies the way we do today. Yet they engineered spaces that acousticians still study and admire. It's a powerful reminder that careful observation and refinement, over generations, can achieve results that rival modern engineering.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How modern microphones improved performances in old theaters
options.B: The history of theatrical performances in ancient Greece
options.C: How ancient amphitheaters achieved excellent natural acoustics
options.D: Why limestone was the most common building material in Greece

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座主旨是古代圆形剧场如何通过设计实现出色的自然声学效果——靠形状和石灰岩座位。A项（现代麦克风如何改善老剧场演出）与讲座相反，讲座强调的正是没有麦克风也能听清，属于wrong_topic。B项（古希腊戏剧演出的历史）是相关话题但不是本讲座内容，讲座讲的是声学设计，属于wrong_topic。D项（石灰岩为何是希腊最常见建材）误用了讲座提到的石灰岩，但讲座讲的是其声学作用而非它作为常见建材，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what surprising role do the limestone seats play?

options.A: They reflect sound back down toward the actors on the stage
options.B: They store heat to keep the audience warm during performances
options.C: They amplify low-frequency sounds so the crowd can be heard
options.D: They filter out low-frequency noise while letting voices carry

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说石灰岩座位像一个声学过滤器，抑制低频背景噪音（人群嗡嗡声、风声），却让人声的高频传过去。A项（把声音反射回舞台上的演员）方向错误，声音是被导向观众，属于wrong_detail。B项（储存热量让观众保暖）讲座完全没提到保暖，属于topic_adjacent。C项（放大低频让人群被听到）与事实相反，座位是抑制而非放大低频，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention hearing a coin drop from the topmost row at Epidaurus?

options.A: To illustrate how remarkably effective the theater's acoustics are
options.B: To explain how archaeologists measured the theater's exact size
options.C: To suggest that ancient audiences were unusually quiet and attentive
options.D: To prove that the theater was originally used for musical concerts

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授提到从最高排能听到硬币落地声，是为了生动说明这座剧场声学效果有多惊人。B项（考古学家如何测量剧场尺寸）与硬币例子无关，属于wrong_purpose。C项（暗示古代观众异常安静专注）把声学效果误解为观众行为，属于over_inference。D项（证明剧场最初用于音乐会）讲座没有这样说，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the ancient builders of these theaters?

options.A: They used advanced mathematical formulas to calculate sound frequencies
options.B: They likely achieved their results through observation and gradual refinement
options.C: They deliberately copied acoustic designs from earlier Roman engineers
options.D: They cared more about seating capacity than about sound quality

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说建造者很可能是通过反复试验、而非物理方程获得这种效果，并强调世代的观察和改进能媲美现代工程，因此可推断他们靠观察和逐步改进。A项（用高等数学公式计算声频）与讲座相反，教授明说他们无法像今天这样测量声频，属于reversed_logic。C项（刻意抄袭更早的罗马工程师设计）讲座没有依据，且希腊剧场早于罗马，属于topic_adjacent。D项（更看重座位容量而非音质）夸大且无依据，讲座强调的恰恰是他们实现了卓越音质，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: music
items.topic: the role of improvisation in early jazz
items.transcript: When you listen to a classical symphony, every note has been written down in advance and is played more or less the same way each performance. Early jazz, which emerged in New Orleans in the early twentieth century, worked on a completely different principle: improvisation. Improvisation means the musicians invent their parts in the moment, spontaneously, often without any written score. But here's a common misconception I want to clear up. Improvisation in early jazz wasn't random or chaotic. It was structured freedom. The musicians shared a framework—an agreed-upon chord progression and a familiar melody—and within that framework, each player could embellish, vary, and respond to the others. Think of it as a conversation. One musician plays a phrase, another answers it, building on what came before. The most distinctive early jazz texture was collective improvisation, especially in New Orleans style. Several instruments—say, a cornet, a clarinet, and a trombone—would improvise simultaneously, weaving independent melodic lines that still fit together harmonically. This demanded extraordinary listening. A player couldn't just plow ahead; he had to constantly adjust to what everyone else was doing. Now, why does this matter beyond music history? Improvisation shifted the very idea of what a composer is. In the classical tradition, composition and performance are separate acts. In jazz, the performer becomes a composer in real time, and no two performances are ever identical. That blurring of roles was genuinely radical, and it reshaped how later musicians—across many genres—thought about creativity, spontaneity, and the relationship between a player and a piece of music.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The differences between New Orleans and later styles of jazz
options.B: How classical symphonies are composed and performed today
options.C: Which instruments were most important in early jazz bands
options.D: The nature and significance of improvisation in early jazz

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座主旨是早期爵士乐中即兴演奏的本质（有结构的自由）及其意义（重塑了作曲家概念）。A项（新奥尔良与后来爵士风格的区别）讲座未做风格间的比较，属于too_narrow。B项（古典交响乐如何创作与演奏）只是开头用来对比的引子，不是主旨，属于wrong_topic。C项（早期爵士乐队哪些乐器最重要）只是集体即兴例子中的细节，并非主旨，属于too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did musicians share as a framework for improvising?

options.A: An agreed-upon chord progression and a familiar melody
options.B: A fully written score that each player memorized in advance
options.C: A conductor who directed when each musician should play
options.D: A fixed set of solos performed in the same order each time

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授明确说音乐家共享一个框架——约定好的和弦进行和一段熟悉的旋律，在此框架内各自装饰、变化、回应。B项（事先背好的完整乐谱）与即兴的本质相反，讲座说常常没有书面乐谱，属于reversed_logic。C项（指挥指定何时演奏）讲座没有提到指挥，属于topic_adjacent。D项（每次以相同顺序演奏的固定独奏）与讲座说的没有两次演奏相同相矛盾，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe early jazz improvisation as a 'conversation'?

options.A: To show how musicians respond to and build on each other's phrases
options.B: To suggest that jazz musicians often talked during performances
options.C: To argue that jazz is easier to understand than classical music
options.D: To explain that early jazz lyrics were based on everyday speech

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授用对话作比喻是为了说明音乐家如何回应并在彼此的乐句基础上发展——一人奏一句，另一人接应。B项（暗示爵士音乐家演奏时常聊天）把比喻当字面意思，属于wrong_purpose。C项（论证爵士比古典更易懂）教授没有做这种比较，属于over_inference。D项（解释早期爵士歌词基于日常口语）讲座根本没谈歌词，属于over_inference。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the relationship between composer and performer in jazz?

options.A: The performer follows the composer's written instructions exactly
options.B: Composers gradually stopped writing music for jazz ensembles
options.C: The traditional separation between the two roles breaks down
options.D: Performers must study classical composition before playing jazz

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说在爵士中演奏者实时成为作曲家，作曲与演奏这两个角色的界限被打破，因此可推断传统上二者的分离在爵士中瓦解。A项（演奏者严格遵循作曲家的书面指示）描述的是古典传统，与爵士相反，属于reversed_logic。B项（作曲家逐渐不再为爵士乐团写曲）讲座没有这层意思，属于topic_adjacent。D项（演奏者必须先学古典作曲才能演奏爵士）讲座完全没有此暗示，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how tardigrades survive extreme conditions through cryptobiosis
items.transcript: Picture an animal that can survive being frozen near absolute zero, boiled, dried out completely, blasted with radiation, and even exposed to the vacuum of outer space. It sounds like science fiction, but this creature is real, and it's tiny—less than a millimeter long. We call them tardigrades, or sometimes water bears. The secret to their survival is a remarkable state called cryptobiosis. Cryptobiosis is a condition in which an organism's metabolism slows down to a virtually undetectable level—essentially, it shuts down. The tardigrade isn't dead, but it isn't really alive in the usual sense either. It's in a kind of suspended animation. So how does this work? When a tardigrade's environment dries out, the animal pulls in its legs and curls into a compact, dehydrated ball called a tun. In this tun state, it replaces much of the water in its cells with special protective molecules. One key group is a set of proteins unique to tardigrades that form a glass-like matrix inside the cell. This matrix essentially freezes everything in place, protecting the delicate cellular machinery from damage. The animal can stay like this for years—some have been revived after decades. Then, just add water, and within hours the tardigrade rehydrates and walks away as if nothing happened. Studying cryptobiosis isn't just about a curious little animal. Researchers hope these protective proteins could one day help us store vaccines and biological materials without refrigeration, or even protect human cells. Sometimes the most extreme survivors teach us the most useful lessons.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions by entering cryptobiosis
options.B: Why tardigrades are among the smallest animals on Earth
options.C: How scientists plan to store vaccines without refrigeration
options.D: The discovery of living organisms in outer space

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。讲座主旨是缓步动物如何通过隐生（cryptobiosis）这一状态在极端条件下存活。B项（缓步动物为何是地球上最小的动物之一）只是提到它们小，并非主旨，属于too_narrow。C项（科学家如何无需冷藏储存疫苗）只是结尾提到的潜在应用，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。D项（在外太空发现生命体）误用了讲座提到的太空真空，但讲座并未说在太空发现生物，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is cryptobiosis?

options.A: A state in which an organism's metabolism slows to an undetectable level
options.B: A process by which tardigrades reproduce in dry environments
options.C: A disease that causes tiny animals to lose all their water
options.D: A type of shell that tardigrades grow to protect themselves

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授明确定义隐生是生物体新陈代谢减慢到几乎无法检测的水平、基本上停摆的状态。B项（缓步动物在干燥环境中繁殖的过程）讲座没有提到繁殖，属于topic_adjacent。C项（一种让小动物失去全部水分的疾病）把保护性的脱水状态曲解为疾病，属于wrong_detail。D项（缓步动物长出的一种保护壳）讲座说的是卷成水桶状（tun），不是长壳，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens inside the tardigrade's cells in the tun state?

options.A: The cells fill with extra water to cushion against freezing
options.B: The cells divide rapidly to replace damaged tissue
options.C: Special proteins form a glass-like matrix that holds everything in place
options.D: The cellular machinery is permanently destroyed but later rebuilt

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说在水桶状态下，缓步动物用特殊保护分子替换细胞中的大部分水，其中一类独特蛋白形成玻璃状基质，把一切固定住、保护细胞机制。A项（细胞充入额外的水）与事实相反，是排出水分而非充水，属于reversed_logic。B项（细胞快速分裂替换受损组织）讲座没有提到细胞分裂，属于topic_adjacent。D项（细胞机制被永久破坏后重建）与讲座相反，蛋白质正是为了防止损坏，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention storing vaccines without refrigeration?

options.A: To claim that tardigrade proteins are already used in medicine today
options.B: To argue that vaccines should replace refrigeration in all hospitals
options.C: To note that vaccines currently require cold storage to stay effective
options.D: To give an example of a potential practical application of the research

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授提到无需冷藏储存疫苗，是为了举例说明研究隐生蛋白可能带来的实际应用。A项（声称缓步动物蛋白今天已用于医学）夸大了，教授说的是"有一天可能"，属于over_inference。B项（论证所有医院应用疫苗取代冷藏）逻辑混乱且非教授本意，属于wrong_purpose。C项（指出疫苗目前需要冷藏才有效）只是复述了应用场景隐含的事实，而非解释教授为何提及，属于literal_restatement。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: why we always see the same side of the Moon (tidal locking)
items.transcript: Here's something you might never have questioned: when you look up at the Moon, night after night, year after year, you always see the same face. The same craters, the same dark patches. We never see the Moon's far side from Earth. Why is that? The answer is a phenomenon called tidal locking. Now, a common misunderstanding is that the Moon doesn't rotate at all. That's actually false. The Moon does rotate on its axis—it's just that it rotates exactly once for every orbit it makes around the Earth. One rotation per orbit. Because those two motions are perfectly synchronized, the same side stays pointed toward us the whole time. So why did the Moon end up locked this way? It comes down to gravity and tides. The Earth's gravity pulls slightly harder on the near side of the Moon than the far side, creating a small bulge. Billions of years ago, the Moon spun faster than it does now. But that gravitational tugging on the bulge acted like a brake, gradually slowing the Moon's rotation. Over a very long time, the rotation slowed until it matched the orbital period, and then it stabilized. This isn't unique to our Moon, by the way. Tidal locking is common throughout the solar system. Many of the moons of Jupiter and Saturn are tidally locked to their planets, always showing the same face. So the Moon's familiar, unchanging face isn't a coincidence—it's the predictable result of gravity quietly working over billions of years.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why we always see the same side of the Moon from Earth
options.B: How craters and dark patches formed on the Moon's surface
options.C: The moons of Jupiter and Saturn and their orbits
options.D: How astronomers first photographed the Moon's far side

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。讲座主旨是为什么我们从地球总是看到月球的同一面——即潮汐锁定现象及其成因。B项（月球表面的环形山和暗斑如何形成）只是开头描述月貌时一带而过，讲座并未讲它们的形成，属于too_narrow。C项（木星和土星的卫星及其轨道）只是结尾用来说明潮汐锁定普遍存在的例子，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。D项（天文学家如何首次拍摄月球背面）误用了"月球背面"，但讲座没有讲拍摄，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does the same side of the Moon always face Earth?

options.A: Because the Moon does not rotate on its axis at all
options.B: Because the Moon rotates exactly once for each orbit around Earth
options.C: Because the Earth's gravity has stopped the Moon from moving
options.D: Because the Moon orbits the Earth faster than it rotates

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说月球每绕地球公转一圈就自转恰好一圈，两种运动完全同步，所以同一面始终朝向我们。A项（月球根本不自转）被教授明确指出是错误的常见误解，属于reversed_logic。C项（地球引力已让月球停止运动）讲座说月球仍在自转和公转，并未停止，属于wrong_detail。D项（月球公转比自转快）与事实相反，二者周期相等，属于reversed_logic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what caused the Moon's rotation to slow over time?

options.A: Collisions with asteroids that struck the Moon's surface
options.B: The cooling and shrinking of the Moon's rocky interior
options.C: The Earth's gravity tugging on a bulge, acting like a brake
options.D: The gravitational pull of the Sun on the Earth-Moon system

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说地球引力对月球近侧拉得稍强，形成隆起，引力对这个隆起的拖拽像刹车一样逐渐减慢月球自转。A项（小行星撞击月面）讲座没有提到撞击，属于topic_adjacent。B项（月球内部冷却收缩）讲座未涉及内部冷却，属于topic_adjacent。D项（太阳对地月系统的引力）讲座讲的是地球的引力，不是太阳，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about tidal locking in the solar system?

options.A: It is a rare event unique to the Earth and its Moon
options.B: It is a widespread result of gravity affecting many moons
options.C: It happens only to moons that have stopped rotating entirely
options.D: It occurs more quickly for moons farther from their planet

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说潮汐锁定在太阳系中很常见，木星和土星的许多卫星都被潮汐锁定，可推断它是引力作用于众多卫星的普遍结果。A项（地月独有的罕见事件）与讲座相反，教授强调它很常见，属于reversed_logic。C项（只发生在完全停止自转的卫星上）误解了潮汐锁定——被锁定的卫星仍在自转，只是与公转同步，属于topic_adjacent。D项（离行星越远的卫星锁定越快）讲座没有提供任何关于距离与速度关系的信息，属于over_generalization。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: how the tragedy of the commons explains overuse of shared resources
items.transcript: Imagine a village with a shared pasture—a commons—open to all the local herders. Each herder can graze as many cattle as they like on it, for free. Now, what do you think happens to that pasture over time? This scenario is the heart of a famous idea in economics called the tragedy of the commons. The phrase was popularized by ecologist Garrett Hardin in 1968. The tragedy of the commons describes how a shared resource that's open to everyone tends to get overused and depleted, even when that outcome is bad for everyone involved. Let's walk through the logic with our pasture. For any individual herder, adding one more cow brings a clear personal benefit—more milk, more meat, all theirs. But the cost of that extra cow—the wear on the pasture—is spread across all the herders. So each person, acting rationally in their own interest, keeps adding cattle. Multiply that by every herder, and the pasture gets stripped bare. Everyone loses. The key insight is that individual rationality can lead to collective ruin. This isn't just about cattle. The same logic applies to overfishing the oceans, polluting the air, and depleting groundwater. These are all shared resources where personal gain and the common good pull in opposite directions. Economists and policymakers have proposed solutions—things like government regulation, or assigning private property rights, or community agreements to manage the resource together. We'll examine how well each of those actually works in our next session.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why raising cattle is no longer profitable for village herders
options.B: How an economic concept explains the overuse of shared resources
options.C: The life and career of the ecologist Garrett Hardin
options.D: How governments set rules for managing private farmland

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。讲座主旨是"公地悲剧"这一经济学概念如何解释共享资源被过度使用和耗竭。A项（养牛为何不再赚钱）误用了牧场的例子，讲座并未讨论养牛的盈利问题，属于surface_word_trap。C项（生态学家加勒特·哈丁的生平与职业）只是提到他普及了这个词，讲座并非讲他的人生，属于too_narrow。D项（政府如何为私有农田制定规则）误用了讲座结尾提到的政府监管，但讲座讲的是共享资源而非私有农田，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why does each herder keep adding more cattle?

options.A: Because the personal benefit goes to them while the cost is shared by all
options.B: Because the village requires each herder to own a minimum number of cattle
options.C: Because adding cattle improves the long-term health of the pasture
options.D: Because herders are unaware that the pasture is being damaged

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授解释每多养一头牛，个人获得明确收益（奶、肉归自己），而成本（牧场损耗）由所有牧民分摊，所以每个人都理性地不断增加牛群。B项（村庄要求每个牧民拥有最低数量的牛）讲座没有这种规定，属于topic_adjacent。C项（增加牛群改善牧场长期健康）与事实相反，增加牛群会耗竭牧场，属于reversed_logic。D项（牧民不知道牧场在受损）讲座说他们是理性自利地行动，而非不知情，属于wrong_detail。

questions.stem: According to the professor, which of the following is given as another example of the tragedy of the commons?

options.A: Building too many houses in a crowded city center
options.B: Companies competing to hire the most skilled workers
options.C: Farmers switching from one crop to another for profit
options.D: Overfishing the oceans and depleting groundwater

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说同样的逻辑适用于过度捕捞海洋、污染空气和抽干地下水。A项（在拥挤市中心盖太多房子）讲座没有提到此例，属于topic_adjacent。B项（公司竞争招聘最熟练的工人）讲座没有此例，属于topic_adjacent。C项（农民为利润改种作物）讲座没有此例，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about individuals acting in their own self-interest?

options.A: It always produces the best outcome for the whole community
options.B: It can lead to outcomes that harm everyone, including themselves
options.C: It is the only rational way to manage any shared resource
options.D: It matters less than the total number of people involved

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授指出关键洞见是个体理性可能导致集体毁灭——每个人理性自利却让牧场被耗尽，人人皆输，因此可推断自利行为会损害包括自己在内的所有人。A项（总能为整个社区带来最好结果）与讲座相反，属于reversed_logic。C项（管理任何共享资源唯一理性的方式）夸大且与讲座批判自利的立场相悖，属于over_generalization。D项（不如涉及的总人数重要）讲座没有把自利与人数做这种比较，属于topic_adjacent。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how the invention of oil paint transformed Renaissance painting
items.transcript: If you visit a museum and stand in front of a Renaissance portrait, you might notice how lifelike the skin looks, how the light seems to glow from within. A lot of that effect comes down to a material innovation: oil paint. Before oil paint became widespread in the fifteenth century, European painters mostly used tempera, which is pigment mixed with egg yolk. Tempera has real limitations. It dries very fast—within minutes—so a painter has to work quickly and can't easily blend colors on the surface. The result tends to be crisp but somewhat flat. Oil paint changed everything. Instead of egg, pigment is suspended in a slow-drying oil, usually linseed oil. Because it dries slowly, the painter can blend colors directly on the canvas, creating soft, gradual transitions between light and shadow. This technique is called sfumato—those smoky, almost imperceptible gradations you see in works like the Mona Lisa. Oil also allowed artists to build up thin, translucent layers, called glazes. Light passes through these layers and reflects back, giving paintings a luminous depth that tempera simply couldn't achieve. The Flemish painter Jan van Eyck is often credited with refining these oil techniques in the early fifteenth century. His work shows astonishing detail—individual hairs, the gleam on a metal surface. So a seemingly small change—what you mix your pigment with—gave painters a whole new expressive range, and it reshaped what European art could look like for centuries to come.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the switch to oil paint transformed Renaissance painting
options.B: The biography and major works of the painter Jan van Eyck
options.C: Why egg yolk was used in European painting for centuries
options.D: How museums preserve and display fragile Renaissance portraits

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。讲座主旨是改用油画颜料如何改变了文艺复兴绘画——带来混色、晕涂法和透明罩染等新表现力。B项（扬·凡·艾克的生平与代表作）只是举例提到他改进了油画技法，并非主旨，属于too_narrow。C项（蛋黄为何在欧洲绘画中用了几个世纪）只是用来对比的蛋彩画背景，不是主旨，属于too_narrow。D项（博物馆如何保存和展示脆弱的文艺复兴肖像）误用了开头的博物馆场景，但讲座没讲保存，属于surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a limitation of tempera paint?

options.A: It dries very fast, making it hard to blend colors on the surface
options.B: It fades quickly when exposed to bright museum lighting
options.C: It can only be applied to wooden panels, never to canvas
options.D: It is far more expensive to produce than oil-based paint

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授说蛋彩画几分钟内就干得很快，画家必须迅速作画，难以在表面混色，效果偏平。B项（在明亮馆灯下迅速褪色）讲座没有提到褪色，属于topic_adjacent。C项（只能画在木板上，不能用于画布）讲座没有此说法，属于wrong_detail。D项（比油性颜料贵得多）讲座没有比较成本，属于topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the smoky gradations in the Mona Lisa?

options.A: To give a concrete example of the sfumato technique oil made possible
options.B: To argue that Leonardo was the greatest painter of the Renaissance
options.C: To explain that the Mona Lisa was painted using tempera, not oil
options.D: To describe how the Mona Lisa is displayed in the museum today

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授提到《蒙娜丽莎》中烟雾般的渐变，是为了具体举例说明油画使之成为可能的晕涂法（sfumato）。B项（论证达·芬奇是文艺复兴最伟大的画家）夸大且非教授本意，属于over_inference。C项（解释《蒙娜丽莎》是用蛋彩而非油画所绘）与讲座相反，正是油画才有此效果，属于wrong_purpose。D项（描述《蒙娜丽莎》如今如何展出）讲座没有谈展出，属于wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the change from tempera to oil paint?

options.A: It was a minor change that had little effect on how paintings looked
options.B: A small change in materials greatly expanded artists' expressive range
options.C: It made painting so easy that almost anyone could master it
options.D: It caused painters to abandon detailed, realistic subject matter

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授在结尾说一个看似微小的改变——颜料用什么调和——给了画家全新的表现力，重塑了几个世纪的欧洲艺术，因此可推断材料的小变化极大扩展了艺术家的表现范围。A项（影响甚微的微小改变）与讲座结论相反，属于reversed_logic。C项（让绘画简单到人人都能掌握）夸大，讲座说的是扩展表现力，不是降低难度，属于over_generalization。D项（导致画家放弃精细写实的题材）与事实相反，油画反而让凡·艾克画出惊人细节，属于reversed_logic。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the function of negative space in Japanese ink painting
items.transcript: Look at a traditional Japanese ink painting, and one thing might surprise you: how much of the paper is left blank. To a Western eye trained on busy, edge-to-edge canvases, all that emptiness can feel like an unfinished work. But in this tradition, the blank space is doing real work. There's a concept called ma, which roughly translates as negative space, or the interval between things. Ma isn't an absence; it's an active element of the composition. A painter might suggest an entire misty mountain landscape with just a few brushstrokes in one corner, leaving the rest of the silk untouched. That emptiness reads as fog, as distance, as sky. Consider the famous sumi-e paintings of the artist Sesshu in the fifteenth century. In some of his works, a tiny boat sits on a vast expanse of plain paper, and somehow you understand instantly that the boat is floating on a wide, still lake. The viewer's mind fills in the water. That's the point. The blankness invites participation; you complete the scene yourself. This reflects a broader aesthetic principle in which restraint signals mastery, and suggestion is valued over explicit detail. So the next time you see one of these paintings and think the artist simply ran out of ink, remember that the empty areas were placed just as deliberately as the brushstrokes. In this tradition, what you leave out matters as much as what you put in.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The deliberate use of blank space as an active element in Japanese ink painting
options.B: The life and major works of the fifteenth-century painter Sesshu
options.C: Differences between silk and paper as surfaces for ink painting
options.D: Techniques for mixing ink to create misty mountain landscapes

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是讲日本水墨画中留白（ma）作为构图的主动元素。A 概括了这一主旨。B 只聚焦 Sesshu 这一个例子，too_narrow，没抓住整体概念。C 谈论丝绸与纸张的材质区别，讲座只是顺带提到，属于 wrong_topic。D 把重点错放在调墨技法上，讲座并未讲如何调墨，是 surface_word_trap（借用了 misty mountain 等词）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the concept of ma refer to?

options.A: A specific brushstroke technique used to paint fog
options.B: Negative space, or the interval between things
options.C: A type of silk preferred by classical ink painters
options.D: The signature an artist places in a painting's corner

questions.explanation: 教授明确说 ma 大致译为 negative space，即事物之间的间隔。B 是直接定义。A 把 ma 说成画雾的笔法，混淆了概念，wrong_detail。C 把 ma 说成一种丝绸，topic_adjacent（材质虽相关但讲座没这么定义）。D 把 ma 说成落款，讲座提到 corner 但指的是构图角落而非签名，reversed_logic 式的张冠李戴。

questions.stem: What does the professor say about Sesshu's painting of a boat?

options.A: The boat is surrounded by densely painted waves
options.B: The boat was added by a later artist to fill empty space
options.C: A tiny boat on blank paper makes viewers sense a wide lake
options.D: The boat illustrates how ink fades over several centuries

questions.explanation: 教授说一只小船画在大片空白纸上，观者立刻理解它漂在宽阔静湖上。C 准确复述。A 说船被密集的波浪环绕，与留白相反，reversed_logic。B 说船是后人补画的，讲座没提，topic_adjacent。D 说船体现墨随世纪褪色，讲座虽提到十五世纪但没讲褪色，wrong_detail（篡改了关键事实）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Japanese ink painting?

options.A: It was technically inferior to Western edge-to-edge painting
options.B: Its painters typically ran out of ink before finishing
options.C: Larger paintings are always considered more masterful
options.D: The viewer plays an active role in completing the image

questions.explanation: 教授说留白邀请观者参与，你自己补全画面，暗示观者扮演主动角色。D 是合理推断。A 说它技法上不如西方，讲座并未贬低，over_generalization。B 说画家通常没墨了，教授恰恰说这是误解，reversed_logic。C 说画越大越显功力，讲座说的是 restraint（克制）显功力，topic_adjacent 且方向相反。

items.subject: musicology
items.topic: why a leitmotif unifies a long musical work
items.transcript: If you've ever watched a film and felt a chill the moment a certain melody returns, you've experienced the power of a leitmotif. The term comes from German and means, roughly, leading motif. A leitmotif is a short, recurring musical phrase associated with a particular character, place, or idea. The composer Richard Wagner is usually credited with developing the technique in his nineteenth-century operas. In his massive Ring cycle, which runs about fifteen hours across four operas, Wagner assigned distinct musical phrases to characters, objects, even abstract concepts like fate. When a hero appears, you hear his theme. When a cursed ring is mentioned, its sinister motif creeps in, even if no one on stage says a word about it. Here's what makes this so clever. The motifs don't just repeat; they transform. A character's theme might shift into a minor key when he's in danger, or blend with another character's motif when their fates intertwine. So the music carries information the words alone can't. Over fifteen hours, these recurring threads give an enormous work a sense of unity and memory. The audience starts to recognize the themes subconsciously, and the whole sprawling story feels connected. Film composers later borrowed the idea wholesale. Think of the looming theme that signals a shark, or the march that announces a villain. So a leitmotif is more than a catchy tune; it's a tool for storytelling, weaving meaning through sound across hours of music.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How leitmotifs use recurring themes to unify and tell a long musical story
options.B: The complete plot of Richard Wagner's four-opera Ring cycle
options.C: The history of the German language in classical opera
options.D: Why film music is generally superior to operatic music

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是 leitmotif 如何用反复出现的主题来统一并讲述一部长篇音乐作品。A 概括准确。B 只聚焦 Ring cycle 的情节，讲座并未讲完整剧情，too_narrow。C 谈德语史，只因 leitmotif 是德语词而被借用，wrong_topic。D 比较电影音乐与歌剧孰优孰劣，讲座没有这种价值判断，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a leitmotif?

options.A: The opening overture played before an opera begins
options.B: A short recurring musical phrase tied to a character or idea
options.C: A fifteen-hour cycle of four connected operas
options.D: A technique for translating German lyrics into music

questions.explanation: 教授定义 leitmotif 是与某角色、地点或概念相关联的简短反复乐句。B 是直接定义。A 说是开场序曲，讲座没这么说，wrong_detail。C 把 leitmotif 误当成 Ring cycle 本身，混淆了概念与作品，topic_adjacent。D 说是把德语歌词译成音乐的技法，讲座没这层意思，reversed_logic 式曲解。

questions.stem: What does the professor say the motifs do beyond simply repeating?

options.A: They are sung aloud by characters to explain the plot
options.B: They are always played in the same key for consistency
options.C: They transform, shifting keys or blending with other motifs
options.D: They gradually disappear as the operas progress

questions.explanation: 教授强调动机不只是重复，而是会变形——可能转小调，或与别的动机融合。C 准确复述。A 说由角色唱出来解释情节，讲座恰说音乐传递台词说不出的信息，reversed_logic。B 说总是同一调以保持一致，与 shift into a minor key 矛盾，reversed_logic。D 说动机逐渐消失，讲座没提，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about leitmotifs in film music?

options.A: Film composers adopted a technique pioneered in opera
options.B: Film leitmotifs are too short to convey any real meaning
options.C: Modern films invented the recurring-theme idea independently
options.D: Film music abandoned leitmotifs in favor of pop songs

questions.explanation: 教授说电影作曲家后来整套借用了这个想法，并举鲨鱼主题、反派进行曲为例，暗示这一技法源自歌剧而被电影继承。A 是合理推断。B 说电影动机太短无法表意，与全篇所讲的表意功能相反，reversed_logic。C 说现代电影独立发明了反复主题，与 borrowed 矛盾，reversed_logic。D 说电影放弃了 leitmotif 改用流行歌，讲座没提，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how the axolotl regenerates lost limbs
items.transcript: So imagine losing an arm and then simply growing a new one, bones, muscles, nerves and all, with no scar at all. For us that's science fiction, but for a small Mexican salamander called the axolotl, it's just an ordinary Tuesday. The axolotl can regenerate entire limbs, parts of its heart, and even sections of its spinal cord. The key to this lies in a structure called the blastema. When an axolotl loses a limb, cells near the wound do something remarkable: they revert to a more flexible, immature state, almost like going backward in development. These dedifferentiated cells gather at the wound site and form the blastema, a mass of cells that can then rebuild whatever was lost. What's striking is the precision. The blastema seems to know exactly what part of the limb was removed. Cut off a hand, and it grows back just a hand. Cut at the shoulder, and it regenerates the entire arm, in correct proportion. Researchers think the cells retain a kind of positional memory, a chemical sense of where they belong along the limb. Now, why does this matter to us? Humans share many of the same genes that axolotls use for regeneration; ours just stay switched off. By studying how the axolotl reactivates these pathways, scientists hope to unlock new approaches to healing in human medicine. The humble salamander may be pointing the way toward regenerative therapies we can barely imagine today.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The natural habitat and diet of Mexican salamanders
options.B: How the axolotl regenerates lost body parts and why it matters
options.C: The differences between salamanders and other amphibians
options.D: Why human wounds form scars when they heal

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是蝾螈如何再生失去的身体部位以及对人类的意义。B 概括准确。A 谈栖息地和食性，讲座未涉及，wrong_topic。C 比较蝾螈与其他两栖类，讲座没做此对比，wrong_topic。D 谈人类伤口为何结疤，只是开头一句对比，too_narrow 且非主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the blastema?

options.A: A protective scar that seals the salamander's wound
options.B: A gene that humans and axolotls both carry
options.C: A mass of flexible cells that rebuilds the lost part
options.D: A section of the spinal cord unique to salamanders

questions.explanation: 教授说伤口附近细胞去分化、聚集形成 blastema——一团能重建失去部位的细胞。C 是直接定义。A 说是封口的疤，讲座强调毫无疤痕，reversed_logic。B 把 blastema 误当成基因，混淆概念，topic_adjacent。D 说是蝾螈独有的脊髓段，张冠李戴，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor say happens when an axolotl's limb is cut at the shoulder?

options.A: It regenerates the entire arm in correct proportion
options.B: It grows back only a hand, regardless of the cut
options.C: It forms a scar and the limb never returns
options.D: It regrows a limb that is larger than the original

questions.explanation: 教授说从肩部切断，它会按正确比例再生整条手臂。A 准确复述。B 说不管从哪切都只长出一只手，与原文相反，reversed_logic。C 说结疤且肢体不再长出，与再生主题矛盾，reversed_logic。D 说长出比原来更大的肢体，讲座强调比例正确，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about humans and regeneration?

options.A: Humans can already regrow limbs as axolotls do
options.B: Humans lack any genes related to regeneration
options.C: Studying axolotls is unlikely to help human medicine
options.D: Humans may have untapped regenerative potential in their genes

questions.explanation: 教授说人类拥有许多与蝾螈相同的再生基因，只是处于关闭状态，研究蝾螈或能解锁人类医学新疗法，暗示人类基因中有未被开发的再生潜力。D 是合理推断。A 说人类已能像蝾螈那样再生肢体，与原文相反，reversed_logic。B 说人类完全没有再生相关基因，与 share many of the same genes 矛盾，reversed_logic。C 说研究蝾螈不大可能帮到人类医学，与教授的乐观相反，over_generalization。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: why astronomers use gravitational lensing to see distant galaxies
items.transcript: Here's a strange idea: you can use a galaxy as a magnifying glass. Not a telescope pointed at a galaxy, but the galaxy itself acting as a lens. This is gravitational lensing, and it follows directly from Einstein's general relativity. The core idea is that mass bends space, and light traveling through that curved space follows the bend. So when light from a very distant galaxy passes near a massive object, like a huge cluster of galaxies, that light gets deflected and focused, much as it would through a glass lens. The result can be dramatic. A single background galaxy might appear smeared into arcs, or duplicated into several images arranged around the foreground cluster. Sometimes the alignment is so precise that the distant galaxy is stretched into a complete ring, what astronomers call an Einstein ring. Why is this so useful? Because the lensing magnifies objects that would otherwise be far too faint and distant to study. It lets telescopes peer at galaxies as they existed billions of years ago, in the early universe. In effect, the lens does for free what no human-built telescope could achieve on its own. There's a bonus, too. The amount of bending reveals how much mass is doing the bending, including invisible dark matter. So lensing acts as a cosmic scale, weighing matter we cannot see directly. A natural phenomenon predicted a century ago has become one of the most powerful tools in modern astronomy.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Einstein personally discovered the planet-bending of light
options.B: The engineering challenges of building larger space telescopes
options.C: How gravitational lensing lets astronomers observe distant, faint galaxies
options.D: The chemical composition of clusters of galaxies

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是引力透镜如何让天文学家观测遥远而暗弱的星系。C 概括准确。A 说爱因斯坦亲自发现行星弯曲光线，史实与表述都被歪曲，surface_word_trap（借用 Einstein）。B 谈建造更大望远镜的工程难题，讲座恰说透镜免费做到望远镜做不到的事，wrong_topic。D 谈星系团的化学成分，讲座未涉及，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what causes light to bend in gravitational lensing?

options.A: The brightness of the distant background galaxy
options.B: Reflection off the surface of a glass lens in space
options.C: Magnetic fields generated by the foreground cluster
options.D: Mass bending space, which the traveling light then follows

questions.explanation: 教授依据广义相对论说质量弯曲空间，光沿弯曲的空间前进。D 是直接定义。A 说是背景星系的亮度导致弯曲，因果错位，wrong_detail。B 说是太空中玻璃透镜的反射，讲座用玻璃透镜只是类比而非真有玻璃，surface_word_trap。C 说是前景星系团的磁场，讲座说的是质量与引力，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention an Einstein ring?

options.A: To illustrate an especially precise alignment that lensing can produce
options.B: To argue that Einstein personally observed such a ring
options.C: To explain how telescope lenses are manufactured today
options.D: To suggest that rings are the only outcome of lensing

questions.explanation: 教授提到爱因斯坦环是为了说明当排列极其精确时，远星系会被拉成完整的环，举例展示透镜效果的一种极端形态。A 正确点明目的。B 说是为了论证爱因斯坦亲眼观测过此环，over_inference，教授没这意思。C 说是为了解释望远镜镜片如何制造，wrong_purpose。D 说环是透镜唯一结果，但讲座先说有弧、多重像等多种形态，over_inference 且与文意相悖。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about gravitational lensing as a tool?

options.A: It can reveal the presence of mass that is otherwise invisible
options.B: It works only when astronomers can already see the foreground cluster clearly
options.C: It has fully replaced all human-built telescopes
options.D: It is useful only for measuring the brightness of nearby stars

questions.explanation: 教授说弯曲量揭示了多少质量在弯曲光线，包括看不见的暗物质，透镜如同宇宙秤，暗示它能揭示本不可见的质量。A 是合理推断。B 说只有先看清前景星系团才管用，讲座没提此前提，topic_adjacent。C 说它已完全取代人造望远镜，讲座说它做到望远镜单凭自身做不到的事，并非取代，over_generalization。D 说它只用于测附近恒星亮度，与观测遥远星系、称量暗物质的主旨相反，reversed_logic。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: why the sunk cost fallacy leads to poor decisions
items.transcript: Let's start with a situation you might know. You buy a ticket to a concert, but on the night of the show you feel exhausted and would honestly rather stay home. Yet you drag yourself out anyway, thinking, well, I already paid for it. That feeling is the heart of today's topic: the sunk cost fallacy. A sunk cost is money, time, or effort you've already spent and cannot get back, no matter what you do next. The fallacy is letting those unrecoverable costs drive your future decisions. Economists point out that a rational choice should depend only on future costs and benefits, not on what's already gone. The concert ticket money is spent either way; the only real question is whether going will make tonight better or worse. Here's a clearer example from business. A company has poured millions into a failing project. The sensible move might be to cancel it, but managers often keep funding it, reasoning that stopping now would waste everything already invested. They call this throwing good money after bad. The trap is emotional. We hate the feeling of waste, so we keep going to justify the past. But the past money is gone regardless. So the lesson is this: when you make a decision, ask what's best from this point forward, not how much you've already sunk into it. Learning to ignore sunk costs is one of the simplest ways to think more clearly.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How companies should set ticket prices for concerts
options.B: Why people let unrecoverable past costs distort their decisions
options.C: The best ways to budget money for entertainment
options.D: How to calculate the future profits of a business project

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是人们为何让无法收回的既往成本扭曲决策，即沉没成本谬误。B 概括准确。A 谈公司如何定演唱会票价，讲座没讲定价，wrong_topic。C 谈如何为娱乐编预算，借用了 concert 但非主旨，surface_word_trap。D 谈如何计算项目未来利润，讲座只是举例，too_narrow 且偏题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a sunk cost?

options.A: A future expense that a company has not yet paid
options.B: Money, time, or effort already spent and unrecoverable
options.C: The profit a project is expected to earn later
options.D: The price difference between two competing products

questions.explanation: 教授定义沉没成本是已经花掉且无法收回的金钱、时间或精力。B 是直接定义。A 说是尚未支付的未来开支，与 already spent 相反，reversed_logic。C 说是项目预期利润，topic_adjacent（经济相关但非定义）。D 说是两产品的价差，与定义无关，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor say a rational decision should depend on?

options.A: How much money has already been invested
options.B: The emotional discomfort of feeling wasteful
options.C: Only future costs and benefits
options.D: The total time spent on the project so far

questions.explanation: 教授说理性选择应只取决于未来的成本与收益。C 准确复述。A 说取决于已投入多少钱，正是谬误本身，reversed_logic。B 说取决于浪费的情绪不适，讲座说这是陷阱而非依据，wrong_detail。D 说取决于已花的总时间，同属沉没成本，reversed_logic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about avoiding the sunk cost fallacy?

options.A: It requires recovering the money already spent
options.B: It is impossible for managers to ever achieve
options.C: It means focusing on what is best going forward
options.D: It only applies to decisions about concerts

questions.explanation: 教授总结说做决定时要问从此刻往前看怎样最好，而非已投入多少，暗示避开谬误就是着眼于未来最优。C 是合理推断。A 说需先收回已花的钱，但沉没成本本就收不回，reversed_logic。B 说管理者永远做不到，讲座说这是最简单的清晰思考方式之一，over_generalization。D 说只适用于演唱会决策，讲座还举了商业例子，too_narrow。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how children acquire grammar through overregularization
items.transcript: Picture a three-year-old who has been saying the word went correctly for months, and then one day starts saying goed instead. Parents sometimes worry their child is getting worse at language. In fact, the opposite is true. This is a phenomenon linguists call overregularization, and it's actually a sign of deep learning. Here's what's happening. Early on, a child learns words like went and ate as separate, memorized chunks, one item at a time. But at some point, the child unconsciously discovers a rule: to make a past tense in English, you add an -ed ending. Once that rule clicks, the child applies it everywhere, even to irregular verbs that don't follow it. So went becomes goed, and ate becomes eated. The striking thing is that the child has never heard an adult say goed. The error can't be imitation; it must be generated from an internal rule the child built independently. Researchers have charted this as a U-shaped curve. First the child is correct, using memorized forms. Then accuracy dips as the overgeneralized rule takes over. Finally, the child sorts out the exceptions and returns to correct usage. This pattern is powerful evidence that children don't just parrot what they hear; they actively construct a grammar in their minds and test it against the language around them. The mistakes, in other words, are a window into a mind busily working out the rules.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why some children learn to speak earlier than others
options.B: How parents should correct their children's speech errors
options.C: How a certain grammar error reveals children actively build rules
options.D: The differences between English and other languages' verb tenses

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是一种语法错误（过度规则化）如何揭示儿童主动构建规则。C 概括准确。A 谈为何有些孩子说话更早，讲座未涉及，wrong_topic。B 谈家长该如何纠错，讲座说家长担心是多余的，并未给纠错建议，surface_word_trap。D 谈英语与其他语言时态差异，讲座只讲英语，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why is saying 'goed' significant?

options.A: It shows the child is forgetting words learned earlier
options.B: It proves the child heard an adult use that form
options.C: It indicates the child has a speech disorder
options.D: It shows the child generated the form from an internal rule

questions.explanation: 教授说孩子从没听大人说过 goed，所以这不可能是模仿，必定源自孩子独立构建的内在规则。D 准确点出意义。A 说表明孩子在遗忘早学的词，讲座说这是深度学习而非退步，wrong_detail。B 说证明孩子听大人说过该形式，与 never heard 直接矛盾，reversed_logic。C 说表明有语言障碍，讲座明确否定这种担心，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe a U-shaped curve?

options.A: To show how accuracy falls and then recovers as the rule is mastered
options.B: To claim that all children learn at exactly the same pace
options.C: To list the vocabulary words a typical three-year-old knows
options.D: To prove that irregular verbs are harder than regular ones

questions.explanation: 教授用 U 形曲线刻画准确率先正确、再因过度规则化下滑、最后理清例外回到正确的过程。A 正确点明目的。B 说是为论证所有孩子节奏完全一致，over_inference，讲座没这么说。C 说是为列出三岁孩子的词汇，wrong_purpose。D 说是为证明不规则动词比规则动词更难，literal_restatement 式的偏离，曲线讲的是学习轨迹而非难度比较。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how children learn language?

options.A: They simply imitate the speech they hear around them
options.B: They actively construct and test grammar rules in their minds
options.C: They cannot learn irregular verbs until adulthood
options.D: They learn grammar mainly through formal correction

questions.explanation: 教授说错误是窥见孩子大脑忙于推导规则的窗口，暗示儿童主动构建并检验语法规则。B 是合理推断。A 说孩子只是模仿，讲座明确说不只是鹦鹉学舌，reversed_logic。C 说成年前学不会不规则动词，讲座说孩子最终会理清例外，over_generalization。D 说主要靠正式纠正学语法，讲座强调内在规则自主生成，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how index fossils help geologists date rock layers
items.transcript: Suppose you find two rock layers, one in Italy and one in Canada, thousands of miles apart. How could you tell whether they formed at the same time in Earth's history? Geologists have a clever tool for this: index fossils. An index fossil is the preserved remains of an organism that lived during a short, well-defined span of time but was spread across a wide geographic area. Those two traits are what make it useful. Think about it. If a species existed for only a few million years, then any rock containing its fossil must have formed during that same narrow window. And if that species lived all over the world, you can match up rock layers on different continents just by finding the same fossil in them. A classic example is the trilobite, a marine creature that scuttled across ancient sea floors. Different trilobite species existed at different periods, so a particular trilobite acts like a time stamp pressed into the rock. Find that species, and you know the layer's age. Notice the contrast with a creature like the horseshoe crab, which has barely changed over hundreds of millions of years. It would make a poor index fossil precisely because it can't pin down a specific time. So the best index fossils are common, widespread, easy to identify, and, crucially, short-lived as a species. They let geologists read the calendar of the rocks.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How geologists use certain fossils to date rock layers
options.B: The anatomy and behavior of ancient trilobites
options.C: Why some marine creatures went extinct long ago
options.D: How rocks form on the ocean floor over time

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是地质学家如何用某些化石（标准化石）为岩层定年。A 概括准确。B 只讲三叶虫的解剖与行为，是举例细节，too_narrow。C 谈海洋生物为何灭绝，讲座未讨论灭绝原因，wrong_topic。D 谈岩石如何在洋底形成，借用了海洋元素但非主旨，surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what two traits make a good index fossil?

options.A: A long-lived species that stayed in one small region
options.B: A creature that is rare and difficult to identify
options.C: A species that changed very little over time
options.D: A short-lived species that was geographically widespread

questions.explanation: 教授说标准化石的两个有用特征是存在时间短、地理分布广。D 准确复述。A 说存在时间长且局限一地，与两个特征都相反，reversed_logic。B 说罕见且难以辨认，讲座说最好是常见易识别的，reversed_logic。C 说几乎不随时间变化，讲座以鲎为例说明这种生物恰恰是糟糕的标准化石，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: What does the professor say about the horseshoe crab?

options.A: It is one of the best index fossils available
options.B: It makes a poor index fossil because it changed little
options.C: It lived for only a few million years
options.D: It is found only on a single continent

questions.explanation: 教授说鲎几亿年来几乎没变，因此无法锁定具体时间，是糟糕的标准化石。B 准确复述。A 说它是最好的标准化石之一，与原文相反，reversed_logic。C 说它只存活了几百万年，恰恰相反，它存续了几亿年，reversed_logic。D 说它只见于单一大陆，讲座没这么说，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about matching rock layers on different continents?

options.A: It is impossible to compare layers that far apart
options.B: It requires digging the layers up and moving them together
options.C: Finding the same index fossil suggests the layers are the same age
options.D: Only the thickness of a layer can reveal its true age

questions.explanation: 教授说若某物种遍布全球，只要在不同大陆的岩层中找到同一化石就能把它们对应起来，暗示找到相同标准化石即提示岩层同龄。C 是合理推断。A 说相隔太远无法比较，与讲座开头设问的解决方案相反，reversed_logic。B 说需把岩层挖出搬到一起，荒谬且讲座没提，topic_adjacent。D 说只有厚度能揭示真实年龄，讲座说的是化石而非厚度，over_generalization。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how the ethnographic method shapes cultural understanding
items.transcript: How do you really come to understand a culture that isn't your own? You could send out a survey, but a questionnaire only captures what people say, not what they actually do. Anthropologists developed a different approach called ethnography, and at its core is a method known as participant observation. The idea is simple but demanding: the researcher lives within a community for an extended period, often a year or more, taking part in everyday life while carefully observing it. The anthropologist Bronisław Malinowski is often credited with pioneering this approach in the early twentieth century. Stranded on a group of Pacific islands during the First World War, he ended up living among the islanders, learning their language, and joining their daily routines rather than observing from a distance. Why does living there matter so much? Because so much of culture is tacit. People follow rules they could never articulate in an interview; they have habits and meanings they take completely for granted. By participating, the researcher can notice the gap between what people claim and what they really do. There's a tension built into the method, though. The researcher must be involved enough to understand from the inside, yet detached enough to analyze clearly. Get too close, and you lose perspective; stay too distant, and you miss the meaning. This balancing act is what makes ethnography both difficult and revealing, and it remains central to how anthropologists study human life.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The biography of the anthropologist Bronisław Malinowski
options.B: Why surveys are the best way to study foreign cultures
options.C: How Pacific islanders lived during the First World War
options.D: An ethnographic method for understanding a culture from within

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是用民族志方法（参与式观察）从内部理解一种文化。D 概括准确。A 只讲 Malinowski 的生平，是举例，too_narrow。B 说问卷是研究异文化的最佳方式，讲座恰恰指出问卷的局限，reversed_logic 式偏离主旨。C 讲太平洋岛民一战期间如何生活，是背景细节，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does participant observation require of the researcher?

options.A: Living within a community for an extended period
options.B: Mailing detailed questionnaires to community members
options.C: Observing the community only from a safe distance
options.D: Interviewing people for a single afternoon

questions.explanation: 教授说参与式观察要求研究者长期生活在社区中并参与日常生活。A 准确复述。B 说邮寄详细问卷，正是讲座对比批评的旧方法，wrong_detail。C 说只从远处观察，与 joining daily routines rather than observing from a distance 相反，reversed_logic。D 说只访谈一个下午，与 a year or more 矛盾，reversed_logic。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Bronisław Malinowski?

options.A: To argue that ethnography only works on islands
options.B: To give an example of who pioneered participant observation
options.C: To describe how surveys were first invented
options.D: To explain the causes of the First World War

questions.explanation: 教授提到 Malinowski 是为举例说明谁开创了参与式观察这一方法。B 正确点明目的。A 说是为论证民族志只适用于岛屿，over_inference，教授没这层意思。C 说是为讲问卷如何发明，wrong_purpose。D 说是为解释一战起因，讲座只把战争当作他滞留岛上的背景，wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the ethnographic method?

options.A: It works best when the researcher avoids all involvement
options.B: It requires balancing closeness with analytical distance
options.C: It produces the same results as a written survey
options.D: It is no longer used by anthropologists today

questions.explanation: 教授说研究者要既投入到能从内部理解，又超脱到能清晰分析，暗示该方法需在亲近与分析性距离之间求平衡。B 是合理推断。A 说研究者应避免一切参与最好，与 involved enough to understand from the inside 相反，reversed_logic。C 说它产生与书面问卷相同的结果，讲座强调两者差异，reversed_logic。D 说今天人类学家已不再使用，与 remains central 矛盾，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: how confirmation bias influences decision-making
items.transcript: Think about the last time you formed a strong opinion about something. Maybe a political issue, maybe just which phone is better. Now, here's an uncomfortable question: did you actually weigh all the evidence fairly? Probably not, and that's because of a powerful mental tendency called confirmation bias. Confirmation bias is our inclination to seek out, interpret, and remember information that supports what we already believe, while discounting information that contradicts it. It operates quietly, beneath our awareness, which is exactly what makes it so influential in decision-making. Consider a classic study by Peter Wason in the 1960s. He gave participants a sequence of three numbers and asked them to figure out the rule behind it. Most people guessed a rule, then tested only number sequences that fit their guess. They almost never tried sequences that would prove them wrong. As a result, they clung to incorrect rules far longer than they should have. This is the heart of the problem. We treat confirming evidence as proof, but we treat disconfirming evidence as an exception, or we explain it away. Think about how this plays out in real decisions. An investor convinced a stock will rise reads only the optimistic forecasts. A doctor expecting a particular diagnosis notices the symptoms that fit and overlooks the ones that don't. The bias isn't a sign of stupidity. Smart, educated people fall into it just as easily, sometimes more so, because they're better at constructing arguments for what they already think. So the real lesson here is that good decision-making isn't just about gathering information. It's about actively seeking the evidence that might prove us wrong.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How confirmation bias shapes the way people make decisions
options.B: The number-sequence experiment conducted by Peter Wason
options.C: Why investors and doctors often make professional errors
options.D: How to gather more information before making a choice

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括了整场讲座的核心——确认偏误如何影响人们的决策，这正是教授从定义、实验到现实例子始终围绕的主题。B错在too_narrow，Wason实验只是用来说明问题的一个例子，并非全篇主旨。C错在too_narrow，投资者和医生只是两个简短的现实例证，不是整体话题。D是surface_word_trap，讲座结尾恰恰反驳了'只要多收集信息就够了'这一说法，用了'gathering information'等表面词汇却歪曲了真正含义。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did most participants do in Wason's study?

options.A: They tested sequences designed to disprove their own guess
options.B: They tested only sequences that fit their initial guess
options.C: They quickly abandoned rules that turned out to be wrong
options.D: They refused to propose any rule for the number sequence

questions.explanation: 正确答案B直接对应讲座中'tested only number sequences that fit their guess'。A是reversed_logic，恰好相反——参与者几乎从不测试能证明自己错的序列。C是reversed_logic，讲座说他们'clung to incorrect rules far longer than they should have'，即固守错误规则而非迅速放弃。D是wrong_detail，参与者确实猜了规则，并非拒绝提出规则。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention an investor and a doctor?

options.A: To argue that experts should not be trusted with decisions
options.B: To prove that confirmation bias is more common in finance
options.C: To show how confirmation bias appears in everyday real decisions
options.D: To describe two professions that rely heavily on forecasts

questions.explanation: 正确答案C点明了教授举投资者和医生例子的目的——展示确认偏误如何在真实的日常决策中表现出来（紧接'Think about how this plays out in real decisions'）。A是over_inference，教授并未主张不该信任专家。B是wrong_purpose，教授并非要证明金融领域偏误更普遍，两个例子是并列的。D是literal_restatement，只是复述了这两个职业的表面特征，没有解释教授提及它们的真正用意。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about intelligent, educated people?

options.A: They are generally immune to confirmation bias
options.B: They make decisions only after weighing all evidence
options.C: They rarely encounter confirmation bias in their work
options.D: Their reasoning skills can make the bias even stronger

questions.explanation: 正确答案D是合理推断——教授说聪明、受过教育的人有时'more so'地陷入偏误，'because they're better at constructing arguments for what they already think'，暗示其推理能力反而强化了偏误。A是reversed_logic，教授明确说他们同样容易、甚至更容易陷入。B是over_generalization，把讲座对良好决策的'理想建议'误当成这些人的实际行为。C是topic_adjacent，讲座并未说他们在工作中很少遇到偏误，反而暗示恰恰相反。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: the symbiosis between cleaner fish and larger marine animals
items.transcript: Picture a large, fierce-looking grouper hovering motionless near a coral reef, its mouth wide open and its gills flared. And swimming right inside that mouth is a tiny fish, calmly nibbling away. You might expect the small fish to become lunch, but instead, it's providing a service. This is one of the ocean's most remarkable partnerships, and it involves what biologists call cleaner fish. Cleaner fish, like the bluestreak cleaner wrasse, feed on the dead skin, mucus, and parasites that accumulate on the bodies of larger marine animals. The larger animal, called the client, gets cleaned, and the cleaner gets a meal. This is a textbook example of mutualism, a relationship in which both species benefit. What's especially fascinating is how organized this system is. Cleaner wrasse set up what researchers call cleaning stations, specific locations on the reef that clients visit regularly. Some large fish will line up and wait their turn. They'll even hold still and allow the cleaner to enter their mouths and gill chambers, places where a small fish would normally be in great danger. Now, you might wonder what stops the client from simply eating the cleaner. Trust, in a sense. Cleaners signal their identity with distinctive coloring and a special dancing motion. Studies have shown that when cleaner wrasse are removed from a reef, the diversity of other fish in that area drops, because many species depend on these cleaning services to stay healthy. So this tiny fish plays a role far bigger than its size suggests.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How groupers hunt smaller fish near coral reefs
options.B: A mutually beneficial relationship between cleaner fish and larger animals
options.C: The variety of fish species found at a single coral reef
options.D: How parasites spread among different marine animals

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括了全篇主题——清洁鱼与大型海洋动物之间互利共生的关系，这是教授从定义、清洁站到移除实验始终围绕的内容。A是wrong_topic，讲座恰恰强调小鱼没有被吃掉，而是提供服务，并非讲捕猎。C是surface_word_trap，'鱼的多样性'只在结尾作为一个论据出现，不是主旨。D是wrong_topic，寄生虫只是清洁鱼的食物来源之一，讲座并非讨论寄生虫的传播。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do cleaner fish feed on?

options.A: The eggs and young of larger reef fish
options.B: Coral polyps growing near cleaning stations
options.C: Dead skin, mucus, and parasites on larger animals
options.D: Small plankton drifting through the open water

questions.explanation: 正确答案C直接对应讲座原文'feed on the dead skin, mucus, and parasites that accumulate on the bodies of larger marine animals'。A是wrong_detail，讲座从未提到清洁鱼吃鱼卵或幼鱼。B是topic_adjacent，珊瑚虫在珊瑚礁环境中合理存在，但讲座并未说清洁鱼以其为食。D是topic_adjacent，浮游生物是许多海洋生物的食物，但不是讲座所述清洁鱼的食物。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the cleaner fish's coloring and dancing motion?

options.A: To show that cleaner fish are more colorful than their clients
options.B: To explain how cleaner fish attract mates on the reef
options.C: To describe how clients locate the nearest cleaning station
options.D: To explain how the cleaner signals its identity to the client

questions.explanation: 正确答案D点明用意——教授描述独特的颜色和舞动是为了说明清洁鱼如何向客户表明身份，从而避免被吃掉（紧接'what stops the client from simply eating the cleaner. Trust... signal their identity'）。A是literal_restatement，仅停留在'更鲜艳'这一表面比较，没有解释功能。B是wrong_purpose，讲座中颜色和舞动与求偶无关。C是over_inference，讲座说颜色舞动是表明身份，并未说是用来帮客户定位清洁站。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens when cleaner wrasse are removed from a reef?

options.A: The diversity of other fish in the area declines
options.B: The remaining cleaner fish grow noticeably larger
options.C: Client fish begin cleaning one another instead
options.D: Parasite populations on the reef quickly disappear

questions.explanation: 正确答案A直接对应讲座'when cleaner wrasse are removed from a reef, the diversity of other fish in that area drops'。B是topic_adjacent，讲座未提及剩余清洁鱼体型变化。C是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座从未说客户鱼会互相清洁。D是reversed_logic，移除清洁鱼后寄生虫应当增多而非消失，与讲座逻辑相反。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: Imagine you're a painter in Paris around 1870. For centuries, serious artists worked indoors, in studios, carefully finishing their paintings over weeks or months. But a group of young painters, the ones we now call the Impressionists, did something radical. They packed up their easels and went outside. This practice is called painting en plein air, a French phrase meaning 'in the open air.' Now, this wasn't just a change of scenery. It transformed how these artists understood color. Working outdoors, painters like Claude Monet noticed something the studio had hidden from them. The color of an object isn't fixed. A haystack isn't simply brown. At dawn it glows pink and orange, at midday it's a pale gold, and in late afternoon it turns deep violet. The light, and the time of day, completely changed the color they saw. So the Impressionists began painting light itself, rather than just objects. They used short, broken brushstrokes and placed small dabs of pure color side by side. Instead of mixing blue and yellow on the palette to make green, they'd put strokes of blue and yellow right next to each other, and let the viewer's eye blend them from a distance. This made their canvases shimmer with a sense of light and movement. Interestingly, a new invention helped make all this possible: paint in portable metal tubes, which meant artists could finally carry their colors outdoors easily. What began as a simple decision to step outside ended up changing the way an entire generation thought about light and color.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a new invention made metal paint tubes widely available
options.B: The daily routine of painters working in Paris studios
options.C: Why Impressionists painted outdoors and how it changed their use of color
options.D: The biography and major works of the painter Claude Monet

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括了全篇——印象派为何走出户外，以及这如何改变了他们对色彩的理解和运用，正是讲座的核心线索。A是too_narrow，便携颜料管只是结尾提到的一个辅助因素。B是wrong_topic，讲座恰恰对比了传统画室作画，重点不在画室日常。D是too_narrow，莫奈只是举例用的代表画家，讲座并非他的传记。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did painting outdoors reveal to artists like Monet?

options.A: That portable paint tubes were too inconvenient to use
options.B: That studios offered better lighting for finishing work
options.C: That brown was the most accurate color for a haystack
options.D: That an object's color changes with light and time of day

questions.explanation: 正确答案D对应讲座核心发现——'The color of an object isn't fixed'，干草堆在不同时间呈现粉橙、淡金、深紫等不同颜色。A是topic_adjacent，颜料管在讲座中是有帮助的发明，并非不便。B是reversed_logic，讲座说画室'hidden'了真相，户外才让画家看清，与'画室光线更好'相反。C是wrong_detail，讲座明确说干草堆'isn't simply brown'，与此选项相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe a haystack at different times of day?

options.A: To illustrate that an object's color shifts with changing light
options.B: To explain why haystacks were Monet's favorite subject
options.C: To show that haystacks are difficult to paint accurately
options.D: To prove that midday is the best time to paint outdoors

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授用干草堆在黎明、正午、午后的不同颜色来具体说明'物体颜色并非固定、随光线变化'这一核心观点。B是over_inference，讲座没有说干草堆是莫奈最爱的主题。C是wrong_purpose，重点不是干草堆难画，而是光线改变颜色。D是over_inference，讲座并未主张正午是户外作画的最佳时间。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the Impressionists' brushstroke technique?

options.A: It was mainly intended to help them paint more quickly
options.B: It relied on the viewer's eye to blend the colors
options.C: It produced duller canvases than traditional mixing did
options.D: It required colors to be fully mixed on the palette first

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说他们把蓝色和黄色并排放置，'let the viewer's eye blend them from a distance'，可推断该技法依赖观者的眼睛来调和颜色。A是topic_adjacent，作画速度并非讲座对笔触技法的论述重点。C是reversed_logic，讲座说画布'shimmer with a sense of light'，效果更生动而非更暗淡。D是reversed_logic，讲座恰恰说他们不在调色板上混合，而是直接并置色块。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: how group polarization affects committee decisions
items.transcript: Let's say a committee meets to make a decision. Everyone walks in leaning slightly in favor of an idea. You might expect that after discussing it, they'd settle on a moderate, balanced position. But often the opposite happens. The group ends up far more extreme than any single member started out. This effect has a name in social psychology: group polarization. Group polarization is the tendency for a group's decisions to become more extreme than the initial inclinations of its individual members. If people lean cautious, the group becomes even more cautious. If they lean toward risk, the group grows even bolder. Why does this happen? Researchers point to two main reasons. First, during discussion, members hear new arguments that support the direction they already favor, which reinforces their views. Second, people compare themselves to others and want to be seen favorably, so they shift toward what seems to be the group's preferred position, sometimes even more strongly. A well-known study by Stoner in the early 1960s found that groups discussing risky decisions often endorsed riskier choices than the individuals had on their own. This is sometimes called the risky shift. Now, this has real consequences. Think about a jury, a board of directors, or a government committee. A group designed to provide balance and careful judgment can actually amplify a one-sided view. Next time, we'll look at what techniques groups can use to guard against this and reach more balanced decisions.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How committees can be designed to save time in meetings
options.B: Why juries usually reach fair and balanced verdicts
options.C: A famous study conducted by Stoner in the 1960s
options.D: A tendency for group decisions to become more extreme

questions.explanation: 正确答案D概括全篇主旨——群体决策趋于比成员个人立场更极端的倾向，即群体极化，这是讲座定义、解释成因和举例始终围绕的话题。A是wrong_topic，讲座并未讨论如何节省会议时间。B是reversed_logic，讲座恰恰指出本应平衡的群体可能放大片面观点，而非陪审团通常公正。C是too_narrow，Stoner的研究只是支撑主题的一个例子。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is group polarization?

options.A: The tendency for group decisions to become more extreme than members' initial views
options.B: The tendency for groups to always reach a moderate compromise
options.C: The tendency for individuals to refuse to change their opinions
options.D: The tendency for committees to split into two opposing sides

questions.explanation: 正确答案A直接对应讲座定义'the tendency for a group's decisions to become more extreme than the initial inclinations of its individual members'。B是reversed_logic，讲座说结果常常相反，并非达成温和折中。C是wrong_detail，群体极化讲的是观点变得更极端，而非个人拒绝改变。D是wrong_detail，'两派对立'不是群体极化的定义，讲座未提及分裂。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is one reason group polarization occurs?

options.A: Members hear new arguments supporting the direction they already favor
options.B: Members are given no chance to discuss the decision at all
options.C: Members are required to vote before any discussion begins
options.D: Members usually distrust the opinions of everyone else present

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座给出的第一个原因——讨论中成员听到支持自己已有倾向的新论点，从而强化观点。B是reversed_logic，讲座强调正是'讨论'放大了极化，而非缺乏讨论。C是wrong_detail，讲座没有提到讨论前必须投票。D是wrong_detail，讲座说成员想被他人正面看待并向群体偏好靠拢，并非互不信任。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: More historical studies on the origins of the risky shift
options.B: How individuals make decisions when completely alone
options.C: Techniques groups can use to avoid polarization
options.D: Why committees should be abolished in favor of single leaders

questions.explanation: 正确答案C对应讲座结尾的明确预告——'we'll look at what techniques groups can use to guard against this and reach more balanced decisions'。A是too_narrow，回到本讲已提的历史研究，而非下一主题。B是wrong_topic，教授接下来谈的是群体如何防范极化，不是个人独自决策。D是reversed_logic，讲座目标是让群体决策更平衡，而非废除委员会，曲解了预告。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: why the Moon always shows the same face to Earth (tidal locking)
items.transcript: Here's something you may have never questioned. When you look up at the Moon, you always see the same face. The same craters, the same dark patches, night after night, year after year. The Moon never turns its back on us, so to speak. So why is that? The answer is a phenomenon called tidal locking. Tidal locking happens when an object's rotation period, the time it takes to spin once, matches its orbital period, the time it takes to circle around another body. The Moon takes about twenty-seven days to spin once on its axis, and about twenty-seven days to orbit the Earth. Because those two periods are equal, the same hemisphere always faces us. Now, an important point: this does not mean the Moon isn't rotating. It is rotating, just at a perfectly matched rate. How did this happen? Billions of years ago, the Moon spun much faster. But Earth's gravity pulls slightly harder on the near side of the Moon than the far side, creating a small bulge. This gravitational tug acted like a brake, gradually slowing the Moon's spin over millions of years until it locked into place. This isn't unique to our Moon, by the way. Many moons in our solar system are tidally locked to their planets, and some planets are even locked to their stars. So the next time someone mentions the dark side of the Moon, you can gently correct them. There's no permanently dark side. Every part of the Moon gets sunlight. It's just that one side is permanently hidden from Earth, not from the Sun.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The reason one side of the Moon always faces the Earth
options.B: How craters and dark patches formed on the Moon's surface
options.C: Why one side of the Moon never receives any sunlight
options.D: How long the Moon takes to travel around the Earth

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括全篇——为什么月球总是同一面朝向地球，即潮汐锁定，这是讲座定义、成因和延伸始终围绕的主题。B是wrong_topic，环形山和暗斑只是开头的视觉描述，讲座不解释它们如何形成。C是surface_word_trap，借用了'dark side'的表面词汇，但讲座结尾恰恰澄清月球没有永久黑暗的一面。D是too_narrow，公转27天只是支撑解释的一个细节，不是主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what causes tidal locking to occur?

options.A: The rotation period and orbital period becoming equal
options.B: The Moon gradually losing all of its rotation entirely
options.C: The Sun's gravity pulling the Moon away from Earth
options.D: A sudden collision that stopped the Moon from spinning

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座定义——当自转周期与公转周期相等时就发生潮汐锁定。B是reversed_logic，教授特别强调月球'isn't NOT rotating'，它仍在自转，只是速率匹配，并非完全失去自转。C是wrong_detail，使月球减速的是地球（而非太阳）的引力，且方向是制动而非把月球拉离。D是topic_adjacent，碰撞在天文学中合理，但讲座说减速是引力在数百万年间逐渐作用，而非突然碰撞。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the Moon spun much faster billions of years ago?

options.A: To explain how the Moon's spin slowed into a locked state
options.B: To prove that the Moon is no longer rotating today
options.C: To show that the Moon is moving closer to the Earth
options.D: To describe how the Moon's craters were first created

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授提到月球过去自转更快，是为了引出地球引力如何像刹车一样逐渐使其减速并锁定，解释潮汐锁定的形成过程。B是over_inference，讲座明确说月球仍在自转，并非要证明它停止旋转。C是wrong_purpose，讲座此处与月球靠近地球无关。D是wrong_purpose，提及更快自转与环形山的形成无关。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the phrase 'the dark side of the Moon'?

options.A: It refers to the coldest region on the Moon's surface
options.B: It is a misleading description of the Moon's hidden side
options.C: It describes the half of the Moon that faces the Sun
options.D: It proves that one hemisphere never receives sunlight

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说可以'gently correct'别人，并指出没有永久黑暗的一面，暗示'dark side'是对月球隐藏一面的误导性说法。A是topic_adjacent，温度高低并非讲座讨论的重点。C是wrong_detail，'dark side'指的是背向地球的一面，而非朝向太阳的一面。D是reversed_logic，讲座明确说月球每一部分都会受到阳光照射，与'某半球从不受光'相反。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how urban heat islands form and why cities are warmer than surrounding areas
items.transcript: Have you ever noticed that a city feels noticeably hotter than the countryside just a few miles away, especially on a summer night? It's not your imagination. Cities really are warmer, and there's a name for this: the urban heat island effect. An urban heat island is a metropolitan area that's significantly warmer than the surrounding rural land, sometimes by several degrees. So what causes it? The biggest factor is the materials we build cities with. Asphalt, concrete, and brick absorb and store a tremendous amount of heat during the day.
