Think of a dark parking lot baking in the afternoon sun. These surfaces then release that stored heat slowly, well into the night, which is why cities stay warm after dark. Vegetation makes a difference too. In the countryside, plants and soil release water vapor, a process that cools the air, much like sweating cools your body. Cities, with far less greenery, lose that natural cooling. And then there's waste heat. Cars, air conditioners, and factories all pump heat directly into the air. Now, this isn't just a matter of comfort. Higher city temperatures increase the demand for air conditioning, which raises energy use and, ironically, generates even more waste heat. It can also worsen air pollution and pose health risks during heat waves. Researchers are exploring solutions, like planting more trees, creating green roofs, and using lighter-colored, more reflective building materials. So a city's temperature isn't just about the weather. It's shaped by the very design of the city itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why cities are warmer than surrounding rural areas and what causes it
options.B: How air conditioning systems function in large modern buildings
options.C: The health risks that heat waves pose to elderly city residents
options.D: Why the countryside receives more rainfall than nearby cities

questions.explanation: 正确答案A概括全篇——城市为何比周边乡村更热以及其成因，即城市热岛效应，这是讲座定义、成因和解决方案始终围绕的主题。B是too_narrow，空调只是产生废热的一个因素，并非主题。C是too_narrow，热浪健康风险只是结尾一笔带过的后果之一。D是wrong_topic，讲座提到乡村植物蒸发降温，但并未讨论降雨量多少。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do cities stay warm after dark?

options.A: Building materials release stored heat slowly into the night
options.B: Plants in the city release large amounts of water vapor
options.C: Rural areas send their warm air toward nearby cities
options.D: Streetlights and signs generate most of the city's heat

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座原文——沥青、混凝土等材料白天储热，'release that stored heat slowly, well into the night'。B是reversed_logic，植物释放水汽是降温过程，且城市绿化少，与让城市夜间变暖相反。C是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座从未说乡村把暖空气送往城市。D是wrong_detail，讲座列举的废热来源是汽车、空调和工厂，并未说路灯和广告牌是主要热源。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare plant water vapor to sweating?

options.A: To explain how releasing water vapor cools the surrounding air
options.B: To argue that the countryside is more humid than the city
options.C: To describe how human bodies regulate their temperature
options.D: To suggest that plants need a great deal of water to survive

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授用'出汗降温'这个类比来帮助听者理解植物释放水汽如何冷却周围空气。B是over_inference，讲座没有比较城乡湿度。C是literal_restatement，仅复述了出汗这一表面内容，没有解释类比的真正目的。D是wrong_purpose，类比的重点是降温机制，而非植物的需水量。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about air conditioning in cities?

options.A: It is the single largest cause of the urban heat island
options.B: It can make the urban heat island problem worse
options.C: It has little effect on a city's overall temperature
options.D: It cools cities enough to offset heat from other sources

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说高温增加空调需求，'raises energy use and, ironically, generates even more waste heat'，可推断空调会使热岛问题更严重。A是over_generalization，讲座说最大因素是建筑材料，而非把空调说成单一最大成因。C是reversed_logic，讲座暗示空调显著加剧升温，并非影响甚微。D是reversed_logic，空调向空气排放废热使城市更热，而非降温抵消其他热源。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how children acquire grammar without being explicitly taught the rules
items.transcript: Think about a four-year-old child. She's never had a grammar lesson, she can't tell you what a verb or a subject is, and yet she produces sentences that follow remarkably complex rules. How is that possible? This puzzle sits at the heart of the study of language acquisition. Children acquire grammar not by memorizing rules, but by unconsciously extracting patterns from the speech they hear around them. And here's the striking evidence: they make errors that no adult ever models for them. A child will say 'I goed to the park' or 'I have two foots.' Now, nobody talks like that. The child has never heard an adult say 'goed.' So where does it come from? The answer is that the child has internalized a rule, namely, that you add an '-ed' to make the past tense, and is applying it consistently, even to irregular verbs. Linguists call this overregularization. It's actually a sign of intelligence, not error. The child isn't parroting. She's generating language from a rule she worked out herself. This observation led the linguist Noam Chomsky to argue that humans are born with an innate capacity for language, a kind of mental blueprint for grammar. The input children receive, he pointed out, is often too messy and incomplete to fully explain how quickly and uniformly they learn. So rather than being blank slates, children may come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them. The errors, paradoxically, reveal the hidden machinery of the mind at work.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How adults can best teach grammar rules to young children
options.B: How children come to follow grammar rules without being taught them
options.C: Why irregular verbs are the hardest part of learning English
options.D: The biography and major theories of the linguist Noam Chomsky

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括全篇——孩子如何在无人明确教授的情况下掌握语法规则，这是讲座从谜题、过度规则化证据到乔姆斯基理论始终围绕的核心。A是reversed_logic，讲座恰恰强调孩子并非被大人教会，而是自己提取规律。C是too_narrow，不规则动词只是说明过度规则化的例子，不是主旨。D是too_narrow，乔姆斯基只是被引用来支持论点的一位学者，讲座并非他的传记。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do children say things like 'goed' or 'foots'?

options.A: They are copying errors they hear adults make
options.B: They have applied a regular rule to an irregular word
options.C: They are unable to remember words they hear
options.D: They are deliberately playing with sounds for fun

questions.explanation: 正确答案B对应讲座解释——孩子内化了'加-ed构成过去式'的规则，并一致地套用到不规则动词上，即过度规则化。A是reversed_logic，讲座明确说'nobody talks like that'，孩子从未听大人这样说，并非模仿。C是wrong_detail，讲座说孩子是在自己生成规则，而非记不住单词。D是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座说这是套用规则的结果，并非故意玩声音。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that overregularization is 'a sign of intelligence, not error'?

options.A: To emphasize that the child is generating language from a rule she worked out
options.B: To argue that children are smarter than adults at learning language
options.C: To point out that the child has simply memorized many new words
options.D: To show that such mistakes will disappear as the child grows older

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授强调过度规则化是智力的体现，是为了说明孩子并非鹦鹉学舌，而是依据自己推导出的规则生成语言。B是over_inference，讲座没有比较孩子和成人谁更聪明。C是literal_restatement并曲解，讲座恰恰说孩子不是死记硬背，而是运用规则。D是over_inference，讲座此处未谈这些错误是否会随年龄消失。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Chomsky's view of children's minds?

options.A: Children learn language mainly by carefully imitating adult speech
options.B: Children are born with a built-in framework that guides language learning
options.C: Children must be explicitly taught grammar to learn it correctly
options.D: Children's language ability depends mostly on how much input they get

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授引述乔姆斯基的观点，认为孩子并非白板，而是'come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them'，即天生具备引导语言学习的内在框架。A是reversed_logic，讲座强调孩子不是单纯模仿大人。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是孩子无需被明确教授就能掌握语法。D是topic_adjacent并被反驳，乔姆斯基恰恰指出输入'too messy and incomplete'，不足以解释学习，因此能力不主要取决于输入量。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: why people value something more once they own it (the endowment effect)
items.transcript: Imagine I give you a coffee mug, just a plain one, for free. A few minutes later, I offer to buy it back from you. How much would you ask for? Now imagine instead that you never received the mug, and I simply ask how much you'd pay to buy one. Studies show something surprising. The people who own the mug typically demand about twice as much to sell it as the people without one are willing to pay. The mug hasn't changed. Only ownership has. This is what economists call the endowment effect. The endowment effect is the tendency for people to value something more highly simply because they own it. It was demonstrated famously by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues in a series of experiments in the 1990s, often using exactly this kind of mug. Why does this happen? A big part of the explanation is something called loss aversion. Psychologically, losing something feels worse than gaining the equivalent thing feels good. So once the mug is yours, giving it up registers as a painful loss, and you demand a higher price to compensate. Notice that this challenges a basic assumption in traditional economics, the idea that an object has a stable value independent of who holds it. The endowment effect shows that value is partly psychological, shaped by ownership and the fear of loss. This has practical implications, from how companies offer free trials to how we cling to possessions we'd never actually choose to buy again.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How companies design free trial offers for new products
options.B: The experiments Daniel Kahneman conducted in the 1990s
options.C: A tendency to value things more highly once we own them
options.D: Why coffee mugs are commonly used in economic research

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括全篇——人们一旦拥有某物就会更看重它的倾向，即禀赋效应，这是讲座定义、实验和理论含义始终围绕的主题。A是too_narrow，免费试用只是结尾提到的一个实际应用。B是too_narrow，Kahneman的实验只是支撑主题的证据之一。D是surface_word_trap，借用了反复出现的'mug'，但杯子只是实验道具，并非讲座主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did studies find about mug owners?

options.A: They demanded about twice as much to sell as buyers would pay
options.B: They refused to sell the mug at any price at all
options.C: They valued the mug exactly as much as non-owners did
options.D: They were willing to pay more than non-owners to buy one

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座原文——拥有杯子的人开出的卖价'about twice as much'于无杯者愿意支付的买价。B是wrong_detail，讲座说他们要价更高，并非拒绝以任何价格出售。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是拥有者比非拥有者更看重，而非估值相同。D是wrong_detail，混淆了买卖双方——是拥有者的卖价高，而非他们更愿出钱去买。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain the concept of loss aversion?

options.A: To explain why owning something makes giving it up feel painful
options.B: To prove that people enjoy gains more than they fear losses
options.C: To describe how Kahneman first discovered the coffee mug study
options.D: To argue that all economic decisions are based on emotion

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授引入损失厌恶是为了解释为何一旦拥有某物，放弃它会被感知为痛苦的损失，从而支撑禀赋效应。B是reversed_logic，损失厌恶恰恰指失去比获得更痛苦，与此选项相反。C是wrong_purpose，损失厌恶用于解释机制，与如何发现杯子实验无关。D是over_inference，教授说价值'partly psychological'，并未主张所有经济决策都基于情绪。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the traditional economic view of value?

options.A: It correctly predicts how owners price their possessions
options.B: It is incomplete because value is partly shaped by ownership
options.C: It has been fully replaced by the concept of loss aversion
options.D: It applies only to inexpensive everyday objects like mugs

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说禀赋效应'challenges'传统经济学关于价值稳定、与持有者无关的假设，可推断该传统观点并不完整，因为价值部分受拥有权影响。A是reversed_logic，传统观点恰恰无法解释拥有者要价更高的现象。C是over_generalization，讲座说'challenges'而非'完全取代'，损失厌恶是解释禀赋效应的原因之一。D是topic_adjacent，讲座没有说传统价值观只适用于廉价日常物品。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript:
Today I want to talk about how recording technology gradually transformed the way composers actually wrote music. Before the late nineteenth century, almost all composition was prepared for live performance only. A piece was finished when the ink dried on the manuscript, and the only feedback the composer received came from rehearsals and the eventual audience.

Things began to change with Edison's phonograph in 1877. For the first time a sound could be captured, played back, and listened to again. Initially, composers treated recordings simply as documentation, a way to preserve a performance. But within two decades, several began to think about the recording itself as a medium with its own constraints. Early discs could hold only about three minutes of music, so popular songwriters compressed verses and shortened instrumental introductions to fit. The three-minute pop song that dominated the twentieth century was, in part, a side effect of disc length.

The larger shift came with magnetic tape after the Second World War. Tape allowed editing: splicing two takes together, removing wrong notes, repeating a single phrase many times to layer it. Composers like Pierre Schaeffer treated tape as a compositional instrument in itself, building entire pieces from manipulated recorded sounds rather than written notation.

By the 1960s, popular musicians had embraced the same approach. Multitrack recording let producers and composers build a song one layer at a time, and the recording studio effectively became the score. What I want you to notice is this: technology did not just preserve composition; it gradually changed what composition was. A piece existed not on paper but on tape.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How recording technology gradually transformed the process of musical composition
options.B: The three-minute pop song as the most important format of the twentieth century
options.C: Edison's invention of the phonograph and its commercial success
options.D: The training of composers in early twentieth-century European conservatories

questions.explanation: 讲座中心是录音技术如何逐步改变作曲过程：从单纯保存声音到唱片限时影响歌曲结构，再到磁带剪辑和多轨录音让录音棚本身成为乐谱。A 概括了这一主线。B 只覆盖一个段落细节，too_narrow。C 把背景信息当作主旨，wrong_topic。D 完全没有出现在讲座中，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what effect did the length limit of early discs have on popular songwriting?

options.A: Composers added longer orchestral introductions
options.B: Songwriters compressed verses and shortened introductions to fit
options.C: Performers refused to make commercial recordings
options.D: Discs were lengthened to match standard song formats

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出早期唱片只能容纳约三分钟音乐，所以词曲作家压缩段落、缩短前奏。B 与原文一致。A 与所述相反，reversed_logic。C 没有依据。D 因果颠倒：是歌曲格式被唱片长度塑造，而不是相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Pierre Schaeffer treat magnetic tape as?

options.A: A purely commercial recording medium
options.B: A replacement for the early disc format
options.C: A compositional instrument in itself
options.D: A scientific tool for studying acoustics

questions.explanation: 原文明说 Schaeffer 把磁带当作一种作曲乐器来使用，用录制和操控的声音构建作品。C 正确。A 把媒介商业用途和创作工具混淆，topic_adjacent。B 没有提到 Schaeffer 与唱片竞争，wrong_detail。D 没有任何科学声学的提法，wrong_topic。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a popular song produced in the 1960s using multitrack recording?

options.A: It was usually still written down as a complete score before any recording took place
options.B: It was technically inferior to recordings made on early Edison discs
options.C: It existed primarily as a written manuscript stored in studio archives
options.D: Its final form effectively existed on the tape rather than in any written notation

questions.explanation: 教授结尾强调录音棚本身成为乐谱，作品不再依赖纸上的乐谱而存在于磁带上。D 正是这一暗示。A 与教授的论点相反，reversed_logic。B 是无依据的价值判断，over_generalization。C 与多轨录音逻辑相悖，topic_adjacent。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: the role of plate tectonics in forming mountain ranges
items.transcript:
Today I want to talk about how mountain ranges are formed, and specifically the role of plate tectonics in that process. The Earth's outer shell is broken into about a dozen large plates that drift slowly across the planet's surface. Most major mountain ranges sit precisely where two of these plates have collided or pushed past each other.

The clearest example is the Himalayas. About fifty million years ago, the plate carrying India crashed into the Asian plate. Neither plate could sink under the other easily, because both pieces of continental crust were too thick and too low in density. So instead, the rocks at the boundary were shoved upward, folded, and stacked. The mountains rose, and they're still rising today by about five millimeters per year.

Not all mountain ranges form in the same way. Along the western edge of South America, an oceanic plate is being forced beneath the continent. The ocean plate is denser, so it slides downward into the Earth's interior, but in the process it scrapes off sediments and triggers volcanic activity above. That's how the Andes formed: partly through compression, but mostly through this long, slow descent of an oceanic plate.

There's a third pattern, too. The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is also a mountain range, but it sits underwater. There, two oceanic plates are moving apart, and molten rock pushes up through the gap to harden into new crust.

So the takeaway is this: collisions, descents, and separations all produce mountains, but the shapes, heights, and rock types depend strongly on which kind of tectonic boundary is involved.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Himalayas continue to rise at five millimeters per year
options.B: How different types of plate boundaries produce different mountain ranges
options.C: The danger of volcanic eruptions along the western edge of South America
options.D: The chemistry of molten rock at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是不同板块边界（碰撞、俯冲、张裂）如何形成不同的山脉。B 是对全文的合理概括。A 只覆盖一个例子，too_narrow。C 仅是顺带提及的火山，too_narrow。D 是次要细节，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do neither plate sinks under the other where the Himalayas form?

options.A: Because the rocks at the boundary are heavier than oceanic plates
options.B: Because volcanic activity prevents subduction at the boundary
options.C: Because both pieces of continental crust are too thick and low in density
options.D: Because the boundary has been moving in opposite directions for fifty million years

questions.explanation: 教授明确说两块大陆地壳都过厚、密度过低，因此谁也无法下潜。C 正确。A 与原因相反，reversed_logic。B 在喜马拉雅段落根本没提火山，wrong_detail。D 把方向问题误当成原因，topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the Mid-Atlantic Ridge?

options.A: To argue that ocean ridges produce taller mountains than continental ranges
options.B: To suggest that all mountain ranges originate under the ocean
options.C: To recall an example already discussed in a previous class
options.D: To illustrate a third mountain-forming process: two plates moving apart

questions.explanation: 教授引入大西洋洋中脊作为第三种模式（板块张裂）的例子，与前两种碰撞、俯冲并列。D 正确。A 是没有依据的价值比较，over_inference。B 是过度概括，over_inference。C 没有这种课堂回顾迹象，wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the Andes?

options.A: Their rock types and shape reflect a subduction zone rather than a continental collision
options.B: They are growing at a faster rate than the Himalayas
options.C: They are formed mainly by two oceanic plates moving apart
options.D: They will eventually become as tall as the Himalayas

questions.explanation: 教授把安第斯归为大洋板块下潜并伴有火山的类型，与喜马拉雅式的两块大陆地壳碰撞不同。A 正确推断。B 没有比较增长率，over_inference。C 把安第斯误归入张裂型，reversed_logic。D 是无依据的未来预测，over_inference。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the anthropology of food taboos across societies
items.transcript:
Today let's look at food taboos, and try to understand why so many human societies tell their members that certain foods are not to be eaten. From an anthropologist's perspective, taboos are interesting precisely because they often look irrational from outside the culture, and yet they're sustained over many generations.

The oldest scholarly explanation, going back to the nineteenth century, treated taboos as superstition. Outside observers concluded that the people involved simply did not understand what was safe to eat. That explanation has fallen out of favor for a fairly obvious reason: many tabooed foods are clearly nutritious and well-known, so ignorance cannot be the answer.

A second approach, popular with mid-twentieth-century anthropologists, is the functional or ecological reading. On this view, taboos quietly conserve scarce or fragile resources. A community that forbids hunting a particular bird during nesting season prevents the population from collapsing. The taboo doesn't have to be explained in ecological terms by the people who follow it; the rule itself does the work. This kind of explanation has its limits, though, because not every taboo corresponds neatly to a real ecological pressure.

A third, more recent perspective treats taboos as markers of group identity. Eating, or refusing to eat, particular foods becomes a daily, visible signal of belonging. The taboo is meaningful because it distinguishes us from outsiders, and the everyday discipline of following it strengthens commitment to a community.

Most contemporary anthropologists now combine these approaches. A given taboo may have multiple layers — partly ecological, partly social, partly historical — and the relative weight of each layer changes from case to case.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why nineteenth-century anthropologists dismissed food taboos as mere superstition
options.B: Whether food taboos prevent over-hunting of fragile bird populations
options.C: How anthropological thinking about food taboos has shifted across competing explanations
options.D: Why eating habits are the strongest available marker of cultural identity

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是回顾人类学对食物禁忌解释的演变：迷信论、生态/功能论、身份论以及当代综合视角。C 概括正确。A 只是第一种解释，too_narrow。B 仅是举例，too_narrow。D 是第三种解释的过度泛化，over_generalization。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the main limitation of the functional/ecological explanation?

options.A: It does not allow anthropologists to compare societies
options.B: It depends on ideas that have been entirely discredited since 1900
options.C: It works only for taboos involving plant foods, not animals
options.D: Not every taboo corresponds neatly to a real ecological pressure

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出，并非每个禁忌都能对应真实的生态压力，这是该解释的局限。D 正确。A 完全没有依据。B 把第一种解释的过时与第二种混淆，wrong_detail。C 没有植物 vs 动物的区分，wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that taboos are sustained over many generations?

options.A: To highlight why anthropologists find taboos a particularly puzzling phenomenon worth explaining
options.B: To prove that older societies were generally more disciplined than modern societies
options.C: To argue that food taboos are likely to disappear in industrialized economies
options.D: To suggest that ecological explanations are the only adequate account of taboos

questions.explanation: 教授提出多世代延续这一现象，是为了说明禁忌从外部看似乎不合理却长期存在，正因如此值得人类学家解释。A 正确。B 是没有依据的价值判断，over_inference。C 与讲座立场相反，wrong_purpose。D 与讲座的多元综合立场相反，wrong_purpose。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how anthropologists today typically explain a particular taboo?

options.A: They usually accept the functional/ecological explanation without further analysis
options.B: They generally consider several factors and weight them differently from case to case
options.C: They prefer to interpret taboos primarily as misunderstandings of nutrition
options.D: They mostly view taboos as transient phenomena that disappear over a single generation

questions.explanation: 教授在结尾说当代人类学家通常综合多种因素，并根据具体禁忌调整权重。B 正是这一含义。A 与综合立场相反，reversed_logic。C 重复了被弃用的迷信解释，wrong_detail。D 与禁忌长期延续的事实相反，reversed_logic。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: the principle behind how a lever multiplies force
items.transcript:
Today I want to look at one of the oldest devices in physics, the lever, and explain why it lets a small force lift a much heavier load. The principle goes back to Archimedes and is sometimes called the law of the lever.

A lever is just a rigid bar that turns around a fixed point called the fulcrum. On one side you apply an input force; on the other side a load resists you. The crucial idea is that what matters isn't simply how hard you push, but how far you are from the fulcrum. The further you sit from the fulcrum, the larger the rotating effect of your push. Engineers call this rotating effect a moment, or a torque.

For the lever to balance, the moment on the load side and the moment on your side must be equal. That means if the load is sitting twice as close to the fulcrum as you are, your input force only needs to be half the weight of the load. Move the fulcrum closer to the load, and the multiplication grows further.

There is, however, a trade-off. You pay for the multiplied force in distance. When the load rises a small height, your end of the lever travels a much greater distance. So total energy is conserved — force is multiplied, but the path of your hand grows in exactly the same proportion.

This principle is everywhere. A crowbar pulls out a nail because you sit far from the contact point. Scissors cut paper because the blade meets the paper close to the fulcrum. Even bones and muscles work the same way: many joints in the human body are levers, with the fulcrum at the joint itself.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Archimedes discovered the principle of buoyancy
options.B: Why crowbars are more useful for daily work than scissors are
options.C: How human bones evolved into highly efficient lever systems
options.D: How a lever multiplies force, and the distance trade-off that accompanies it

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是杠杆放大力的原理以及伴随的距离代价。D 正确。A 把杠杆原理混淆为浮力原理，wrong_topic。B 是举例之一的价值化判断，too_narrow。C 是结尾的一个例子，too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what determines the rotating effect of a force on a lever?

options.A: Both the size of the force and the distance from the fulcrum
options.B: The mass of the rigid bar itself
options.C: The angle at which the load is attached
options.D: The total height the load needs to be lifted

questions.explanation: 教授强调力的旋转效果（力矩）由力的大小和距离支点的距离共同决定。A 正确。B、C、D 在讲座中都没有作为力矩决定因素出现，都是 wrong_detail 或 topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that the input end of the lever travels a much greater distance?

options.A: To remind students that levers only function when they are perfectly balanced
options.B: To make the point that total energy is conserved — the force gained is paid for in distance
options.C: To argue that long levers are usually preferred to short ones in modern engineering
options.D: To suggest that moving the fulcrum has no effect on the input force required

questions.explanation: 教授提到行程更长是为了说明能量守恒——放大的力以更长的行程为代价。B 正确。A 偏离杠杆的功用，wrong_purpose。C 没有此类价值判断，over_inference。D 与原文相反，literal_restatement 但是颠倒。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention scissors and bones at the end of the lecture?

options.A: To suggest that the lever principle is gradually being replaced by newer mechanisms
options.B: To complain that schools rarely teach simple machines anymore
options.C: To illustrate that the same lever principle applies across many familiar examples
options.D: To warn students that not every human joint actually behaves like a lever

questions.explanation: 结尾的剪刀和人体骨骼都是杠杆原理普遍适用的例证。C 正确。A 与论旨相反，wrong_purpose。B 是离题的抱怨，wrong_purpose。D 与教授所说骨骼按杠杆原理工作相反，reversed_logic。

items.subject: history
items.topic: the role of coffee houses in the European Enlightenment
items.transcript: Coffee houses played a remarkably important role in shaping European intellectual life during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. The first widely known one opened in Oxford in 1650, and within fifty years, Paris, Vienna, and Amsterdam each had dozens. So what made these places matter? Well, they functioned as informal universities. For roughly the price of a single cup of coffee, anyone could enter, sit down, read the newspapers laid out on the tables, and join an ongoing conversation. This was genuinely radical. Earlier social spaces had been organized around rank or religion, but a coffee house welcomed merchants, lawyers, scientists, and even tradesmen on essentially equal terms. Let me give you a concrete example. In London, a coffee house called Lloyd's became the meeting point for ship captains and underwriters comparing notes on insurance. Out of those daily conversations grew Lloyd's of London, one of the great insurance markets of the modern era. Similar gatherings in Paris reshaped how news was discussed, and historians have shown that the most influential pamphlets of the period circulated through coffee-house networks before they ever reached printers. And because the conversation was open and the cost of entry was low, ideas spread faster than the authorities of the day could control them. By the late eighteenth century, governments in several countries were closing or monitoring coffee houses, precisely because of the political weight they carried. So when we discuss the Enlightenment, it's worth remembering that many of its debates happened not in lecture halls but in noisy rooms full of coffee, newspapers, and ordinary people.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How European coffee houses functioned as influential spaces for public conversation
options.B: The economic origins of the modern insurance industry
options.C: How coffee was first imported and traded across Europe
options.D: Why governments banned newspapers during the Enlightenment

questions.explanation: 讲座核心论点是咖啡馆作为开放交流空间塑造了启蒙时代的思想流通；保险业起源、咖啡贸易、新闻禁令都是细节或不相关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what made coffee houses unusual social spaces?

options.A: They were funded by universities to spread official ideas.
options.B: They welcomed people from very different social ranks on roughly equal terms.
options.C: They were restricted to merchants and ship captains by local laws.
options.D: They charged different prices based on a customer's profession.

questions.explanation: 教授明确说咖啡馆 "welcomed merchants, lawyers, scientists, and even tradesmen on essentially equal terms"。其他选项与原文矛盾或没有依据。

questions.stem: What grew out of conversations at Lloyd's coffee house in London?

options.A: A network of municipal libraries
options.B: A movement to regulate the newspaper trade
options.C: A major insurance market
options.D: A scientific society for naval engineers

questions.explanation: 教授说船长和保险商在 Lloyd's 的日常讨论催生了 Lloyd's of London 这一保险市场。其他选项与讲座中提到的主题无关。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about late-eighteenth-century governments?

options.A: They funded coffee houses to encourage scientific research.
options.B: They prohibited the printing of insurance contracts.
options.C: They closed all newspapers across major European cities.
options.D: They viewed coffee houses as politically threatening enough to restrict.

questions.explanation: 教授说后期政府 "closing or monitoring coffee houses, precisely because of the political weight they carried"，暗示政府将其视为政治威胁。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: convergent evolution in unrelated species
items.transcript: Today let's look at one of the most striking patterns in evolutionary biology, something we call convergent evolution. The basic idea is that totally unrelated species, separated by tens of millions of years, sometimes evolve almost identical solutions to the same environmental problem. Now, why does this happen? It comes down to the fact that physics and ecology impose tight constraints. If you need to move quickly through open water, the most efficient body shape is going to be streamlined, narrow at both ends, with paired fins or flippers. So organisms that live in that habitat tend to converge on that solution, no matter where they started. The classic example is the comparison of dolphins, sharks, and the extinct marine reptiles called ichthyosaurs. These three groups have almost nothing in common evolutionarily. Sharks are fish, dolphins are mammals, and ichthyosaurs were reptiles. They diverged hundreds of millions of years ago. And yet, when you place fossil ichthyosaurs alongside modern sharks and dolphins, the body plans are eerily similar. Same streamlined torso, dorsal fin, flippers, and even similar tail proportions. Another famous case involves wings. Birds, bats, and the extinct pterosaurs all evolved flapping flight independently. Their wing bones are organized completely differently, yet the resulting structure achieves similar lift. This tells biologists something important. Evolution does not invent endless novel solutions. It tends to converge on the limited set of designs that actually work under given physical conditions. And once you start looking, you find convergence everywhere — in camera eyes, in venom systems, even in carnivorous plants. Next class, I want to look at a related question: when convergence breaks down, and what that tells us about evolutionary history.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why most marine reptiles became extinct millions of years ago
options.B: How unrelated species evolve similar solutions to similar problems
options.C: How sharks and dolphins compete for prey in open water
options.D: Why bird wings are mechanically superior to bat wings

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心概念是 convergent evolution：无关物种独立演化出相似解。其他选项是细节或无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, which three groups share strikingly similar body plans despite distant evolutionary origins?

options.A: Bats, pterosaurs, and ostriches
options.B: Squids, dolphins, and tuna
options.C: Sharks, dolphins, and ichthyosaurs
options.D: Penguins, sharks, and seals

questions.explanation: 教授明确比较了鲨鱼、海豚和鱼龙三者的体型相似性。其他组合并未在讲座中作为该例子提及。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention birds, bats, and pterosaurs?

options.A: To explain why bats are nocturnal hunters
options.B: To argue that wing bones are identical across all flying species
options.C: To describe the order in which flight evolved in vertebrates
options.D: To give another example where unrelated groups converged on flight independently

questions.explanation: 鸟、蝙蝠、翼龙是为了再举一个 convergent evolution 的例子。其他选项要么夸大（骨骼完全相同），要么偏题。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss in the next class?

options.A: Cases in which convergence does not occur and what those cases reveal
options.B: How dolphins use echolocation to navigate dark waters
options.C: The fossil record of marine reptiles in the Jurassic period
options.D: How carnivorous plants attract specific insect species

questions.explanation: 教授结尾明确预告下次讨论 "when convergence breaks down"。其他选项虽然相关但并非他预告的内容。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: how confirmation bias influences decision-making
items.transcript: Confirmation bias is one of the most well-documented patterns in cognitive psychology, and it shapes a surprising amount of how we make everyday decisions. The basic definition is this: when people hold a belief, they tend to seek out, remember, and weigh information that supports that belief, while ignoring or downplaying information that challenges it. It sounds almost too simple to be a big deal, but the effects are powerful. Researchers have studied this for decades. One classic experiment from the early 1980s asked participants to read mixed evidence about whether the death penalty deters crime. Both supporters and opponents of the policy read the exact same studies, yet after reading, each side felt more confident in its original position. They had selectively absorbed the parts that fit. Now, why does this happen? Part of it is cognitive economy. Carefully evaluating every piece of conflicting information would be exhausting, so the brain takes shortcuts. Another part is emotional. Admitting we were wrong feels uncomfortable, and our minds work hard to avoid that feeling. Importantly, confirmation bias is not a sign of low intelligence. In fact, studies show that more analytical thinkers are sometimes better at constructing sophisticated arguments to defend their existing views, which can make the bias worse, not better. So what can you do about it? Researchers recommend deliberately seeking out the strongest case against your current position, and engaging with people who disagree with you in good faith. It's not a cure, but it weakens the bias. As you read your textbook chapter for next week, watch for how confirmation bias shows up in scientific debates, in juries, and in your own study habits.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the death penalty deters crime in the United States
options.B: Why analytical thinkers are usually more accurate than others
options.C: How confirmation bias affects how people process information
options.D: Why cognitive shortcuts evolved in early human ancestors

questions.explanation: 讲座主要介绍 confirmation bias 及其在思考与决策中的作用。其他选项是细节、对立观点或无关进化主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the death-penalty experiment show?

options.A: Most participants changed their views after reading the studies.
options.B: Supporters and opponents agreed on the studies' main findings.
options.C: Participants struggled to recall the studies after reading.
options.D: Each side became more confident in its original position.

questions.explanation: 教授说双方读完混合证据后反而更确信原立场。其他选项与实验结果相反或无据。

questions.stem: What recommendation does the professor offer for reducing confirmation bias?

options.A: Reading only mainstream news sources
options.B: Seeking out the strongest arguments against one's current position
options.C: Avoiding emotionally charged topics altogether
options.D: Memorizing checklists of common cognitive biases

questions.explanation: 教授明确建议主动寻找反对自己观点的最强论据并与不同意者真诚对话。其他选项均未出现于讲座。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about highly analytical thinkers?

options.A: They are usually immune to confirmation bias.
options.B: They sometimes use their reasoning skill to reinforce existing beliefs.
options.C: They reject emotional arguments before evaluating evidence.
options.D: They tend to change their opinions more quickly than others.

questions.explanation: 教授说更善于分析的人有时会构造更精致的论据来捍卫旧观点，使偏差更强。其他选项与原文相反或无关。

items.subject: history
items.topic: how the printing press changed the spread of scientific ideas
items.transcript: The printing press is sometimes described, a bit too neatly, as the invention that single-handedly created modern science. The reality is more interesting. What movable type actually did was reshape the conditions under which scientific knowledge could circulate, accumulate, and be challenged. Before the press, scientific manuscripts existed in a small number of hand-copied editions. Each copy was expensive, slightly different from every other, and accessible only to those who could travel to specific libraries. A scholar in Vienna and another in Paris might be working from texts that disagreed in subtle but important ways. Now, after Gutenberg, printed editions changed that dynamic in three distinct ways. First, identical copies meant that scholars across distant cities could reference the same diagram, the same numerical table, the same wording. Disputes could finally be settled by pointing to a shared page. Second, printed errata sheets allowed corrections to accumulate over successive editions. A mistake noticed in Bologna in 1530 could be fixed in the next print run in Antwerp. The scientific record became, for the first time, genuinely cumulative. Third, and perhaps most consequentially, the cost of producing a book dropped sharply. This let new and controversial findings reach readers far beyond traditional university circles. Astronomers in particular benefited. Copernicus's De revolutionibus, published in 1543, would have been almost impossible to circulate widely in the manuscript era. Now, this rosy picture has a complication. Print also made it easier to spread mistakes at scale. A textbook error reprinted hundreds of times could entrench misconceptions for generations. So the press accelerated both knowledge and confusion. Understanding which one dominated in any given period requires careful historical work, not a simple celebration of technology.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Gutenberg single-handedly invented modern scientific method
options.B: Why Copernicus rejected the geocentric model of the universe
options.C: How a Vienna scholar's manuscript was corrected in Antwerp
options.D: How the printing press changed the conditions under which scientific ideas circulated

questions.explanation: 讲座核心论点：印刷术改变了科学知识流通、积累与质疑的条件。其他选项要么是反对的论调，要么是细节。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the scholars in Vienna and Paris?

options.A: To illustrate that manuscript copies often differed in subtle ways
options.B: To argue that Paris was the most important center of medieval science
options.C: To explain how scholars used the printing press to translate ancient texts
options.D: To prove that disputes between cities slowed scientific progress

questions.explanation: 维也纳和巴黎的对比是为了说明印刷术之前的手抄本之间存在微妙差异。其他选项与原文主旨或细节不符。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was one direct effect of the printing press on the scientific record?

options.A: Manuscripts replaced printed books as the preferred research format.
options.B: Errata sheets allowed corrections to accumulate across successive editions.
options.C: All printed editions were initially banned by university libraries.
options.D: Identical copies of texts became impossible to produce reliably.

questions.explanation: 教授明确说勘误表使纠正在多次重印中积累，使科学记录变得 cumulative。其他选项与原文事实相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about evaluating the printing press's impact on science?

options.A: It produced unambiguously positive effects in every period.
options.B: It made widespread scientific errors essentially impossible.
options.C: It requires weighing both knowledge spread and amplified mistakes.
options.D: It was historically less important than universities for science.

questions.explanation: 教授结尾说既加速了知识也加速了混淆，需要仔细的历史评估，暗示需要权衡两面。其他选项要么过于乐观要么矛盾。

items.subject: astronomy
items.topic: the role of dark matter in galaxy formation
items.transcript:
Okay, today I want to talk about something that's been puzzling astronomers for decades. When we look at how galaxies move, the math just doesn't add up. The visible stars and gas in a typical spiral galaxy simply don't contain enough mass to keep the outer regions rotating at the speeds we observe. Something invisible must be pulling on them.

That something is what we call dark matter. We can't see it directly because it doesn't absorb or emit light, but we infer its presence from gravity. And here's the key point: dark matter wasn't just decoration in the early universe. It actually drove galaxy formation. In the first few hundred million years after the Big Bang, ordinary matter — hydrogen and helium gas — was too hot and too smooth to collapse on its own. But dark matter, which interacts only through gravity, began clumping much earlier.

These dark matter clumps, which astronomers call halos, became gravitational wells. Ordinary gas then fell into these wells, cooled down, and eventually formed the first stars and galaxies. So in a real sense, every galaxy we see today, including our own Milky Way, sits inside a much larger invisible halo. Without that scaffolding, galaxies as we know them simply couldn't exist.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How dark matter halos enabled galaxies to form in the early universe
options.B: The methods astronomers use to weigh distant spiral galaxies accurately
options.C: The history of the Big Bang theory and its competing alternatives
options.D: Why visible stars rotate slower than the outer arms of galaxies

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是讲解暗物质晕在早期宇宙中如何推动星系形成。A 项准确概括了这一中心论点；B 项只是讲座中提到的现象之一，不是主旨；C 项偏离了主题；D 项颠倒了旋转速度的事实。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did dark matter begin clumping before ordinary matter?

options.A: Because dark matter is significantly heavier than hydrogen and helium gas
options.B: Because dark matter interacts only through gravity and not through light
options.C: Because dark matter halos contained the first stars in the early universe
options.D: Because dark matter cooled at faster rates than the surrounding hot gas

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出，暗物质只通过引力相互作用，所以能在普通气体太热太均匀时先聚集，故 B 正确。A 把质量大小作为原因，不符合讲座；C 颠倒了因果顺序；D 提到的“冷却速度”不是讲座所说的原因。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe dark matter halos as gravitational wells?

options.A: To suggest that astronomers can now image halos directly with telescopes
options.B: To imply that halos prevent dark matter from interacting with ordinary matter
options.C: To explain how ordinary gas was pulled inward to form stars and galaxies
options.D: To indicate that the Milky Way is surrounded by an unusual halo type

questions.explanation: 教授用“引力井”形象比喻晕的功能，是为了说明气体如何被吸入并形成恒星和星系，故 C 正确。A 与讲座无关；B 把功能弄反了；D 过度延伸到银河系的特殊性。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a galaxy without a dark matter halo?

options.A: It would still form but would rotate at much slower outer speeds
options.B: It would eventually generate its own dark matter through gas cooling
options.C: It would resemble the earliest galaxies that astronomers can observe today
options.D: It would not assemble into the kind of structure we recognize as a galaxy

questions.explanation: 教授指出没有暗物质晕的支撑，星系“根本无法存在”，所以 D 是合理推断。A 过度概括地认为仍能形成；B 与讲座说的暗物质不来自普通物质相矛盾；C 与早期星系无关。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how stalactites and stalagmites record environmental changes
items.transcript:
Alright, so let's talk about something most people see only in vacation photographs but that geologists treat as serious scientific data: cave formations, specifically stalactites and stalagmites. These features, which grow as calcium carbonate is deposited by dripping water, can act as natural archives of past climate.

Here's how it works. When rainwater seeps through soil above a cave, it picks up dissolved minerals. As that water drips inside the cave, the minerals slowly accumulate, layer by layer. Each layer carries a chemical signature of the conditions outside at the time it formed. So if you carefully drill a thin core through a stalagmite, you get something a bit like the tree rings botanists love to study — except these records can stretch back hundreds of thousands of years.

What's especially valuable is the chemistry of the oxygen atoms locked inside. The ratio of heavier to lighter oxygen isotopes shifts when temperature or rainfall amounts change. Researchers analyzing caves in southern China, for example, have reconstructed how monsoon patterns weakened during periods of glacial cooling.

And because caves are remarkably stable environments, the record survives largely undisturbed. That makes stalagmite records unusually useful when scientists want to test how Earth's climate has fluctuated over very long timescales.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How rainwater is filtered as it seeps through cave systems worldwide
options.B: How cave formations serve as records of past environmental conditions
options.C: How researchers in southern China discovered ancient cave systems
options.D: How oxygen isotopes determine the age of glacial deposits in Asia

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是解释钟乳石和石笋如何记录过去的环境，B 准确概括。A 只是过程中的一步；C 把例子当成主旨；D 偏离了主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does each layer of a stalagmite preserve?

options.A: A microscopic sample of the cave's living bacteria
options.B: A measurable amount of organic plant material from outside
options.C: A chemical signature of conditions when the layer formed
options.D: A radioactive marker placed by previous research expeditions

questions.explanation: 教授明确说每一层都携带形成时外部条件的化学信号，故 C 正确。A 把生物因素引入；B 把有机物质混淆进来；D 与讲座完全无关。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare stalagmite cores to tree rings?

options.A: To suggest that stalagmites are studied mainly by plant biologists
options.B: To argue that tree-ring analysis is the more precise of the two methods
options.C: To indicate that scientists prefer studying caves over forests for climate data
options.D: To highlight that stalagmites also provide a sequenced historical record

questions.explanation: 教授用树木年轮作类比，是为了强调石笋同样提供按时间顺序排列的记录，故 D 正确。A 错把研究学科混淆；B 把比较解释成排名；C 引入了讲座未提到的偏好。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the stable conditions inside caves?

options.A: Cave stability allows the chemical record to remain reliable across long periods
options.B: Cave stability prevents researchers from collecting fresh dripping samples
options.C: Cave stability ensures that all stalagmites grow at exactly the same rate
options.D: Cave stability is the primary reason caves attract recreational visitors

questions.explanation: 教授提到稳定环境使记录长期未受干扰，A 是合理推断。B 把稳定误解为阻碍采样；C 把稳定夸大为生长速率一致；D 与讲座无关。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: the concept of carbon sequestration in forests and soils
items.transcript:
So today we're going to look at carbon sequestration — the process of capturing carbon dioxide from the atmosphere and storing it for long periods of time. Most discussions focus on technological capture, but the natural systems, particularly forests and soils, actually account for a much larger share of the carbon currently being removed each year.

Forests sequester carbon in two main pools. Above ground, trees lock carbon into wood, leaves, and branches as they grow. Below ground, however, the larger and often overlooked pool sits in the soil itself, where decaying roots and fallen leaves are gradually transformed into stable organic compounds. Soil organic matter can hold carbon for centuries if the soil remains undisturbed.

But here's where things get complicated. The same forests that sequester carbon can release it again when they burn, when trees are harvested, or when soil is plowed. So sequestration is not a one-way process — it's a balance. A mature forest may store a lot of carbon, but it isn't necessarily adding to that pool every year.

When policymakers talk about using forests for climate mitigation, they really need to think about both pools, the time scales involved, and the risk that disturbance can release stored carbon back into the atmosphere relatively quickly.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why technological carbon capture currently outperforms natural sequestration globally
options.B: How forest harvesting practices became central to international climate negotiations
options.C: How natural carbon sequestration works and why it remains complicated to manage
options.D: Why soil organic matter forms the largest carbon pool on the planet's surface

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是介绍自然碳封存机制及其管理复杂性，C 准确。A 与讲座观点相反；B 偏离主旨；D 过于具体且未必准确。

questions.stem: According to the professor, where does the larger forest carbon pool reside?

options.A: Inside leaves and branches of mature canopy trees
options.B: Inside specialized fungi attached to forest tree roots
options.C: Inside the stable wood produced by old-growth conifer species
options.D: Inside soil organic matter formed from decaying material

questions.explanation: 教授指出常被忽视的更大碳库是土壤有机质，故 D 正确。A 是地上较小的部分；B 与讲座无关；C 偏离了讲座的对比重点。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what can release sequestered carbon back into the atmosphere?

options.A: Wildfires, harvesting, and plowing of the soil layer
options.B: Long droughts that prevent any new tree growth
options.C: Cold winters that slow microbial activity inside forest soils
options.D: Heavy rainfall events that flood the lower root systems

questions.explanation: 教授明确列出燃烧、采伐和翻耕会释放碳，A 正确。B 干扰项错把干旱当成释放原因；C 颠倒了寒冷的效果；D 与讲座无关。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe sequestration as a balance rather than a one-way process?

options.A: To indicate that mature forests stop sequestering carbon at predictable ages
options.B: To emphasize that gains can be reversed by disturbances within the same forest
options.C: To argue that soil pools always outweigh the above-ground carbon storage
options.D: To suggest that forests are now expanding faster than they are being lost

questions.explanation: 教授强调“平衡”是为了说明扰动会让原本储存的碳重新释放，B 正确。A 把比喻夸大为可预测时间；C 偏离重点；D 与讲座无关。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript:
Alright, let's turn to the Impressionists for a moment. One of the things that really set them apart from earlier painters was where they actually worked. Before the 1870s, most serious painting happened inside studios. Artists made quick outdoor sketches, sure, but the finished oil paintings were built up later, in carefully controlled indoor light.

The Impressionists changed that. Painters like Monet and Pissarro began carrying their canvases and easels outside and finishing entire pictures right there in the field. And this wasn't just a fashion. Recent inventions — collapsible easels and ready-mixed paint in metal tubes — finally made outdoor work practical.

Now, here's why that matters for color. When you paint indoors, you tend to mix colors that look right under one steady kind of light. But sunlight outdoors shifts constantly throughout the day. So the Impressionists started noticing that shadows aren't really gray or black — they pick up blues and purples from the sky, and warm tones from nearby surfaces.

That insight changed painting. Instead of mixing one neutral shadow color, Impressionists laid down small touches of pure color side by side and let the viewer's eye blend them. So outdoor painting wasn't just a habit — it produced a whole new color theory.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The financial difficulties faced by individual Impressionist painters in Paris
options.B: How the invention of ready-mixed paint tubes affected nineteenth-century commerce
options.C: The technical training that prepared Impressionists for outdoor field painting
options.D: How outdoor painting shaped the Impressionists' approach to color and shadow

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是户外作画如何塑造印象派的色彩理论，D 正确。A 偏离主题；B 把背景误为主旨；C 与讲座无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what made outdoor finishing of paintings practical?

options.A: Portable easels and ready-mixed paint sold in metal tubes
options.B: Government grants that funded large outdoor studios in Paris
options.C: A new generation of pigments that resisted fading in sunlight
options.D: Improved transportation networks linking villages to the major cities

questions.explanation: 教授提到可折叠画架和金属管颜料让户外作画成为可能，A 正确。B 与讲座无关；C 把颜料的属性弄错；D 是不相关的背景。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Impressionists notice about shadows?

options.A: Shadows actually contain a range of cool and warm colors
options.B: Shadows usually appear darker than the surface they fall upon
options.C: Shadows are best rendered using carefully mixed neutral grays
options.D: Shadows change less than the surrounding sunlit areas do

questions.explanation: 教授指出影子带有蓝紫色和暖色调，A 正确。B 是常识性陈述但非讲座要点；C 与讲座观点相反；D 把变化关系颠倒。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about earlier painters working in indoor studios?

options.A: They generally relied on a less varied range of shadow colors than Impressionists did
options.B: They typically left their canvases unfinished due to unstable interior lighting conditions
options.C: They consciously avoided sunlight because outdoor pigments faded extremely quickly
options.D: They generally produced more accurate reproductions of natural landscape scenery

questions.explanation: 教授说室内画家在稳定光线下倾向于混合一种灰中性影子，因此推断他们使用的影子色彩更单一，A 正确。B 与讲座说的“完成画作”相反；C 把户外原因混淆；D 与讲座暗示相反。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Expressionism represented emotional experience over physical reality
items.transcript: Expressionism is one of the most influential art movements of the early twentieth century, and it was born from a specific frustration. Painters like Edvard Munch and Ernst Ludwig Kirchner felt that the dominant styles of the late nineteenth century had become obsessed with surface appearance — with light, with optical accuracy. They wanted to do something completely different. They wanted to paint what was happening inside the figure, not outside. So the central idea of Expressionism is this: the canvas should express subjective emotional experience, not reproduce visible reality. That's the move. And once you make that move, everything else follows. You don't need realistic skin tones, so a face can be green or scarlet to register fear. You don't need straight architectural lines, so a street can buckle and tilt to register anxiety. Take Munch's most famous painting, The Scream, from 1893. The sky is striped with violent oranges, the human figure is dissolving, and the bridge in the background curves unnaturally. None of that is what Munch saw with his eyes. He's painting the experience of overwhelming dread. And he said as much in his diary — he wrote that he felt a great scream pass through nature, and the painting is his attempt to record that internal scream visually. Now, this approach really did change painting permanently. After Expressionism, the assumption that a serious artist's job was to copy the visible world was no longer the only legitimate position. The internal life of the subject became valid material. And you'll see that shift carry forward into Abstract Expressionism, into film, even into modern graphic design.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Expressionism prioritized inner emotion over visual accuracy
options.B: The biographical life of Edvard Munch and his diaries
options.C: How nineteenth-century painters used optical realism in landscapes
options.D: The technical materials used by early twentieth-century painters

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是表现主义如何把表达内在情感置于复制视觉现实之上。B 只是用来举例的细节，C 是被反对的对立立场，D 与讲座无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why might an Expressionist painter use a green or scarlet face?

options.A: To compete with photography for realistic accuracy
options.B: To register an emotional state such as fear
options.C: To match the bright color choices of Impressionists
options.D: To experiment with new chemical pigments

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说脸可以是绿色或猩红色来表现恐惧。A 与表现主义反对照相式真实相矛盾，C 是与印象派无关的混淆，D 是讲座没提的话题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Munch record in his diary about The Scream?

options.A: That he painted it from a sketch made years earlier
options.B: That he chose the colors to match the actual sunset
options.C: That he felt a great scream pass through nature
options.D: That he intended it to be displayed only in private

questions.explanation: 讲座原文引用了 Munch 日记中说他感到一声尖叫穿过自然。A、D 都是没有提到的细节，B 与 Munch 反对照实复制颜色的立场相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the legacy of Expressionism?

options.A: It revived the academic standards of nineteenth-century painting
options.B: It is now considered an artistic dead end with no successors
options.C: It produced techniques used mainly outside the visual arts
options.D: It permanently expanded what counts as legitimate artistic subject matter

questions.explanation: 讲座说表现主义之后，内在生活成为合法的艺术材料，并影响了抽象表现主义、电影甚至平面设计。A、B 与讲座相反，C 把影响范围缩得太窄。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the diffusion of responsibility in large groups
items.transcript: Social psychologists have spent decades studying a counterintuitive pattern in human behavior: when more people witness an emergency, any individual witness is actually less likely to step in and help. This pattern is called the diffusion of responsibility, and it gets its name in a literal sense — responsibility seems to spread thin and dilute across the size of the group. The classic series of studies on this comes from John Darley and Bibb Latané in 1968. They put students in a room and piped in what sounded like another student having a seizure in a nearby room. Now, when the participant believed they were the only witness, eighty-five percent intervened within a minute. But when they believed four other people were also listening, that number dropped to thirty-one percent. Same emergency, same participant population — the only variable was perceived group size. Now, let me make a point about how to interpret this. It would be easy to conclude that the participants who didn't help were callous, but Darley and Latané argued strongly against that reading. The participants who didn't help were genuinely upset. They were sweating, pacing, asking the experimenter what to do. They weren't apathetic — they were paralyzed by the assumption that someone else would handle it. That distinction matters because it changes what we should do about it. If the problem were callousness, you'd need to change people's values. But if the problem is the math of a crowd, then the solution is much more practical: in any emergency, name a specific person. "You, in the blue jacket, call an ambulance." That single act collapses the diffusion and assigns responsibility to one identifiable individual. Crisis training in hospitals and aviation now teaches exactly this technique.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How emergency-room nurses respond differently from bystanders
options.B: Why individual willingness to help decreases as group size increases
options.C: The methodology used in Darley and Latané's seizure experiment
options.D: Differences in moral values across modern cultures

questions.explanation: 讲座主题是责任扩散：群体越大，个体越不愿介入。A 与讲座无关，C 只是讲座中举例的实验细节，D 偏离了讲座关于群体规模的核心。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the participants who were sweating and pacing?

options.A: To show that the experiment caused unethical psychological harm
options.B: To compare seizure symptoms with anxiety symptoms
options.C: To establish that the non-helpers were paralyzed rather than indifferent
options.D: To suggest that the experimental design was poorly executed

questions.explanation: 讲座用这些细节证明不帮忙的人不是冷漠而是被动僵住。A 把这扩展成伦理批评，B 是对症状的字面误读，D 是讲座没有支持的判断。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the "you, in the blue jacket" instruction?

options.A: To illustrate that emergency clothing should be reflective
options.B: To repeat what Darley and Latané told their participants
options.C: To imply that aviation crews wear color-coded uniforms
options.D: To demonstrate a practical technique that overrides diffusion of responsibility

questions.explanation: 讲座用这个例子展示如何通过指名打破责任扩散。A 是对蓝夹克的字面误读，B 是讲座没有的事实错误，C 是与场景不符的过度推断。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how to reduce bystander inaction?

options.A: Practical group-size interventions can be more effective than changing values
options.B: Bystander inaction can only be reduced through years of moral education
options.C: Aviation training is the only setting where these techniques succeed
options.D: Larger groups will always respond better than smaller ones to emergencies

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示比起改变价值观，群体规模的实用干预更有效。B 与讲座相反，C 把适用范围说得太窄，D 与讲座论点直接相反。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how different cultures conceptualize time as linear vs cyclical
items.transcript: One of the more striking findings to come out of cross-cultural cognitive anthropology is that human societies do not share a single way of conceptualizing time. The framework we tend to assume in industrial societies — that time is a one-way arrow running from past to future, with each moment never returning — is sometimes called linear time. But it isn't universal. Many other cultures, especially those organized around agricultural or ritual cycles, conceptualize time as fundamentally cyclical: a series of repeating phases that loop rather than advance. Now, this distinction is not just a metaphor in people's heads. It shapes everything from grammar to economic planning to bereavement practices. Let me give you a concrete example. In several Andean Quechua-speaking communities, ethnographers have documented something unusual about how speakers gesture when discussing time. Most speakers in linear-time cultures point behind themselves to indicate the past — because the past is, in a sense, behind us. But Quechua speakers point in front, because, as they explain, the past is what they can see — it has already happened — and the future is what they cannot see, which is therefore behind them. So the spatial mapping is inverted. Now, why does this matter beyond a fascinating ethnographic detail? Because economic and policy frameworks built on linear assumptions can collide badly with cyclical-time worldviews. Development programs that emphasize a single irreversible trajectory of growth often miss why communities resist abandoning seasonal practices, because for those communities, those practices are not delays — they are how time actually moves. Recognizing the diversity of time concepts isn't just academic. It's a precondition for designing policy that doesn't impose one cognitive model on populations that organize reality through a different one.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why Andean economies have grown more slowly than industrial economies
options.B: How grammatical features predict the development of agriculture
options.C: How different cultures conceptualize time and why that matters
options.D: Why all human cultures eventually adopt a linear view of time

questions.explanation: 讲座论述不同文化对时间的不同概念化以及其影响。A 是对例子的曲解，B 与讲座无关，D 与讲座反对的普遍化立场相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Quechua speakers pointing in front to indicate the past?

options.A: To suggest that Quechua grammar is influenced by Spanish contact
options.B: To recommend that linguists revise their gesture classification system
options.C: To restate that Quechua speakers gesture differently from other groups
options.D: To illustrate that a non-linear time concept reshapes spatial reasoning

questions.explanation: 这个例子说明非线性时间观如何重塑空间推理。A 是讲座没有的猜测，B 是对内容的过度引申，C 只是把事实重复了一遍而没解释其用意。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why might development programs fail in cyclical-time communities?

options.A: They treat seasonal practices as delays rather than as time itself
options.B: They underestimate the literacy levels of agricultural workers
options.C: They depend on government subsidies that are not delivered
options.D: They overestimate the climate stability of the target regions

questions.explanation: 讲座说线性框架把季节性实践当作延迟，而对当地人来说那些就是时间本身。B、C、D 都是讲座没有提到的实际原因。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about policy design?

options.A: Policy should always defer entirely to local cosmologies without modification
options.B: Policy must take cognitive diversity into account to avoid imposing one model
options.C: Policy is generally too abstract to be informed by anthropological research
options.D: Policy frameworks based on linear time succeed once translated correctly

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示政策设计必须考虑认知多样性以免强加单一模型。A 是夸大，C 与讲座立场相反，D 与讲座对线性模型局限的批评相反。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: the cocktail party effect and selective attention
items.transcript: The cocktail party effect is one of the most studied phenomena in attention research, and it gets its name from a very ordinary experience. You're at a noisy party. There are dozens of conversations happening around you. And yet, somehow, you're able to focus on the one person you're talking to and basically tune out everyone else. That selective filtering is what cognitive psychologists call the cocktail party effect. Now, the first systematic work on this was done by Colin Cherry at Imperial College London in 1953. Cherry put participants in a setup where they wore headphones, and a different audio stream was played into each ear. He told them to repeat aloud, in real time, the message coming into one specific ear and to ignore the other. This procedure is called dichotic listening, and what Cherry wanted to know was how much of the ignored stream people actually noticed. The results were striking. Participants could repeat the attended message accurately, but they remembered almost nothing about the content of the unattended message. They didn't notice when the language switched, they didn't notice when the speaker changed gender — they essentially filtered the unattended ear out at a very deep level. But — and this is the famous twist — most participants did notice if their own name was spoken in the unattended ear. That's now called the own-name effect, and it tells us that selective attention isn't a complete shutdown. Some filter remains open, monitoring for signals that matter to us personally. So the cocktail party effect is really two findings at once: we filter aggressively, but the filter still lets through information that's biologically or socially urgent.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why social parties have grown noisier over the last century
options.B: How headphones changed the design of psychology laboratories
options.C: How human languages differ in their use of attention markers
options.D: How the brain selectively filters competing streams of speech

questions.explanation: 讲座讲大脑如何在多个语流中选择性过滤。A、B、C 都是和讲座无关或离题的方向。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Cherry's dichotic listening task require participants to do?

options.A: Repeat aloud the message played into one specific ear
options.B: Identify the gender of the speaker on each side
options.C: Recall the unattended message word for word
options.D: Switch attention between ears every thirty seconds

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说参与者需复述指定耳朵中的信息。B、D 是讲座没有提的任务，C 与实验结果相反——参与者几乎记不住非关注耳的内容。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the own-name effect?

options.A: To repeat that participants ignored the unattended message
options.B: To show that the attentional filter still admits personally important signals
options.C: To suggest that names are stored in a separate part of the brain
options.D: To argue that Cherry's experimental design was flawed

questions.explanation: 提到名字效应是为了说明过滤并非彻底关闭，仍会放过对个人重要的信号。A 只是复述前文，C 是越界推断，D 与讲座对实验的肯定相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about selective attention?

options.A: It works equally well for any signal we want to monitor
options.B: It shuts out all sensory input from the unattended channel
options.C: It permits some signals through based on personal relevance
options.D: It is largely a learned skill that disappears in noisy settings

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示选择性注意会放过与个人相关的信号。A 是过度概括，B 与讲座结论相反，D 与讲座对鸡尾酒效应的描述无关。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: So today I want to take you back to Paris, around 1870, and ask a question that probably sounds obvious now but was genuinely radical at the time. Why did the Impressionists paint outdoors? Why did Monet, Pissarro, Renoir leave the studio and set up their easels in fields and on riverbanks? The standard answer is that they wanted realism. But that's not quite right. They wanted something more specific. They wanted to capture light. And once you understand that goal, you understand why everything else in Impressionism follows from it. Now, think about what happens to color in a studio. The light is constant, often filtered through north-facing windows. Shadows are stable. A painter could spend weeks on a canvas and the conditions never changed. But outdoors, light shifts every minute. A field at ten in the morning is a completely different color from the same field at two in the afternoon. The Impressionists realized that shadows were not gray or black. They were colored. A shadow on grass might be violet or deep blue, because it picks up reflected light from the sky. This was a complete break from academic painting, which used dark earth tones to depict shadow. So when Monet painted his haystacks series, the same haystacks, over and over, at different times of day, he wasn't being repetitive. He was documenting how light changes color itself. The haystack is just a pretext. The real subject is the light.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Impressionists painted outdoors to capture shifting light
options.B: The biographical relationship between Monet and Pissarro
options.C: The technical methods used by academic painters in studios
options.D: The marketing strategies that made Impressionism commercially successful

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是印象派为了捕捉变化的光线而走出画室。B 与个人传记无关，C 是被对比的反面，D 完全没提到。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what surprising discovery did the Impressionists make about shadows?

options.A: Shadows were always darker than expected in outdoor scenes
options.B: Shadows were not gray or black but contained reflected colors
options.C: Shadows shifted in shape every minute due to wind movement
options.D: Shadows could only be painted accurately during midday hours

questions.explanation: 教授明确说阴影不是灰色或黑色，而是带颜色的，能反映天空的光。A 与讲座对学院派的描述相反，C 与形状无关，D 是与白天时间的混淆。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Monet's haystacks series?

options.A: To argue that Monet eventually abandoned outdoor painting
options.B: To explain why Monet preferred rural subjects to city scenes
options.C: To illustrate how the same subject documents changing light
options.D: To compare Monet's productivity with that of other Impressionists

questions.explanation: 干草堆系列被用来说明印象派如何用同一物体记录光线变化。A 与讲座立场相反，B 是表面词陷阱，D 是讲座没提的对比。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did academic painters use to depict shadow?

options.A: Bright cobalt and ultramarine blue pigments
options.B: Reflected light from neighboring color surfaces
options.C: Multiple thin layers of unmixed primary colors
options.D: Dark earth tones such as browns and blacks

questions.explanation: 教授对比印象派和学院派，说学院派用深的土色调画阴影。A 是印象派的颜色，B 是印象派的方法，C 是与混合颜料相关的混淆。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: how dual-process theory explains fast and slow thinking
items.transcript: Okay, so today I want to walk you through one of the most influential ideas in modern psychology. It's called dual-process theory, and the basic claim is that your mind has two different ways of thinking. Psychologists usually call them System 1 and System 2. So System 1 is fast. It's automatic, it doesn't feel like effort. When you see a friend's face and recognize them, that's System 1. When you drive on a familiar road and don't really remember the last five minutes of driving, that's System 1. It runs in the background, all the time, making quick judgments based on patterns and emotions. Now, System 2 is the opposite. It's slow, it's deliberate, and it actually feels like work. When you're solving a math problem, weighing the pros and cons of a big decision, or learning a new skill, you're running System 2. It uses a lot of mental energy, which is why we get tired after a long day of focused work. Here's the key point. We rely on System 1 most of the time because it's efficient. But System 1 also makes mistakes. It's prone to biases — confirmation bias, anchoring, the way first impressions stick. So good thinkers learn to recognize when a problem is hard enough that System 1 will get it wrong, and they deliberately slow down and switch to System 2. Next class, we'll look at specific experiments by Daniel Kahneman that demonstrate exactly when each system kicks in.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Daniel Kahneman's biography and main publications
options.B: The dual-process theory and its two cognitive systems
options.C: How mathematical reasoning differs from emotional reasoning
options.D: Why mental fatigue affects long-term decision-making outcomes

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是双系统理论。A 是细节人物，C 是片面，D 是单一细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what kind of thinking is System 1?

options.A: Slow and energy-intensive
options.B: Reserved only for new tasks
options.C: Fast, automatic, and effortless
options.D: Only used during sleep

questions.explanation: 教授描述系统1是快速、自动、不费力的。A 是系统2，B 颠倒了，D 完全不对。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention recognizing a friend's face?

options.A: To argue that face recognition uses System 2 thinking
options.B: To suggest that System 1 is reserved for emergency responses
options.C: To compare visual processing with mathematical reasoning
options.D: To give a concrete example of System 1 in everyday life

questions.explanation: 认脸是为了说明系统1日常运行的例子。A 反了，B 与紧急无关，C 是讲座没做的对比。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss in the next class?

options.A: Specific experiments by Daniel Kahneman showing when each system activates
options.B: Critiques of dual-process theory by neuroscientists
options.C: How children develop the two thinking systems over time
options.D: Medical conditions that impair one of the two systems

questions.explanation: 教授明说下节课讲 Kahneman 的实验。B/C/D 都是讲座没暗示的方向。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: So here's a strange fact about human memory. When you remember an experience — a vacation, a concert, a difficult meeting — your memory doesn't actually average out everything that happened. It compresses the whole event down to two specific moments: the most emotionally intense moment, and the way the experience ended. Psychologists call this the peak-end rule. Daniel Kahneman demonstrated it most famously in a study involving cold water. Participants were asked to keep one hand in painfully cold water for sixty seconds. Then, in a separate trial, the same participants kept their hand in the same cold water for sixty seconds, plus an additional thirty seconds in slightly warmer water. Logically, the second trial was worse. It had thirty extra seconds of discomfort. But when asked which trial they would rather repeat, most participants chose the longer one. Why? Because the second trial ended with the water getting warmer. The end was less painful, so the whole experience was remembered as more bearable. This finding has huge practical implications. It means that businesses can deliberately design experiences with strong endings. Restaurants give you a small free dessert at the end of the meal. Theme parks save their best fireworks for last. The middle of the experience can be flat, but the ending dominates what you remember. The peak matters too — one moment of intense joy, or intense frustration, will define how you recall the whole thing.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How memory chooses to encode entire experiences neutrally
options.B: Why customers prefer shorter restaurant visits to longer ones
options.C: How memory compresses experiences into peak and end moments
options.D: The biological basis of pain tolerance in cold-water experiments

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是峰终定律。A 与讲座立场相反，B 是从例子误推的细节，D 是细节支撑。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did most participants prefer the longer cold-water trial?

options.A: Because the longer trial used colder water overall
options.B: Because they wanted to demonstrate higher pain tolerance
options.C: Because the second trial was administered later in the experiment
options.D: Because the ending of the longer trial was less painful

questions.explanation: 更长的一次以更温暖的水结束。A 与事实相反，B/C 都是讲座没提的猜测。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what two moments dominate how an experience is remembered?

options.A: The most emotionally intense moment and the ending
options.B: The first impression and the average of all moments
options.C: The longest stretch of comfort and the longest stretch of discomfort
options.D: The starting expectation and the final cost

questions.explanation: 教授明确说是最强烈的瞬间和结尾。其他都是错误描述。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention restaurants giving small free desserts?

options.A: To criticize how businesses exploit psychological research
options.B: To illustrate how businesses engineer positive endings deliberately
options.C: To explain why dessert is the most enjoyable part of a meal
options.D: To compare restaurant pricing strategies with theme parks

questions.explanation: 免费甜点是说明企业有意设计积极结尾的例子。A 是讲座没有的立场，C/D 是分散注意的解读。

items.subject: physics
