options.A: It is the single largest cause of the urban heat island
options.B: It can make the urban heat island problem worse
options.C: It has little effect on a city's overall temperature
options.D: It cools cities enough to offset heat from other sources

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说高温增加空调需求，'raises energy use and, ironically, generates even more waste heat'，可推断空调会使热岛问题更严重。A是over_generalization，讲座说最大因素是建筑材料，而非把空调说成单一最大成因。C是reversed_logic，讲座暗示空调显著加剧升温，并非影响甚微。D是reversed_logic，空调向空气排放废热使城市更热，而非降温抵消其他热源。

items.subject: linguistics
items.topic: how children acquire grammar without being explicitly taught the rules
items.transcript: Think about a four-year-old child. She's never had a grammar lesson, she can't tell you what a verb or a subject is, and yet she produces sentences that follow remarkably complex rules. How is that possible? This puzzle sits at the heart of the study of language acquisition. Children acquire grammar not by memorizing rules, but by unconsciously extracting patterns from the speech they hear around them. And here's the striking evidence: they make errors that no adult ever models for them. A child will say 'I goed to the park' or 'I have two foots.' Now, nobody talks like that. The child has never heard an adult say 'goed.' So where does it come from? The answer is that the child has internalized a rule, namely, that you add an '-ed' to make the past tense, and is applying it consistently, even to irregular verbs. Linguists call this overregularization. It's actually a sign of intelligence, not error. The child isn't parroting. She's generating language from a rule she worked out herself. This observation led the linguist Noam Chomsky to argue that humans are born with an innate capacity for language, a kind of mental blueprint for grammar. The input children receive, he pointed out, is often too messy and incomplete to fully explain how quickly and uniformly they learn. So rather than being blank slates, children may come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them. The errors, paradoxically, reveal the hidden machinery of the mind at work.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How adults can best teach grammar rules to young children
options.B: How children come to follow grammar rules without being taught them
options.C: Why irregular verbs are the hardest part of learning English
options.D: The biography and major theories of the linguist Noam Chomsky

questions.explanation: 正确答案B概括全篇——孩子如何在无人明确教授的情况下掌握语法规则，这是讲座从谜题、过度规则化证据到乔姆斯基理论始终围绕的核心。A是reversed_logic，讲座恰恰强调孩子并非被大人教会，而是自己提取规律。C是too_narrow，不规则动词只是说明过度规则化的例子，不是主旨。D是too_narrow，乔姆斯基只是被引用来支持论点的一位学者，讲座并非他的传记。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do children say things like 'goed' or 'foots'?

options.A: They are copying errors they hear adults make
options.B: They have applied a regular rule to an irregular word
options.C: They are unable to remember words they hear
options.D: They are deliberately playing with sounds for fun

questions.explanation: 正确答案B对应讲座解释——孩子内化了'加-ed构成过去式'的规则，并一致地套用到不规则动词上，即过度规则化。A是reversed_logic，讲座明确说'nobody talks like that'，孩子从未听大人这样说，并非模仿。C是wrong_detail，讲座说孩子是在自己生成规则，而非记不住单词。D是topic_adjacent，听上去合理，但讲座说这是套用规则的结果，并非故意玩声音。

questions.stem: Why does the professor say that overregularization is 'a sign of intelligence, not error'?

options.A: To emphasize that the child is generating language from a rule she worked out
options.B: To argue that children are smarter than adults at learning language
options.C: To point out that the child has simply memorized many new words
options.D: To show that such mistakes will disappear as the child grows older

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授强调过度规则化是智力的体现，是为了说明孩子并非鹦鹉学舌，而是依据自己推导出的规则生成语言。B是over_inference，讲座没有比较孩子和成人谁更聪明。C是literal_restatement并曲解，讲座恰恰说孩子不是死记硬背，而是运用规则。D是over_inference，讲座此处未谈这些错误是否会随年龄消失。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about Chomsky's view of children's minds?

options.A: Children learn language mainly by carefully imitating adult speech
options.B: Children are born with a built-in framework that guides language learning
options.C: Children must be explicitly taught grammar to learn it correctly
options.D: Children's language ability depends mostly on how much input they get

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授引述乔姆斯基的观点，认为孩子并非白板，而是'come equipped with a built-in framework that guides them'，即天生具备引导语言学习的内在框架。A是reversed_logic，讲座强调孩子不是单纯模仿大人。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是孩子无需被明确教授就能掌握语法。D是topic_adjacent并被反驳，乔姆斯基恰恰指出输入'too messy and incomplete'，不足以解释学习，因此能力不主要取决于输入量。

items.subject: economics
items.topic: why people value something more once they own it (the endowment effect)
items.transcript: Imagine I give you a coffee mug, just a plain one, for free. A few minutes later, I offer to buy it back from you. How much would you ask for? Now imagine instead that you never received the mug, and I simply ask how much you'd pay to buy one. Studies show something surprising. The people who own the mug typically demand about twice as much to sell it as the people without one are willing to pay. The mug hasn't changed. Only ownership has. This is what economists call the endowment effect. The endowment effect is the tendency for people to value something more highly simply because they own it. It was demonstrated famously by Daniel Kahneman and his colleagues in a series of experiments in the 1990s, often using exactly this kind of mug. Why does this happen? A big part of the explanation is something called loss aversion. Psychologically, losing something feels worse than gaining the equivalent thing feels good. So once the mug is yours, giving it up registers as a painful loss, and you demand a higher price to compensate. Notice that this challenges a basic assumption in traditional economics, the idea that an object has a stable value independent of who holds it. The endowment effect shows that value is partly psychological, shaped by ownership and the fear of loss. This has practical implications, from how companies offer free trials to how we cling to possessions we'd never actually choose to buy again.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How companies design free trial offers for new products
options.B: The experiments Daniel Kahneman conducted in the 1990s
options.C: A tendency to value things more highly once we own them
options.D: Why coffee mugs are commonly used in economic research

questions.explanation: 正确答案C概括全篇——人们一旦拥有某物就会更看重它的倾向，即禀赋效应，这是讲座定义、实验和理论含义始终围绕的主题。A是too_narrow，免费试用只是结尾提到的一个实际应用。B是too_narrow，Kahneman的实验只是支撑主题的证据之一。D是surface_word_trap，借用了反复出现的'mug'，但杯子只是实验道具，并非讲座主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did studies find about mug owners?

options.A: They demanded about twice as much to sell as buyers would pay
options.B: They refused to sell the mug at any price at all
options.C: They valued the mug exactly as much as non-owners did
options.D: They were willing to pay more than non-owners to buy one

questions.explanation: 正确答案A对应讲座原文——拥有杯子的人开出的卖价'about twice as much'于无杯者愿意支付的买价。B是wrong_detail，讲座说他们要价更高，并非拒绝以任何价格出售。C是reversed_logic，讲座的核心正是拥有者比非拥有者更看重，而非估值相同。D是wrong_detail，混淆了买卖双方——是拥有者的卖价高，而非他们更愿出钱去买。

questions.stem: Why does the professor explain the concept of loss aversion?

options.A: To explain why owning something makes giving it up feel painful
options.B: To prove that people enjoy gains more than they fear losses
options.C: To describe how Kahneman first discovered the coffee mug study
options.D: To argue that all economic decisions are based on emotion

questions.explanation: 正确答案A点明用意——教授引入损失厌恶是为了解释为何一旦拥有某物，放弃它会被感知为痛苦的损失，从而支撑禀赋效应。B是reversed_logic，损失厌恶恰恰指失去比获得更痛苦，与此选项相反。C是wrong_purpose，损失厌恶用于解释机制，与如何发现杯子实验无关。D是over_inference，教授说价值'partly psychological'，并未主张所有经济决策都基于情绪。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the traditional economic view of value?

options.A: It correctly predicts how owners price their possessions
options.B: It is incomplete because value is partly shaped by ownership
options.C: It has been fully replaced by the concept of loss aversion
options.D: It applies only to inexpensive everyday objects like mugs

questions.explanation: 正确答案B是合理推断——教授说禀赋效应'challenges'传统经济学关于价值稳定、与持有者无关的假设，可推断该传统观点并不完整，因为价值部分受拥有权影响。A是reversed_logic，传统观点恰恰无法解释拥有者要价更高的现象。C是over_generalization，讲座说'challenges'而非'完全取代'，损失厌恶是解释禀赋效应的原因之一。D是topic_adjacent，讲座没有说传统价值观只适用于廉价日常物品。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Expressionism represented emotional experience over physical reality
items.transcript: Expressionism is one of the most influential art movements of the early twentieth century, and it was born from a specific frustration. Painters like Edvard Munch and Ernst Ludwig Kirchner felt that the dominant styles of the late nineteenth century had become obsessed with surface appearance — with light, with optical accuracy. They wanted to do something completely different. They wanted to paint what was happening inside the figure, not outside. So the central idea of Expressionism is this: the canvas should express subjective emotional experience, not reproduce visible reality. That's the move. And once you make that move, everything else follows. You don't need realistic skin tones, so a face can be green or scarlet to register fear. You don't need straight architectural lines, so a street can buckle and tilt to register anxiety. Take Munch's most famous painting, The Scream, from 1893. The sky is striped with violent oranges, the human figure is dissolving, and the bridge in the background curves unnaturally. None of that is what Munch saw with his eyes. He's painting the experience of overwhelming dread. And he said as much in his diary — he wrote that he felt a great scream pass through nature, and the painting is his attempt to record that internal scream visually. Now, this approach really did change painting permanently. After Expressionism, the assumption that a serious artist's job was to copy the visible world was no longer the only legitimate position. The internal life of the subject became valid material. And you'll see that shift carry forward into Abstract Expressionism, into film, even into modern graphic design.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Expressionism prioritized inner emotion over visual accuracy
options.B: The biographical life of Edvard Munch and his diaries
options.C: How nineteenth-century painters used optical realism in landscapes
options.D: The technical materials used by early twentieth-century painters

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是表现主义如何把表达内在情感置于复制视觉现实之上。B 只是用来举例的细节，C 是被反对的对立立场，D 与讲座无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why might an Expressionist painter use a green or scarlet face?

options.A: To compete with photography for realistic accuracy
options.B: To register an emotional state such as fear
options.C: To match the bright color choices of Impressionists
options.D: To experiment with new chemical pigments

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说脸可以是绿色或猩红色来表现恐惧。A 与表现主义反对照相式真实相矛盾，C 是与印象派无关的混淆，D 是讲座没提的话题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Munch record in his diary about The Scream?

options.A: That he painted it from a sketch made years earlier
options.B: That he chose the colors to match the actual sunset
options.C: That he felt a great scream pass through nature
options.D: That he intended it to be displayed only in private

questions.explanation: 讲座原文引用了 Munch 日记中说他感到一声尖叫穿过自然。A、D 都是没有提到的细节，B 与 Munch 反对照实复制颜色的立场相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the legacy of Expressionism?

options.A: It revived the academic standards of nineteenth-century painting
options.B: It is now considered an artistic dead end with no successors
options.C: It produced techniques used mainly outside the visual arts
options.D: It permanently expanded what counts as legitimate artistic subject matter

questions.explanation: 讲座说表现主义之后，内在生活成为合法的艺术材料，并影响了抽象表现主义、电影甚至平面设计。A、B 与讲座相反，C 把影响范围缩得太窄。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the diffusion of responsibility in large groups
items.transcript: Social psychologists have spent decades studying a counterintuitive pattern in human behavior: when more people witness an emergency, any individual witness is actually less likely to step in and help. This pattern is called the diffusion of responsibility, and it gets its name in a literal sense — responsibility seems to spread thin and dilute across the size of the group. The classic series of studies on this comes from John Darley and Bibb Latané in 1968. They put students in a room and piped in what sounded like another student having a seizure in a nearby room. Now, when the participant believed they were the only witness, eighty-five percent intervened within a minute. But when they believed four other people were also listening, that number dropped to thirty-one percent. Same emergency, same participant population — the only variable was perceived group size. Now, let me make a point about how to interpret this. It would be easy to conclude that the participants who didn't help were callous, but Darley and Latané argued strongly against that reading. The participants who didn't help were genuinely upset. They were sweating, pacing, asking the experimenter what to do. They weren't apathetic — they were paralyzed by the assumption that someone else would handle it. That distinction matters because it changes what we should do about it. If the problem were callousness, you'd need to change people's values. But if the problem is the math of a crowd, then the solution is much more practical: in any emergency, name a specific person. "You, in the blue jacket, call an ambulance." That single act collapses the diffusion and assigns responsibility to one identifiable individual. Crisis training in hospitals and aviation now teaches exactly this technique.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How emergency-room nurses respond differently from bystanders
options.B: Why individual willingness to help decreases as group size increases
options.C: The methodology used in Darley and Latané's seizure experiment
options.D: Differences in moral values across modern cultures

questions.explanation: 讲座主题是责任扩散：群体越大，个体越不愿介入。A 与讲座无关，C 只是讲座中举例的实验细节，D 偏离了讲座关于群体规模的核心。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the participants who were sweating and pacing?

options.A: To show that the experiment caused unethical psychological harm
options.B: To compare seizure symptoms with anxiety symptoms
options.C: To establish that the non-helpers were paralyzed rather than indifferent
options.D: To suggest that the experimental design was poorly executed

questions.explanation: 讲座用这些细节证明不帮忙的人不是冷漠而是被动僵住。A 把这扩展成伦理批评，B 是对症状的字面误读，D 是讲座没有支持的判断。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the "you, in the blue jacket" instruction?

options.A: To illustrate that emergency clothing should be reflective
options.B: To repeat what Darley and Latané told their participants
options.C: To imply that aviation crews wear color-coded uniforms
options.D: To demonstrate a practical technique that overrides diffusion of responsibility

questions.explanation: 讲座用这个例子展示如何通过指名打破责任扩散。A 是对蓝夹克的字面误读，B 是讲座没有的事实错误，C 是与场景不符的过度推断。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how to reduce bystander inaction?

options.A: Practical group-size interventions can be more effective than changing values
options.B: Bystander inaction can only be reduced through years of moral education
options.C: Aviation training is the only setting where these techniques succeed
options.D: Larger groups will always respond better than smaller ones to emergencies

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示比起改变价值观，群体规模的实用干预更有效。B 与讲座相反，C 把适用范围说得太窄，D 与讲座论点直接相反。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how different cultures conceptualize time as linear vs cyclical
items.transcript: One of the more striking findings to come out of cross-cultural cognitive anthropology is that human societies do not share a single way of conceptualizing time. The framework we tend to assume in industrial societies — that time is a one-way arrow running from past to future, with each moment never returning — is sometimes called linear time. But it isn't universal. Many other cultures, especially those organized around agricultural or ritual cycles, conceptualize time as fundamentally cyclical: a series of repeating phases that loop rather than advance. Now, this distinction is not just a metaphor in people's heads. It shapes everything from grammar to economic planning to bereavement practices. Let me give you a concrete example. In several Andean Quechua-speaking communities, ethnographers have documented something unusual about how speakers gesture when discussing time. Most speakers in linear-time cultures point behind themselves to indicate the past — because the past is, in a sense, behind us. But Quechua speakers point in front, because, as they explain, the past is what they can see — it has already happened — and the future is what they cannot see, which is therefore behind them. So the spatial mapping is inverted. Now, why does this matter beyond a fascinating ethnographic detail? Because economic and policy frameworks built on linear assumptions can collide badly with cyclical-time worldviews. Development programs that emphasize a single irreversible trajectory of growth often miss why communities resist abandoning seasonal practices, because for those communities, those practices are not delays — they are how time actually moves. Recognizing the diversity of time concepts isn't just academic. It's a precondition for designing policy that doesn't impose one cognitive model on populations that organize reality through a different one.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why Andean economies have grown more slowly than industrial economies
options.B: How grammatical features predict the development of agriculture
options.C: How different cultures conceptualize time and why that matters
options.D: Why all human cultures eventually adopt a linear view of time

questions.explanation: 讲座论述不同文化对时间的不同概念化以及其影响。A 是对例子的曲解，B 与讲座无关，D 与讲座反对的普遍化立场相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Quechua speakers pointing in front to indicate the past?

options.A: To suggest that Quechua grammar is influenced by Spanish contact
options.B: To recommend that linguists revise their gesture classification system
options.C: To restate that Quechua speakers gesture differently from other groups
options.D: To illustrate that a non-linear time concept reshapes spatial reasoning

questions.explanation: 这个例子说明非线性时间观如何重塑空间推理。A 是讲座没有的猜测，B 是对内容的过度引申，C 只是把事实重复了一遍而没解释其用意。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why might development programs fail in cyclical-time communities?

options.A: They treat seasonal practices as delays rather than as time itself
options.B: They underestimate the literacy levels of agricultural workers
options.C: They depend on government subsidies that are not delivered
options.D: They overestimate the climate stability of the target regions

questions.explanation: 讲座说线性框架把季节性实践当作延迟，而对当地人来说那些就是时间本身。B、C、D 都是讲座没有提到的实际原因。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about policy design?

options.A: Policy should always defer entirely to local cosmologies without modification
options.B: Policy must take cognitive diversity into account to avoid imposing one model
options.C: Policy is generally too abstract to be informed by anthropological research
options.D: Policy frameworks based on linear time succeed once translated correctly

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示政策设计必须考虑认知多样性以免强加单一模型。A 是夸大，C 与讲座立场相反，D 与讲座对线性模型局限的批评相反。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: the cocktail party effect and selective attention
items.transcript: The cocktail party effect is one of the most studied phenomena in attention research, and it gets its name from a very ordinary experience. You're at a noisy party. There are dozens of conversations happening around you. And yet, somehow, you're able to focus on the one person you're talking to and basically tune out everyone else. That selective filtering is what cognitive psychologists call the cocktail party effect. Now, the first systematic work on this was done by Colin Cherry at Imperial College London in 1953. Cherry put participants in a setup where they wore headphones, and a different audio stream was played into each ear. He told them to repeat aloud, in real time, the message coming into one specific ear and to ignore the other. This procedure is called dichotic listening, and what Cherry wanted to know was how much of the ignored stream people actually noticed. The results were striking. Participants could repeat the attended message accurately, but they remembered almost nothing about the content of the unattended message. They didn't notice when the language switched, they didn't notice when the speaker changed gender — they essentially filtered the unattended ear out at a very deep level. But — and this is the famous twist — most participants did notice if their own name was spoken in the unattended ear. That's now called the own-name effect, and it tells us that selective attention isn't a complete shutdown. Some filter remains open, monitoring for signals that matter to us personally. So the cocktail party effect is really two findings at once: we filter aggressively, but the filter still lets through information that's biologically or socially urgent.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why social parties have grown noisier over the last century
options.B: How headphones changed the design of psychology laboratories
options.C: How human languages differ in their use of attention markers
options.D: How the brain selectively filters competing streams of speech

questions.explanation: 讲座讲大脑如何在多个语流中选择性过滤。A、B、C 都是和讲座无关或离题的方向。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Cherry's dichotic listening task require participants to do?

options.A: Repeat aloud the message played into one specific ear
options.B: Identify the gender of the speaker on each side
options.C: Recall the unattended message word for word
options.D: Switch attention between ears every thirty seconds

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说参与者需复述指定耳朵中的信息。B、D 是讲座没有提的任务，C 与实验结果相反——参与者几乎记不住非关注耳的内容。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the own-name effect?

options.A: To repeat that participants ignored the unattended message
options.B: To show that the attentional filter still admits personally important signals
options.C: To suggest that names are stored in a separate part of the brain
options.D: To argue that Cherry's experimental design was flawed

questions.explanation: 提到名字效应是为了说明过滤并非彻底关闭，仍会放过对个人重要的信号。A 只是复述前文，C 是越界推断，D 与讲座对实验的肯定相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about selective attention?

options.A: It works equally well for any signal we want to monitor
options.B: It shuts out all sensory input from the unattended channel
options.C: It permits some signals through based on personal relevance
options.D: It is largely a learned skill that disappears in noisy settings

questions.explanation: 讲座暗示选择性注意会放过与个人相关的信号。A 是过度概括，B 与讲座结论相反，D 与讲座对鸡尾酒效应的描述无关。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: So today I want to take you back to Paris, around 1870, and ask a question that probably sounds obvious now but was genuinely radical at the time. Why did the Impressionists paint outdoors? Why did Monet, Pissarro, Renoir leave the studio and set up their easels in fields and on riverbanks? The standard answer is that they wanted realism. But that's not quite right. They wanted something more specific. They wanted to capture light. And once you understand that goal, you understand why everything else in Impressionism follows from it. Now, think about what happens to color in a studio. The light is constant, often filtered through north-facing windows. Shadows are stable. A painter could spend weeks on a canvas and the conditions never changed. But outdoors, light shifts every minute. A field at ten in the morning is a completely different color from the same field at two in the afternoon. The Impressionists realized that shadows were not gray or black. They were colored. A shadow on grass might be violet or deep blue, because it picks up reflected light from the sky. This was a complete break from academic painting, which used dark earth tones to depict shadow. So when Monet painted his haystacks series, the same haystacks, over and over, at different times of day, he wasn't being repetitive. He was documenting how light changes color itself. The haystack is just a pretext. The real subject is the light.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why the Impressionists painted outdoors to capture shifting light
options.B: The biographical relationship between Monet and Pissarro
options.C: The technical methods used by academic painters in studios
options.D: The marketing strategies that made Impressionism commercially successful

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是印象派为了捕捉变化的光线而走出画室。B 与个人传记无关，C 是被对比的反面，D 完全没提到。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what surprising discovery did the Impressionists make about shadows?

options.A: Shadows were always darker than expected in outdoor scenes
options.B: Shadows were not gray or black but contained reflected colors
options.C: Shadows shifted in shape every minute due to wind movement
options.D: Shadows could only be painted accurately during midday hours

questions.explanation: 教授明确说阴影不是灰色或黑色，而是带颜色的，能反映天空的光。A 与讲座对学院派的描述相反，C 与形状无关，D 是与白天时间的混淆。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Monet's haystacks series?

options.A: To argue that Monet eventually abandoned outdoor painting
options.B: To explain why Monet preferred rural subjects to city scenes
options.C: To illustrate how the same subject documents changing light
options.D: To compare Monet's productivity with that of other Impressionists

questions.explanation: 干草堆系列被用来说明印象派如何用同一物体记录光线变化。A 与讲座立场相反，B 是表面词陷阱，D 是讲座没提的对比。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did academic painters use to depict shadow?

options.A: Bright cobalt and ultramarine blue pigments
options.B: Reflected light from neighboring color surfaces
options.C: Multiple thin layers of unmixed primary colors
options.D: Dark earth tones such as browns and blacks

questions.explanation: 教授对比印象派和学院派，说学院派用深的土色调画阴影。A 是印象派的颜色，B 是印象派的方法，C 是与混合颜料相关的混淆。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: how dual-process theory explains fast and slow thinking
items.transcript: Okay, so today I want to walk you through one of the most influential ideas in modern psychology. It's called dual-process theory, and the basic claim is that your mind has two different ways of thinking. Psychologists usually call them System 1 and System 2. So System 1 is fast. It's automatic, it doesn't feel like effort. When you see a friend's face and recognize them, that's System 1. When you drive on a familiar road and don't really remember the last five minutes of driving, that's System 1. It runs in the background, all the time, making quick judgments based on patterns and emotions. Now, System 2 is the opposite. It's slow, it's deliberate, and it actually feels like work. When you're solving a math problem, weighing the pros and cons of a big decision, or learning a new skill, you're running System 2. It uses a lot of mental energy, which is why we get tired after a long day of focused work. Here's the key point. We rely on System 1 most of the time because it's efficient. But System 1 also makes mistakes. It's prone to biases — confirmation bias, anchoring, the way first impressions stick. So good thinkers learn to recognize when a problem is hard enough that System 1 will get it wrong, and they deliberately slow down and switch to System 2. Next class, we'll look at specific experiments by Daniel Kahneman that demonstrate exactly when each system kicks in.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Daniel Kahneman's biography and main publications
options.B: The dual-process theory and its two cognitive systems
options.C: How mathematical reasoning differs from emotional reasoning
options.D: Why mental fatigue affects long-term decision-making outcomes

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是双系统理论。A 是细节人物，C 是片面，D 是单一细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what kind of thinking is System 1?

options.A: Slow and energy-intensive
options.B: Reserved only for new tasks
options.C: Fast, automatic, and effortless
options.D: Only used during sleep

questions.explanation: 教授描述系统1是快速、自动、不费力的。A 是系统2，B 颠倒了，D 完全不对。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention recognizing a friend's face?

options.A: To argue that face recognition uses System 2 thinking
options.B: To suggest that System 1 is reserved for emergency responses
options.C: To compare visual processing with mathematical reasoning
options.D: To give a concrete example of System 1 in everyday life

questions.explanation: 认脸是为了说明系统1日常运行的例子。A 反了，B 与紧急无关，C 是讲座没做的对比。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss in the next class?

options.A: Specific experiments by Daniel Kahneman showing when each system activates
options.B: Critiques of dual-process theory by neuroscientists
options.C: How children develop the two thinking systems over time
options.D: Medical conditions that impair one of the two systems

questions.explanation: 教授明说下节课讲 Kahneman 的实验。B/C/D 都是讲座没暗示的方向。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: So here's a strange fact about human memory. When you remember an experience — a vacation, a concert, a difficult meeting — your memory doesn't actually average out everything that happened. It compresses the whole event down to two specific moments: the most emotionally intense moment, and the way the experience ended. Psychologists call this the peak-end rule. Daniel Kahneman demonstrated it most famously in a study involving cold water. Participants were asked to keep one hand in painfully cold water for sixty seconds. Then, in a separate trial, the same participants kept their hand in the same cold water for sixty seconds, plus an additional thirty seconds in slightly warmer water. Logically, the second trial was worse. It had thirty extra seconds of discomfort. But when asked which trial they would rather repeat, most participants chose the longer one. Why? Because the second trial ended with the water getting warmer. The end was less painful, so the whole experience was remembered as more bearable. This finding has huge practical implications. It means that businesses can deliberately design experiences with strong endings. Restaurants give you a small free dessert at the end of the meal. Theme parks save their best fireworks for last. The middle of the experience can be flat, but the ending dominates what you remember. The peak matters too — one moment of intense joy, or intense frustration, will define how you recall the whole thing.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How memory chooses to encode entire experiences neutrally
options.B: Why customers prefer shorter restaurant visits to longer ones
options.C: How memory compresses experiences into peak and end moments
options.D: The biological basis of pain tolerance in cold-water experiments

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是峰终定律。A 与讲座立场相反，B 是从例子误推的细节，D 是细节支撑。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did most participants prefer the longer cold-water trial?

options.A: Because the longer trial used colder water overall
options.B: Because they wanted to demonstrate higher pain tolerance
options.C: Because the second trial was administered later in the experiment
options.D: Because the ending of the longer trial was less painful

questions.explanation: 更长的一次以更温暖的水结束。A 与事实相反，B/C 都是讲座没提的猜测。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what two moments dominate how an experience is remembered?

options.A: The most emotionally intense moment and the ending
options.B: The first impression and the average of all moments
options.C: The longest stretch of comfort and the longest stretch of discomfort
options.D: The starting expectation and the final cost

questions.explanation: 教授明确说是最强烈的瞬间和结尾。其他都是错误描述。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention restaurants giving small free desserts?

options.A: To criticize how businesses exploit psychological research
options.B: To illustrate how businesses engineer positive endings deliberately
options.C: To explain why dessert is the most enjoyable part of a meal
options.D: To compare restaurant pricing strategies with theme parks

questions.explanation: 免费甜点是说明企业有意设计积极结尾的例子。A 是讲座没有的立场，C/D 是分散注意的解读。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: why objects in motion resist changes in direction (inertia)
items.transcript: Today I want to talk about one of the most counterintuitive ideas in physics — inertia. The basic claim is this. An object in motion will continue in a straight line at constant speed unless something acts on it. This is Newton's first law, but it took centuries for people to accept because it contradicts what we see every day. After all, if you slide a book across a table, it stops. It doesn't continue forever. So how can Newton be right? The answer is friction. The book stops because the table is pushing back on it through friction. Take away the friction, and the book really would slide forever. We've actually proven this in experiments. Astronauts inside the International Space Station can give a tool a small push, and it will drift across the cabin at the same speed for as long as nobody touches it. There's no gravity holding it to a surface, almost no air resistance — so inertia takes over. Now here's what's interesting. Inertia is also what makes turning a car difficult at high speed. Your body, traveling at sixty kilometers an hour, wants to keep going in a straight line. When the car turns, the seatbelt and the seat have to physically redirect you, and you feel that as a sideways force. The faster the car, the harder the redirection. This is why race-car drivers train their necks specifically — at extreme speeds, just turning your head against your own inertia takes real strength.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Newton's biographical influence on physics education
options.B: How astronauts conduct experiments on the Space Station
options.C: The dangers of high-speed automobile driving
options.D: The principle of inertia and why it counters everyday observation

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是惯性原理与日常直觉相反。A/B/C 都是支撑例子或细节。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the book sliding across a table?

options.A: To set up the apparent contradiction that friction resolves
options.B: To suggest that Newton's first law has exceptions in everyday life
options.C: To compare different types of dining-table surfaces
options.D: To recommend a specific demonstration for the next lab

questions.explanation: 书本停止是一个看似与牛顿第一定律矛盾的现象，教授随后用摩擦力解释。B 是错误结论，C/D 与讲座无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what would happen to a book on a frictionless table?

options.A: It would speed up due to gravity
options.B: It would continue sliding at constant speed
options.C: It would eventually rise off the surface
options.D: It would rotate in place

questions.explanation: 没有摩擦，书本会沿直线匀速继续滑动。其他都是错误。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why do race-car drivers specifically train their necks?

options.A: To improve their reaction time during emergency braking
options.B: To reduce the risk of damaging hearing in loud environments
options.C: To overcome their own inertia when turning their head at speed
options.D: To stay alert during long endurance races

questions.explanation: 高速下转头要对抗自身的惯性。其他都是错误细节。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript:
The peak-end rule is one of the most striking findings in the psychology of memory. It comes from work by Daniel Kahneman and Barbara Fredrickson in the 1990s, and it answers a simple-sounding question: when we look back on an experience, what actually shapes how we remember it? It turns out we don't average everything that happened. Instead, we weight two specific moments very heavily — the emotional peak, whether that's the most intense pleasure or the worst pain, and the very end. Everything in between gets compressed, summarized, or dropped.

Kahneman's most famous demonstration involved a clinical study of patients undergoing colonoscopies. This is an uncomfortable, sometimes painful procedure, and patients rated their discomfort in real time. Half of them received a standard procedure. The other half received a slightly longer one, in which the doctor deliberately kept the scope in place for a few extra minutes at a lower, less painful intensity at the very end. Objectively, the second group endured MORE total discomfort. But when patients were asked weeks later to remember the experience, and to decide whether they would return for a follow-up, the group with the longer, gentler ending remembered the procedure as significantly less bad — and they were more willing to come back.

So the implication is striking: a longer experience that ends well can leave a better memory than a shorter experience that ends abruptly at its peak. This matters far beyond medicine. Service designers, teachers, even people planning a vacation all face the same memory bias. We don't really remember our experience; we remember our story of our experience.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: A memory bias that gives extra weight to peaks and endings of experiences
options.B: The history of clinical psychology research on pain management
options.C: The career of Daniel Kahneman as a cognitive scientist
options.D: How medical colonoscopies have changed since the 1990s

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是 peak-end rule（峰终定律），即记忆主要由情绪峰值和结尾决定。A 概括了这一中心。B 只是案例之一，太窄；C 偏离主题；D 是表面词陷阱，把 colonoscopy 这个例子误当成主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the second group of patients receive?

options.A: A shorter procedure that ended at the most painful moment
options.B: A longer procedure that ended at a lower pain intensity
options.C: A standard procedure with no modifications to its timing
options.D: A pain medication administered during the final minutes

questions.explanation: 教授明确说第二组的过程更长，但结尾痛感更轻。B 正确。A 把方向反过来；C 是第一组的描述；D 引入未提及的药物。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention service design and vacation planning?

options.A: To compare their methods to clinical pain research
options.B: To argue that all industries should adopt Kahneman's framework
options.C: To show that the peak-end rule applies far beyond medical contexts
options.D: To explain why service designers study cognitive psychology

questions.explanation: 提到服务设计和度假是为了说明 peak-end rule 应用范围广，不限于医疗。C 正确。A 把目的弄错；B 过度推论；D 仅复述字面而非解释为什么。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how people remember experiences?

options.A: People always remember the entire experience accurately
options.B: People recall every minute of an experience equally well
options.C: People remember experiences best when they are documented in writing
options.D: People remember the story of an experience rather than the experience itself

questions.explanation: 讲座最后一句直接暗示我们记的是 "故事" 而非真实体验。D 正确。A、B 都把方向反过来；C 引入了未涉及的书面记录主题。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how folk melodies were absorbed into nationalist compositions
items.transcript:
Imagine you are listening to a symphony from late nineteenth-century Bohemia, what is now the Czech Republic. The harmonies are sophisticated, the orchestration is rich, and the form is classical. But underneath, there is a tune you can almost picture being sung at a village wedding. That blend is at the heart of what historians call musical nationalism, a movement that swept across Europe roughly between 1850 and 1910.

Nationalist composers were responding to a political environment in which many regions — Bohemia, Norway, Russia, Finland — were seeking a cultural identity distinct from the dominant Austro-German tradition. So they reached for folk material. Bedřich Smetana, in his cycle Má vlast, weaves Czech dance rhythms and rural melodies into orchestral tone poems. Edvard Grieg in Norway collected songs from local fiddlers and tucked their modal scales into his concert works. Mily Balakirev led a circle in Saint Petersburg that did the same with Russian peasant tunes.

Now, this borrowing was almost never a literal quotation. The melody might be reharmonized in unexpected ways, fragmented, or set against a counterpoint that no village singer would have used. The point was not authenticity in a folkloric sense. The point was to make a familiar, regionally specific sound carry the prestige of European concert tradition. And it worked: a Bohemian audience hearing Smetana could feel that their own language and landscape had earned a seat in the international canon.

This is what makes nationalist music interesting as cultural history. It is not pure folk, and it is not pure imported classical. It is a deliberate hybrid built to make a political claim audible.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The careers of three composers from late nineteenth-century Europe
options.B: How nationalist composers blended folk material with classical forms
options.C: The political conflicts between Bohemia and Austria-Germany
options.D: How to identify folk melodies hidden inside symphonic works

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是民族主义作曲家把民歌素材融入古典形式。B 概括最准确。A 只是举例，太窄；C 偏向政治冲突，本讲座的政治背景只是动机；D 是表面词陷阱，把识别民歌作为主题。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe the harmonies of a Bohemian symphony?

options.A: To argue that Smetana invented a new system of harmony
options.B: To compare nineteenth-century music to medieval music
options.C: To set up the contrast between sophisticated form and underlying folk material
options.D: To restate that Bohemian music sounds rich and orchestral

questions.explanation: 开场描述精致的和声是为了引出 "表面古典 / 底下民歌" 的对比。C 正确。A 过度推论；B 误判目的；D 只是复述描述本身。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Grieg do with songs collected from local fiddlers?

options.A: He published them in their original form for choirs
options.B: He sold the recordings to Russian music publishers
options.C: He used them to teach village children in Norway
options.D: He incorporated their modal scales into his concert works

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说 Grieg 把民间的 modal scales 放进音乐会作品。D 正确。A 错描他保留原样；B、C 都是讲座未提及的内容。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did composers reharmonize folk melodies?

options.A: To make a regional sound carry the prestige of concert tradition
options.B: To document folk songs accurately for later musicologists
options.C: To preserve the original village singing style intact
options.D: To imitate the Austro-German classical canon directly

questions.explanation: 讲座原话: the point was to make a regionally specific sound carry the prestige of European concert tradition。A 直接对应。B、C 都把方向反过来（讲座说他们不追求 folkloric authenticity）；D 错，他们恰恰是想区别于德奥传统。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: the concept of carbon sequestration in forests and soils
items.transcript:
Carbon sequestration is the natural process by which carbon dioxide is captured from the atmosphere and locked away in long-lived storage. It is a key part of how Earth's climate system has stayed stable over geological time, and it is central to current discussions about climate change. So let me walk you through the two big places where this happens in terrestrial ecosystems: forests and soils.

Start with forests. When trees grow, they pull carbon dioxide out of the air and use the carbon to build wood, leaves, and roots. A mature forest can store enormous amounts of carbon — sometimes hundreds of tons per hectare. The catch is that this storage is conditional. If the forest burns or is cut down, much of that carbon goes right back into the atmosphere. That is why protecting old-growth forests does much more for the climate, ton for ton, than planting new seedlings: the old trees are already storing the carbon.

Soils are the less visible but actually larger reservoir. Globally, soils hold about three times as much carbon as the atmosphere. When plants die and decay, much of their carbon enters the soil as organic matter, where microbes and minerals can stabilize it for centuries or even millennia. Practices that disturb soil — heavy tilling, draining wetlands, urban development — can release that carbon quickly. Conversely, restoring grasslands and wetlands can rebuild it.

So when policymakers talk about nature-based climate solutions, they really mean managing these two systems. The atmosphere does not distinguish between a ton of carbon stored in a tree and a ton stored in a meadow soil. Both count.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of climate-change discussions in environmental policy
options.B: The growth biology of mature old-growth forests
options.C: How forests and soils naturally capture and store carbon
options.D: The role of microbes in decomposing dead plant material

questions.explanation: 讲座核心介绍 carbon sequestration 的两大系统：森林与土壤。C 概括正确。A 偏离主题；B、D 都只是局部细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how much carbon do soils store globally compared with the atmosphere?

options.A: Soils store about the same amount of carbon as the atmosphere
options.B: Soils store about half as much carbon as the atmosphere
options.C: Soils store twice as much carbon as the atmosphere
options.D: Soils store about three times as much carbon as the atmosphere

questions.explanation: 讲座原话: soils hold about three times as much carbon as the atmosphere。D 正确。其余选项都改了具体倍数。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare protecting old-growth forests with planting new seedlings?

options.A: To show that protecting existing forests stores more carbon than planting new trees
options.B: To recommend that governments plant more seedlings worldwide
options.C: To explain how foresters certify carbon credits in carbon markets
options.D: To restate that trees pull carbon out of the atmosphere

questions.explanation: 教授的目的是强调保护老林子比新种树存碳更有效。A 正确。B 过度推论；C 谈了讲座没提到的市场；D 只是复述前面的字面。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about draining wetlands?

options.A: It permanently improves the carbon storage of nearby grasslands
options.B: It can quickly release carbon that was stored in the soil
options.C: It has no measurable effect on atmospheric carbon levels
options.D: It is the most efficient strategy for managing urban land

questions.explanation: 讲座将抽干湿地列入 "扰动土壤迅速释放碳" 的例子。B 是直接可推。A、C 把方向反过来；D 是无关推论。

items.subject: music
items.topic: the role of improvisation in early jazz
items.transcript:
Early jazz, the music that developed in New Orleans in the first decades of the twentieth century, sounds a lot more spontaneous than the European concert music it grew up alongside. And there is a reason for that. Improvisation was not a flourish on top of the music — it was central to how the music actually worked.

Take a classic New Orleans ensemble — cornet, clarinet, and trombone, with a rhythm section behind them. When the band played a piece like Tiger Rag or Basin Street Blues, there was a melody everyone knew, sometimes called the head. But after the first chorus, the cornet would take the lead line and start altering it on the spot, the clarinet would weave a countermelody around it, and the trombone would fill in supporting figures from below. Each musician was inventing in real time, but always in relation to what the others were doing. The result was a kind of group conversation, with the underlying chord structure acting as the ground rules.

Now, this matters historically because it sets early jazz apart from the popular tunes of its day. A ragtime piece, for example, was usually played exactly as printed. Improvisation, by contrast, treated the printed score as a starting point, not the destination. That gave each performance a one-time character: the same band could play St. Louis Blues on Monday and again on Friday and produce two very different recordings.

Later jazz styles — swing, bebop, free jazz — built on this foundation in different directions. But the core idea that the musicians compose during the performance, working within and against an agreed structure, traces directly back to those early New Orleans bands.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The careers of three early New Orleans cornet players
options.B: The historical influence of ragtime on later jazz styles
options.C: How European concert music differed from American popular tunes
options.D: The central role of improvisation in early New Orleans jazz

questions.explanation: 讲座反复强调即兴 (improvisation) 是早期爵士的核心。D 正确。A 太窄；B 是反向，讲座是用 ragtime 对比衬托而非讲它的影响；C 偏离主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was the role of the cornet after the first chorus?

options.A: The cornet led by altering the melody on the spot
options.B: The cornet stopped playing and rested for a chorus
options.C: The cornet repeated the printed score exactly as written
options.D: The cornet copied the clarinet's countermelody note for note

questions.explanation: 讲座原话: after the first chorus, the cornet would take the lead line and start altering it on the spot。A 直接对应。其余均与讲座事实相反或换错对象。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention ragtime?

options.A: To argue that ragtime should now be classified as jazz
options.B: To contrast a music played exactly as printed with improvised jazz
options.C: To suggest that ragtime composers learned directly from jazz bands
options.D: To restate that ragtime was a popular form in the same era

questions.explanation: 提到 ragtime 是为了对照 "按谱照弹" 和 "即兴"。B 正确。A 过度推论；C 把因果搞反；D 只是复述细节。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what gave each early jazz performance a one-time character?

options.A: The use of expensive recording equipment in New Orleans clubs
options.B: The strict adherence to the printed score during the head section
options.C: The use of the score as a starting point rather than a destination
options.D: The decision to omit the rhythm section from some choruses

questions.explanation: 讲座原话: improvisation treated the printed score as a starting point, not the destination。C 正确。A、D 完全未提；B 与讲座事实相反。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how tardigrades survive extreme environments
items.transcript: Let me tell you about one of the strangest organisms on Earth, the tardigrade. These tiny eight-legged creatures, sometimes called water bears, are only about half a millimeter long, but they have survived conditions that would destroy most life. Tardigrades have been frozen, boiled, dehydrated, and even exposed to the vacuum of space, and many came back to active life. How do they do it? When their environment becomes too harsh, tardigrades enter a state called cryptobiosis. They lose almost all of the water in their bodies and shrink down into a compact form called a tun. In this state, their metabolism essentially stops. To protect their cells, they produce special sugars and proteins. One group of proteins, recently identified by biologists in Tokyo, replaces water in the cell, keeping the delicate structures intact even when nearly all moisture is gone. When water returns, the tardigrade rehydrates within minutes and resumes normal activity. Now, this matters beyond just being a curiosity. Researchers are studying these proteins to find new ways of preserving medicines, blood products, and even human cells without refrigeration. So in a sense, this tiny animal could change how hospitals store treatments in remote areas. It is a remarkable example of how solutions in biology can come from the most unexpected places.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How tardigrades survive extreme conditions and what their biology might offer human medicine
options.B: How biologists in Tokyo first identified the tardigrade species
options.C: Why tardigrades evolved their unusual eight-legged body plan
options.D: How astronauts use tardigrades to study weightless environments

questions.explanation: 讲座先介绍水熊虫在极端条件下的生存机制，再说明这些机制对医学保存的潜在意义。其他选项要么聚焦次要细节（Tokyo实验室、太空），要么不在讲座中讨论（八条腿的进化）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens when a tardigrade enters cryptobiosis?

options.A: It increases its body size to retain heat
options.B: It loses almost all body water and forms a compact tun
options.C: It enters a deeper layer of soil to find moisture
options.D: It releases eggs that can survive in space conditions

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说水熊虫几乎排出所有水分并形成紧密的tun状态。A正好相反；C讲座未提；D混淆了水熊虫和孢子的生存方式。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the proteins identified by biologists in Tokyo?

options.A: To show that Japanese laboratories lead the field in microbiology
options.B: To suggest that tardigrades evolved in tropical climates
options.C: To illustrate a specific molecular mechanism that explains the survival ability
options.D: To repeat that tardigrades survive in dehydrated conditions

questions.explanation: Tokyo蛋白质的提及是用来具体说明替代水分子的分子机制，回答"how do they do it"。A夸大、B无依据、D只是字面重复细节而非解释功能。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how could tardigrade research potentially benefit medicine?

options.A: By creating new vaccines specifically targeting tardigrade species
options.B: By enabling longer freezing storage of organs at very low temperatures
options.C: By replacing all current laboratory refrigeration equipment globally
options.D: By preserving medicines and cells without refrigeration

questions.explanation: 讲座说研究者希望用这些蛋白质在没有冷藏条件下保存药物、血液和细胞。A未提；B反转了机制（不是更深的冷冻）；C过度概括。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how different cultures conceptualize time as linear vs cyclical
items.transcript: Today I want to discuss something quite fundamental about culture: the way different societies think about time itself. In the Western tradition that most of you grew up in, time is generally treated as a line. There's a past, a present, and a future, arranged in a strict sequence, and we tend to think of progress as movement along that line. But that view is not universal. Many societies, both ancient and contemporary, have understood time as cyclical. In the Maya civilization of Mesoamerica, for example, calendars combined multiple repeating cycles. Events were not unique points on a line, but expected to recur as patterns moved through their cycles again. A similar view appears in classical Indian thought, where ages of the world rise and fall and then begin again. Now, why does this matter to us as anthropologists? Because how a culture conceptualizes time shapes how it makes decisions. A linear society plans for new outcomes, invests in growth, and tries to leave a record of unprecedented achievements. A cyclical society, by contrast, looks to past patterns as guides for the present, and treats long-term continuity, rather than change, as a sign of success. Neither model is more correct than the other. Each is an internally consistent way of organizing experience. And recognizing this is important, because researchers studying these cultures sometimes assume Western time as a default, which can distort their interpretation of ritual, history, and decision-making.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the Maya calendar was constructed from astronomical observations
options.B: How linear and cyclical views of time shape cultural decisions and interpretation
options.C: How Western anthropology has misrepresented ancient Indian texts
options.D: How accurate timekeeping developed across multiple early civilizations

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是对比两种时间观及其对文化决策与人类学解读的影响。A、C太局限；D偏离主题（不是讨论计时精度）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what characterizes cyclical conceptions of time?

options.A: Events are seen as unique points along a strict line
options.B: Time stops periodically at fixed intervals of one century
options.C: Events are expected to recur as patterns move through cycles
options.D: The future is treated as fundamentally unknowable to mortals

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说在循环时间观中事件不是线上的独特点，而是会随着循环重复出现。A正好相反；B虚构的细节；D未提及。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention classical Indian thought?

options.A: To argue that Indian philosophy influenced Maya calendars
options.B: To debate which civilization first developed cyclical time
options.C: To imply that all ancient cultures shared identical worldviews
options.D: To provide a second example of cyclical time outside Mesoamerica

questions.explanation: 印度思想是用作循环时间观的第二个例子，证明这种观点跨文化存在。A、B无依据；C过度推断。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what risk does Western default time pose for researchers?

options.A: It can distort the interpretation of ritual, history, and decision-making
options.B: It accelerates the loss of indigenous languages over generations
options.C: It causes anthropologists to overestimate population sizes
options.D: It prevents collaboration between Western and non-Western scholars

questions.explanation: 讲座结尾明确说默认西方时间观会扭曲对仪式、历史和决策的解读。其他选项都没有讲座支持。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: the influence of climate on traditional vernacular housing
items.transcript: I want to spend today talking about vernacular architecture, which means the traditional, often anonymous, building styles that develop in a particular region over generations. Now, vernacular buildings are interesting precisely because they evolve as practical solutions to local conditions, and one of the most powerful forces shaping them is climate. Think about traditional houses in hot, dry regions, like parts of southwestern North Africa or the American Southwest. Builders there typically use thick walls made of adobe or stone. These walls absorb heat slowly during the day and release it slowly at night, creating a stable, cool interior even when the outside temperature swings dramatically between morning and afternoon. The flat or low-pitched roofs make sense too, because rainfall is scarce. Now compare that with the steep-roofed wooden houses of northern Scandinavia or alpine Europe. Here heavy snowfall is a yearly fact of life, and a steep pitch lets snow slide off rather than accumulate and collapse the structure. Lightweight wooden walls with insulation work well because the temperature differential is consistent throughout the year, not the dramatic daily swing of the desert. What I want you to take from this, beyond the specific examples, is that these are not just aesthetic choices. The forms emerge from generations of practical experiments. Modern architects today are increasingly studying these traditional buildings because they offer time-tested ideas for sustainable design in a warming climate.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How modern architects have revived adobe construction techniques
options.B: Why steep roofs became dominant in alpine European villages
options.C: How climate has shaped traditional building forms in different regions
options.D: How vernacular architecture differs from formal architectural training

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是气候塑造各地传统建筑形式，并举两个相反气候的例子。A、B太局限；D虽相关但不是讲座焦点。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do thick adobe walls help in hot, dry regions?

options.A: They reflect direct sunlight before it reaches the interior
options.B: They trap rainwater for later use during dry months
options.C: They allow rapid air circulation during midday hours
options.D: They absorb and release heat slowly to keep interiors stable

questions.explanation: 讲座说厚墙吸收和释放热量的速度都很慢，因而内部温度稳定。A、B、C都没有讲座支持。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the steep-roofed wooden houses of Scandinavia?

options.A: To provide a contrasting example of climate-driven design
options.B: To argue that wooden houses are environmentally superior
options.C: To explain that snow loads are universal in temperate regions
options.D: To restate that vernacular buildings vary across cultures

questions.explanation: 斯堪的纳维亚的例子用来与沙漠建筑形成对比，展示完全不同气候下的设计逻辑。B、C无依据，D只是字面重复。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why are modern architects studying vernacular buildings?

options.A: They offer time-tested ideas for sustainable design in a warming climate
options.B: They protect government heritage status from being revoked
options.C: They prove all modern construction methods to be ineffective
options.D: They require fewer permits than contemporary buildings

questions.explanation: 讲座末尾说现代建筑师研究这些传统建筑是因为它们为气候变暖背景下的可持续设计提供经过时间检验的思路。其他选项都没有讲座支持。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the role of patronage in shaping Baroque art
items.transcript: Let's turn now to the Baroque period in seventeenth-century Europe. When we look at Baroque painting and sculpture today in museums, we tend to focus on the artist, but to understand the art, we really need to understand who paid for it. The Baroque is one of the clearest examples in art history of how patronage shapes style. The two main patrons of the period were the Catholic Church, especially in Rome, and absolute monarchs across Europe. After the Protestant Reformation, the Catholic Church wanted art that could inspire strong emotional response, draw worshippers in, and clearly communicate religious narratives. So Baroque religious paintings often have dramatic lighting, dynamic poses, and figures that seem to break out of the picture frame, as if the saints were leaning into the viewer's own space. Royal patrons wanted something a little different. They commissioned works that emphasized power, wealth, and ceremony. Think of the enormous palace decorations at Versailles in France, or grand portraits showing kings in armor and ermine. These were political tools as much as art. Now, what I want you to take away is this: the formal characteristics we identify as Baroque, the drama, the scale, the rich materials, didn't emerge in a vacuum. They were responses to what patrons wanted, and to what those patrons wanted the art to do in the world. Next time we'll look at how this differs from Dutch Baroque, where the patrons were merchants instead of kings.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why Dutch painters preferred small-scale domestic scenes
options.B: How religious painters trained apprentices in seventeenth-century studios
options.C: How techniques for grinding pigments improved during the Baroque period
options.D: How patronage by churches and monarchs shaped Baroque art

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是赞助人（教会和君主）如何塑造巴洛克艺术风格。A是预告下次讲座；B、C未涉及。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Catholic Church want Baroque art to do?

options.A: Inspire emotional response and communicate religious narratives
options.B: Document Protestant Reformation history accurately for archives
options.C: Train new artists in techniques imported from East Asia
options.D: Replace medieval altarpieces in rural village churches

questions.explanation: 讲座说教会希望艺术能激发强烈情感反应并清晰地传达宗教故事。B、C、D都没有讲座依据。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the palace decorations at Versailles?

options.A: To suggest that French palaces were larger than Italian ones
options.B: To illustrate how royal patrons used art to emphasize power
options.C: To compare French Baroque with the Renaissance in Italy
options.D: To repeat that Baroque art was made for monarchs

questions.explanation: 凡尔赛宫装饰是用来举例说明君主如何用艺术彰显权力和财富。A、C无依据；D只是字面重复。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: How Italian Baroque influenced eighteenth-century Russia
options.B: How religious patronage continued into nineteenth-century France
options.C: How Dutch Baroque differs from court-patronage Baroque
options.D: How museum curators identify forgeries of Baroque art today

questions.explanation: 讲师明确预告下次会讨论荷兰巴洛克——其赞助者是商人而非国王——与本课内容的对比。其他选项偏离预告。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how geologists use ice cores to reconstruct ancient climates
items.transcript: Ice cores are cylinders of ice drilled from glaciers and polar ice sheets, and they're one of the most powerful tools geologists have for reconstructing ancient climates. Each year, snow accumulates and gets buried, slowly compacting into ice. So if you drill straight down, you're essentially traveling backward through time, layer by layer. The deepest cores recovered from Antarctica reach about 800,000 years into the past. Now, what makes these cores so valuable isn't just the ice itself. It's the tiny air bubbles trapped inside. When snow compresses into ice, it traps small pockets of atmosphere from the year it fell. So when we extract a bubble from a layer dating to, say, 120,000 years ago, we get a direct sample of the air that surrounded those ancient snowflakes. By measuring carbon dioxide and methane concentrations in those bubbles, we can chart greenhouse gas levels through multiple ice age cycles. Geologists also analyze the chemistry of the ice itself. The ratio between two oxygen isotopes — oxygen-16 and oxygen-18 — shifts with temperature. Lighter isotopes evaporate more easily in cold conditions, so the ratio acts as a thermometer. Combine that with the gas bubbles, and you have temperature and atmosphere data, year by year, across hundreds of thousands of years. This work is what allowed scientists to demonstrate that today's atmospheric carbon dioxide levels are higher than at any point in the entire ice-core record. It transformed climate science from a debate about general trends into a quantitative, time-resolved field. Future drilling projects in East Antarctica aim to push the ice-core record back to about 1.5 million years.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How geologists use ice cores to reconstruct past climates
options.B: How greenhouse gases have changed over the past century
options.C: How polar glaciers form and move across the ocean floor
options.D: How modern thermometers measure global temperatures

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心内容是介绍冰芯如何被用来重建古代气候，从气泡的成分到氧同位素分析，都是为这个核心方法服务。B选项太窄，只覆盖一个细节；C选项偏离主题，没讨论冰川形成；D选项与冰芯无关。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the tiny air bubbles trapped inside the ice?

options.A: To compare modern atmospheres with prehistoric ones
options.B: To illustrate why ice cores can preserve samples of ancient atmosphere
options.C: To explain why glaciers melt during warm interglacial periods
options.D: To question whether ice-core data are truly reliable

questions.explanation: 教授提到气泡的目的，是说明它们能保留古代大气样本，使我们能直接分析当时的空气成分。A是过度推论，没有强调比较；C偏离主题，与气泡无关；D违背教授肯定立场的逻辑。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the ratio between oxygen-16 and oxygen-18 indicate?

options.A: The age of the ice in years before present
options.B: The concentration of methane in trapped bubbles
options.C: The temperature at the time the snow originally fell
options.D: The thickness of each annual snow layer recovered

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出氧同位素比值随温度变化，相当于一支温度计。A混淆深度与时间；B把它和甲烷混为一谈；D则把厚度搞错。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about current research priorities in this field?

options.A: Ice-core analysis has been abandoned in favor of newer methods.
options.B: Most existing cores already reach the entire useful ice record.
options.C: Greenhouse gas trends have stopped changing in recent decades.
options.D: Researchers are working to extend the ice-core record further into the past.

questions.explanation: 教授最后提到南极东部正计划取得回溯到约150万年前的冰芯，暗示当前的方向是延长记录。A与教授的肯定基调矛盾；B忽略未来计划；C不符合最新数据信息。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: how Expressionism represented emotional experience over physical reality
items.transcript: Expressionism is an influential modernist art movement that emerged in northern Europe during the early twentieth century. It's important because it deliberately rejected the goal of representing physical reality. Instead, Expressionist painters aimed to convey emotional experience — sometimes anxiety, sometimes alienation, sometimes joy — through deliberate distortion of form and color. Now, to understand what's happening here, consider the contrast with Impressionism. Impressionist painters wanted to capture how light actually appears at a fleeting moment, often outdoors. Expressionists asked a different question: how does this scene feel to me as I experience it? The most famous example is Edvard Munch's painting The Scream, completed in 1893. The figure on the bridge is barely human; the face is stretched, the body twisted, and the sky behind him is a swirling sea of orange and red. None of that depicts what Munch literally saw. He's painting an internal sensation of dread that surged through him on an evening walk, an emotion he later described in his journal. Other Expressionist artists, such as those in the German group Die Brücke, used violently bright colors and sharp angular brushwork to translate urban anxiety onto canvas. Architecture and crowds appeared jagged because city life felt jagged. This deliberate distortion influenced almost every later twentieth-century movement, from Abstract Expressionism in postwar New York to contemporary video art. The lesson is that Expressionism gave painters permission to treat the canvas as a record of feeling rather than a record of sight. So the next time you stand in front of an Expressionist work and think, that doesn't look right, remember — it isn't supposed to.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Impressionist painters depicted light outdoors
options.B: How Expressionists prioritized emotion over realistic depiction
options.C: Why Edvard Munch became the most famous painter in Europe
options.D: How architects of the early twentieth century used color

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是说明表现主义如何把情感置于真实再现之上。A是教授提到的对照背景，不是主题；C只是举例，过窄；D则与建筑无关，偏题。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Impressionism?

options.A: To suggest that it is less important than Expressionism
options.B: To question whether Impressionism truly captures reality
options.C: To contrast its goals with those of the Expressionists
options.D: To trace the historical origins of modern photography

questions.explanation: 教授提到印象派的目的，是与表现主义形成对比，强调两者关注的问题不同。A加入价值判断；B不符合教授中性介绍的语气；D偏题，没讨论摄影。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does Munch's The Scream depict?

options.A: A realistic portrait of a friend Munch saw on a bridge
options.B: A precise depiction of a sunset over the Norwegian fjord
options.C: A theoretical study of facial muscles under expression
options.D: An internal sensation of dread that Munch experienced

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出这幅画呈现的是Munch内心的恐惧感，而不是真实景象。A、B把画当成现实主义；C则与画作主题完全不符。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about later twentieth-century art movements?

options.A: They were significantly shaped by Expressionism's emphasis on feeling.
options.B: They abandoned distortion in favor of strict realism.
options.C: They rejected the use of bright colors in painting.
options.D: They were limited mostly to architecture and design.

questions.explanation: 教授提到表现主义影响了战后抽象表现主义和当代视频艺术，暗示后续运动深受其情感优先观念影响。B、C逆转其立场；D局限错误。

items.subject: environmental_science
items.topic: how green roofs reduce urban stormwater runoff
items.transcript: Today we're going to talk about a relatively simple but powerful idea in urban environmental design — the green roof. A green roof is exactly what it sounds like. It's a rooftop that's been planted with vegetation, often grasses, sedums, or even small shrubs, growing in a thin layer of soil over a waterproof membrane. They've been used in northern Europe for centuries, but they've become increasingly common in dense cities like Chicago, Toronto, and Singapore. One of their most important functions is managing stormwater. So let's talk about why that matters. When rain falls on a conventional roof — concrete, asphalt, or metal — the water simply runs off, gets funneled into drains, and floods the city's sewer system within minutes. During heavy storms, that surge often overwhelms treatment plants, and untreated water spills into rivers. A green roof works differently. The soil and plants absorb a substantial fraction of the rainfall, depending on how much water was already in the soil. Some of that water evaporates back into the atmosphere through the leaves. The rest is released slowly into the drainage system over hours, instead of minutes. Studies in Toronto have shown that an extensive green roof can retain about sixty percent of annual rainfall. Beyond stormwater, the same systems also cool the building below, reduce air pollution, and provide habitat for pollinators. The trade-off, of course, is cost and weight. Buildings have to be engineered to support the wet soil, and installation runs significantly more than a standard roof. Still, as cities face heavier storms under climate change, green roofs are becoming an increasingly attractive option for urban planners.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The pollinators that benefit from rooftop gardens
options.B: The history of vegetation use in northern Europe
options.C: How green roofs help cities manage stormwater runoff
options.D: How sewer systems are designed in dense metropolises

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心是说明屋顶绿化如何缓解城市雨水径流问题。A、B只触及次要话题；D偏题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to rain that falls on a conventional roof?

options.A: It evaporates from the surface within hours.
options.B: It is mostly absorbed by the roof material.
options.C: It is stored in tanks for later treatment.
options.D: It runs off quickly and floods the sewer system.

questions.explanation: 教授指出常规屋顶让雨水迅速流走，导致下水道在几分钟内被淹没。A、B、C都与教授所说的情况相反或不符。

questions.stem: According to the Toronto studies cited, what percentage of annual rainfall can a green roof retain?

options.A: About 60 percent
options.B: About 25 percent
options.C: About 90 percent
options.D: About 10 percent

questions.explanation: 教授提到多伦多研究显示绿屋能截留约60%的年降雨。其他选项与研究数据不符。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the future of green roofs?

options.A: Cities are likely to abandon them due to high installation costs.
options.B: Their popularity will likely grow as cities face heavier storms.
options.C: They will soon be required by international environmental law.
options.D: They are mainly suitable for rural rather than urban settings.

questions.explanation: 教授提到在气候变化下绿屋成为更具吸引力的选择，暗示其普及度将上升。A、C、D不符合教授的总体立场。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how certain plants use mimicry to attract pollinators
items.transcript: Today's topic is one of the most striking examples of evolution in plants — the use of mimicry to attract pollinators. Mimicry, in this context, means that a plant has evolved to look or smell like something else, in order to trick a specific pollinator into visiting it. The pollinator gets nothing in return, but the plant gets its pollen transferred. Now, the textbook example is the bee orchid, which grows across parts of Europe and the Mediterranean. The flower has evolved a structure that closely resembles the body of a female bee. It even produces chemical compounds that mimic the female bee's sex pheromones. When a male bee detects these cues, he attempts to mate with the flower. As he struggles, the orchid's pollen sticks to his body. When he eventually moves on and tries to mate with another orchid, he carries the pollen with him, completing the transfer. Notice the trade-off here: the male bee invests time and energy and gets no reward at all. From an evolutionary perspective, you might expect natural selection to favor bees that learn to avoid these orchids. And to some extent, that does happen — experienced bees often refuse the flowers. But because young or naive bees keep falling for the trick, the orchid still gets enough successful pollinations to persist. Other plants use related strategies. Some carrion-mimicking flowers smell like rotting meat to attract flies that lay eggs in carcasses. So mimicry, in plants, isn't a single trick. It's a whole family of evolutionary strategies that exploit the perceptual systems of insects, and it tells us something deep about how natural selection shapes communication between species.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How plants regulate water loss through their flowers
options.B: How bees learn to identify reliable food sources
options.C: How orchids evolved to produce more nectar than other flowers
options.D: How some plants use mimicry to deceive their pollinators

questions.explanation: 讲座聚焦于植物如何通过模拟欺骗传粉者。A、B、C都偏离了核心主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the bee orchid produce to attract a male bee?

options.A: Chemical compounds that mimic the female bee's sex pheromones
options.B: A sweet sugary nectar that contains additional sugars
options.C: Brightly colored pigments visible only at night
options.D: Ultrasonic vibrations within the petal structure

questions.explanation: 教授指出兰花产生模拟雌蜂性信息素的化学物质来吸引雄蜂。其他选项都不符合讲座内容。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens after the male bee tries to mate with the orchid?

options.A: The bee receives a sugary reward from the orchid.
options.B: The bee carries pollen from the flower to other orchids.
options.C: The bee dies shortly after the encounter.
options.D: The bee learns to avoid all orchids permanently.

questions.explanation: 教授指出雄蜂在试图交配时让花粉粘在身上，飞到下一朵兰花时便完成了传粉。A与教授强调没有回报相反；C讲座中未提；D是反向逻辑。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about evolutionary pressure on bees?

options.A: Bees will eventually evolve immunity to floral chemicals entirely.
options.B: Bees no longer pollinate any orchids of any kind.
options.C: Natural selection may favor bees that learn to avoid mimic orchids.
options.D: Bees and orchids will always benefit equally from their interaction.

questions.explanation: 教授提到自然选择可能青睐能避开兰花欺骗的蜜蜂，并且经验丰富的蜜蜂常常会拒绝这些花。其他选项与讲座观点相悖。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Take a look at this fresco from around 1300. Notice how flat it feels, how the figures seem to float without any real depth behind them. For centuries, European painters had essentially lost the ability to show space convincingly. So what changed? In the early fifteenth century, artists in Florence rediscovered linear perspective, a mathematical system for representing three-dimensional space on a flat surface. The key figure here is the architect Filippo Brunelleschi. Around 1420, he conducted a famous demonstration. He painted the Florence Baptistery on a small wooden panel, drilled a tiny hole through the center, and had viewers look through the back while holding a mirror up in front of it. When they compared the reflection to the actual building, the painted version matched the real one almost perfectly. The trick was the vanishing point, a single point on the horizon where parallel lines appear to converge. Once artists grasped this principle, painting transformed. The painter Masaccio applied it in his fresco of the Holy Trinity. Suddenly the painted chapel looked like a real architectural space you could walk right into. Now, it's important to understand what this was not. The ancient Romans had used some intuitive depth effects in their wall paintings, but they never formalized the underlying geometry. The Renaissance achievement was making it systematic and repeatable, something any trained painter could learn and reproduce. And that's exactly why perspective spread so quickly across Europe.
