questions.explanation: 教授提到在气候变化下绿屋成为更具吸引力的选择，暗示其普及度将上升。A、C、D不符合教授的总体立场。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how certain plants use mimicry to attract pollinators
items.transcript: Today's topic is one of the most striking examples of evolution in plants — the use of mimicry to attract pollinators. Mimicry, in this context, means that a plant has evolved to look or smell like something else, in order to trick a specific pollinator into visiting it. The pollinator gets nothing in return, but the plant gets its pollen transferred. Now, the textbook example is the bee orchid, which grows across parts of Europe and the Mediterranean. The flower has evolved a structure that closely resembles the body of a female bee. It even produces chemical compounds that mimic the female bee's sex pheromones. When a male bee detects these cues, he attempts to mate with the flower. As he struggles, the orchid's pollen sticks to his body. When he eventually moves on and tries to mate with another orchid, he carries the pollen with him, completing the transfer. Notice the trade-off here: the male bee invests time and energy and gets no reward at all. From an evolutionary perspective, you might expect natural selection to favor bees that learn to avoid these orchids. And to some extent, that does happen — experienced bees often refuse the flowers. But because young or naive bees keep falling for the trick, the orchid still gets enough successful pollinations to persist. Other plants use related strategies. Some carrion-mimicking flowers smell like rotting meat to attract flies that lay eggs in carcasses. So mimicry, in plants, isn't a single trick. It's a whole family of evolutionary strategies that exploit the perceptual systems of insects, and it tells us something deep about how natural selection shapes communication between species.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How plants regulate water loss through their flowers
options.B: How bees learn to identify reliable food sources
options.C: How orchids evolved to produce more nectar than other flowers
options.D: How some plants use mimicry to deceive their pollinators

questions.explanation: 讲座聚焦于植物如何通过模拟欺骗传粉者。A、B、C都偏离了核心主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does the bee orchid produce to attract a male bee?

options.A: Chemical compounds that mimic the female bee's sex pheromones
options.B: A sweet sugary nectar that contains additional sugars
options.C: Brightly colored pigments visible only at night
options.D: Ultrasonic vibrations within the petal structure

questions.explanation: 教授指出兰花产生模拟雌蜂性信息素的化学物质来吸引雄蜂。其他选项都不符合讲座内容。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens after the male bee tries to mate with the orchid?

options.A: The bee receives a sugary reward from the orchid.
options.B: The bee carries pollen from the flower to other orchids.
options.C: The bee dies shortly after the encounter.
options.D: The bee learns to avoid all orchids permanently.

questions.explanation: 教授指出雄蜂在试图交配时让花粉粘在身上，飞到下一朵兰花时便完成了传粉。A与教授强调没有回报相反；C讲座中未提；D是反向逻辑。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about evolutionary pressure on bees?

options.A: Bees will eventually evolve immunity to floral chemicals entirely.
options.B: Bees no longer pollinate any orchids of any kind.
options.C: Natural selection may favor bees that learn to avoid mimic orchids.
options.D: Bees and orchids will always benefit equally from their interaction.

questions.explanation: 教授提到自然选择可能青睐能避开兰花欺骗的蜜蜂，并且经验丰富的蜜蜂常常会拒绝这些花。其他选项与讲座观点相悖。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: the role of oral traditions in preserving cultural knowledge
items.transcript: Oral traditions are one of humanity's oldest tools for preserving knowledge across generations. Long before any society developed writing, people stored everything they needed to remember — laws, lineages, agricultural calendars, navigational routes — inside carefully structured stories, songs, and chants. And this wasn't haphazard. The structures themselves were designed to protect the information from being forgotten or distorted. So let me give you a concrete example. The Polynesian voyaging chants are remarkable. Pacific Islander navigators encoded entire ocean routes — star positions, wave patterns, the timing of bird migrations — into rhythmic songs that could be recited word for word, sometimes lasting hours. Anthropologists who recorded these chants in the twentieth century discovered something striking: when older navigators sang the same route decades apart, the lyrics were almost identical. The rhyme, the meter, the parallel structure — all of these features acted as built-in error correction. Drop a word, and the rhythm breaks. Replace a phrase, and the rhyme falls apart. The form forces the content to stay intact. Now, why does this matter? For a long time, Western scholars dismissed oral traditions as unreliable — assuming they would naturally drift over time, like the children's game of telephone. But evidence from the Maori, the Aboriginal Australians, and Indigenous communities in the Americas shows that some oral traditions remain accurate over remarkably long timescales. Australian Aboriginal stories about coastal landforms appear to encode geographical features that have been underwater for more than seven thousand years. That tells us oral traditions can preserve real information across periods we used to think only written records could span.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How structured oral traditions can reliably preserve knowledge over long periods
options.B: The differences between Western and indigenous scholarly methods
options.C: Pacific navigation techniques used by Polynesian voyagers
options.D: How children's games illustrate the spread of stories

questions.explanation: 讲座主旨是口头传统如何可靠地长期保存知识。B错在将主旨缩小为方法论对比。C只是讲座中的一个例子，过于狭窄。D借用了讲座中提到的儿童游戏类比，是表面词陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why are oral traditions so stable over time?

options.A: They are typically passed down within written family records
options.B: Their poetic structure provides built-in error correction
options.C: They are recited by professional scholars during ceremonies
options.D: They tend to be very short and easy to memorize

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出韵律、节奏和平行结构起到内置纠错作用。A颠倒事实——传统并非依赖书面记录。C是话题相关但讲座未提。D颠倒了讲座事实，口头传统可以很长。

questions.stem: What does the example of Australian Aboriginal stories suggest about oral traditions?

options.A: They are accurate only for events within recent centuries
options.B: They were originally written down by early colonists
options.C: They can preserve information across thousands of years
options.D: They focus exclusively on supernatural stories

questions.explanation: 教授提到澳大利亚原住民的故事记录了七千年前的海岸地貌，说明它们可以跨越数千年保存信息。A与讲座事实相反。B改变了真实关系——讲座说是口头传统而非书面记录。D是话题相关但讲座并未做此限制。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about earlier Western scholars?

options.A: They created the first systematic recordings of oral traditions
options.B: They mostly agreed with indigenous communities about story accuracy
options.C: They considered oral traditions superior to written records

questions.explanation: 教授指出西方学者过去认为口头传统不可靠，因此可推知他们低估了其可靠性。A是话题相关但讲座未支持。B与讲座完全相反。C颠倒了讲座中描述的态度。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: the rediscovery of perspective in early Renaissance painting
items.transcript: Linear perspective is one of those ideas that seems obvious once you know it — but for centuries, European painters didn't have it. Medieval art often depicted scenes with figures and buildings at strange, flattened angles. Distant objects were just smaller. There was no systematic way to make a painted surface look like a window into a three-dimensional world. Then, in early fifteenth-century Florence, that changed. The breakthrough is usually attributed to the architect Filippo Brunelleschi. Around 1413, Brunelleschi performed a famous experiment in the piazza outside the Florence Cathedral. He painted a precise image of the baptistery on a small wooden panel, drilled a tiny hole through its center, and asked viewers to look through the hole from the back of the panel while holding a mirror in front. The mirror reflected the painting; when they lowered the mirror, they saw the actual baptistery. The two images matched perfectly. Brunelleschi had cracked the mathematical rules of single-point perspective. His friend, the architect and theorist Leon Battista Alberti, then wrote those rules down in a treatise titled On Painting. Once Alberti's manual circulated, painters across Italy could replicate the technique systematically. Within a generation, artists like Masaccio were producing frescoes with deep, convincing spatial illusions — scenes that pulled the viewer in. So perspective wasn't just a new painting trick. It was a fundamentally different way of representing the world, one that linked art to geometry, optics, and architecture. And it transformed European visual culture for the next four centuries.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How Brunelleschi designed buildings using mirrors
options.B: The development of linear perspective in early Renaissance Italy
options.C: Differences between medieval and modern painting techniques
options.D: The use of geometry and optics in European architecture

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是线性透视法在文艺复兴早期意大利的发展。A错误地把镜子实验理解为建筑设计。C是话题相关但偏离主旨。D借用了几何和光学等词汇做表面陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Brunelleschi do with his painting of the baptistery?

options.A: He hid it in the cathedral for almost a century
options.B: He compared it to a fresco by Masaccio
options.C: He used a mirror and a small hole to verify its accuracy
options.D: He sent it to other painters across Italy

questions.explanation: 教授详细描述了用镜子和小孔验证画作的实验。A是凭空捏造的细节。B把后来的画家与实验搞混。D借用了讲座词汇但改变了事实——后来传播的是Alberti的论文。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention Alberti's treatise 'On Painting'?

options.A: To compare Alberti's painting style with Brunelleschi's experiments
options.B: To suggest that Alberti opposed the use of perspective
options.C: To explain why medieval painters resisted geometric methods
options.D: To explain how the new technique spread quickly through Italy

questions.explanation: 教授提及该论文是为了说明它使新技术能在意大利各地传播。A误解了提及的目的。B强加了教授未表达的反对立场。C完全偏离主题。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about European art after Brunelleschi's discovery?

options.A: It became closely tied to mathematics and science
options.B: It moved away from religious subject matter entirely
options.C: It abandoned traditional materials like tempera and fresco
options.D: It quickly spread to medieval painters across Europe

questions.explanation: 教授提到透视法把艺术与几何、光学、建筑联系起来，可推知艺术开始与数学和科学紧密相关。B过度概括。C是话题相关但讲座未支持。D颠倒了时间顺序——中世纪在前。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: Until the early twentieth century, music was almost entirely a live phenomenon. A composer wrote down notes; performers played them; listeners heard them once and then the sound was gone. The invention of sound recording fundamentally changed that relationship. And what's interesting — and what we'll focus on today — is that recording didn't just preserve existing music. Over time, it actively reshaped how composers wrote new music. In the first decades of recording, around the 1910s and 1920s, the technology had strict limits. Cylinders held about two minutes; early discs, around three. Composers who wanted their work captured had to write pieces that fit those time slots. Stride pianists, jazz bandleaders, and early blues musicians condensed extended improvisations into tight three-minute statements. That length wasn't a natural musical form — it was an industrial constraint that became a creative norm. Things shifted again when magnetic tape arrived after World War II. Tape could be cut, spliced, and overdubbed. By the 1960s, composers like the Beach Boys' Brian Wilson and the Beatles' George Martin treated the studio itself as a compositional instrument. The recording could include sounds that no human ensemble could perform live — backward tape, layered vocals impossible to sing in one take, orchestral textures assembled section by section. So the deeper lesson here is that technology and art shape each other. Recording didn't passively document music; it pushed composers toward new sonic possibilities and away from others. When we listen to a song that runs exactly three minutes and twenty seconds, we're hearing a form that was partly written by the machine.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of jazz improvisation in the early twentieth century
options.B: The technical differences between cylinders, discs, and magnetic tape
options.C: How recording technology influenced the composition of new music
options.D: The careers of producers like George Martin and Brian Wilson

questions.explanation: 讲座的核心论点是录音技术如何反过来塑造作曲。A只是讲座中的一个具体例子。B只关注技术细节。D借用了Martin和Wilson的名字做表面陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why did early jazz musicians compress improvisations into three-minute pieces?

options.A: Listeners had short attention spans during the early radio era
options.B: Live performances were limited to three minutes by venue rules
options.C: Composers experimented with rhythm faster than melody
options.D: Recording technology could only hold a few minutes per side

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出圆筒约两分钟、早期唱片约三分钟，是技术限制。A是话题相关但讲座未提。B改变了真实原因。C完全跑题。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention George Martin and Brian Wilson?

options.A: To illustrate how composers began treating the studio as an instrument
options.B: To show that pop producers preferred live recording over splicing
options.C: To explain why orchestral music declined after World War II
options.D: To suggest that tape recording was unpopular among composers

questions.explanation: 教授用他们的名字说明作曲家如何把录音棚当作乐器来用。B强加了与讲座相反的观点。C完全偏离话题。D也与讲座相反。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the typical length of pop songs today?

options.A: It has gradually grown longer as streaming replaced records
options.B: It still reflects choices that early recording technology imposed
options.C: It varies widely with no relationship to historical formats
options.D: It was determined mainly by live performance traditions

questions.explanation: 教授结尾用三分二十秒的歌曲说明，今天的歌曲长度仍部分由早期录音机决定。A是话题相关但与讲座结尾相反。C过度概括。D颠倒了原因。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: stereotype threat and academic performance
items.transcript: In the 1990s, the psychologist Claude Steele introduced an idea that explained a puzzle researchers had been studying for years: the persistent gap in standardized test scores between different demographic groups, even when students had identical preparation. Steele's hypothesis was that part of the gap had nothing to do with ability, prior knowledge, or motivation. It came from something he called stereotype threat. The basic idea is this. When you are aware that a negative stereotype exists about a group you belong to — say, the stereotype that women perform worse on advanced math — that awareness can become a cognitive burden in the very moment you're being tested. Part of your mental capacity goes to worrying about confirming the stereotype, and that leaves less working memory for the actual problem in front of you. Let me describe the classic experiment. Steele and his colleague Joshua Aronson gave a difficult math test to a group of female and male undergraduates. Before one version of the test, they told participants that the test had shown gender differences in the past. Before another version, they said the test had shown no gender differences. The result was striking. Women in the second condition performed essentially the same as the men. Women in the first condition performed substantially worse, even though every other variable was identical. Now, here's why this matters. Stereotype threat shows up across many domains — for older adults on memory tests, for white athletes in certain sports settings, even for boys on tasks framed as testing social sensitivity. So it's not about any particular group; it's about the cognitive cost of fighting a stereotype while trying to perform.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of standardized testing in the United States
options.B: Methods for closing demographic gaps in mathematics education
options.C: The career of psychologist Claude Steele and his colleagues
options.D: A psychological phenomenon that can lower test performance

questions.explanation: 讲座主线是刻板印象威胁这一心理现象如何影响测试表现。A脱离话题。B只是其中一个应用领域。C用Steele的名字做表面陷阱，但讲座焦点是现象本身。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe Steele and Aronson's experiment?

options.A: To provide concrete evidence supporting the concept of stereotype threat
options.B: To argue that women are inherently weaker at advanced math
options.C: To explain why standardized tests should be banned outright
options.D: To show how identical preparation always produces identical results

questions.explanation: 教授描述实验是为了用具体证据支持刻板印象威胁概念。B强加了与讲座完全相反的立场。C超出讲座结论。D颠倒了实验要点。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention older adults and white athletes?

options.A: To compare the social pressures faced by different age groups
options.B: To show that stereotype threat affects a wide range of groups
options.C: To explain why athletic performance has nothing to do with cognition
options.D: To suggest that the original Steele studies were too narrow

questions.explanation: 教授列举这些例子是为了说明该现象影响很多群体。A是错误目的。C是过度推论。D强加了教授未表达的批评。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about reducing stereotype threat?

options.A: It requires removing all references to standardized testing
options.B: It is most effective when participants are unaware of the threat
options.C: Changing how a test is described to participants may help reduce it
options.D: It will gradually disappear without any intervention

questions.explanation: 实验显示，仅仅改变测试介绍方式就能改变结果，可推知改变描述可以减轻威胁。A过度概括。B与实验设计相反。D是话题相关但讲座没暗示。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: Convergent evolution in marine animals
items.transcript: Convergent evolution is one of the most striking patterns in biology. The basic idea is that organisms from completely separate lineages can end up with very similar body plans because they face similar environmental pressures. So let me give you the classic marine example. Look at a shark, a dolphin, and an extinct marine reptile called an ichthyosaur. A shark is a fish. A dolphin is a mammal that evolved from land animals about fifty million years ago. An ichthyosaur was a reptile from the age of dinosaurs. Three totally different starting points. And yet they all converged on the same streamlined torpedo shape, with a vertical or horizontal tail and dorsal fins for stability. Why? Because moving fast through water rewards a very narrow set of body geometries. Drag forces in water are intense, so any species that doesn't streamline efficiently uses far more energy and gets outcompeted. It turns out the physics of the medium constrains the solution. Now here's what's really interesting. Convergent evolution tells us that evolution isn't completely random. The same problem, presented to different lineages, often produces the same answer. That doesn't mean evolution is predictable in detail, but it does suggest there are deep structural rules at work. Many of the body plans we see today aren't accidents. They're answers to physical and ecological problems that nature has solved more than once.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The history of how scientists discovered ichthyosaurs
options.B: Why dolphins evolved from land-dwelling mammals
options.C: A pattern in which unrelated species independently develop similar features
options.D: How sharks and dolphins compete for food in the ocean

questions.explanation: 讲座主要讨论趋同进化——不同物种独立演化出相似特征。A、B、D 只涉及具体细节或不相关的内容。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do sharks, dolphins, and ichthyosaurs share?

options.A: A common evolutionary ancestor in the ocean
options.B: A streamlined body shape suited to fast swimming
options.C: The same type of skeletal structure
options.D: An extinct lineage from the same geological era

questions.explanation: 教授指出三种动物都演化出了流线型的鱼雷状身体。A 反了——它们不是同一祖先。C 和 D 与文本不符。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention drag forces?

options.A: To compare swimming speeds across species
options.B: To explain why streamlined shapes are favored in water
options.C: To describe how sharks digest food efficiently
options.D: To suggest that water animals evolve slowly

questions.explanation: 教授提到水中阻力，是为了说明流线型身体如何节省能量，从而被选择。其他选项与水中阻力的作用无关。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about evolution?

options.A: It always proceeds through random mutations alone
options.B: It produces unique solutions for each lineage
options.C: It can converge on the same answer under similar pressures
options.D: It rarely produces useful traits in marine species

questions.explanation: 教授暗示进化在相似压力下会得出相同答案。A 与教授观点相反——他说进化并非完全随机。B 是反向逻辑。D 与文本无关。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: The mere-exposure effect
items.transcript: Have you ever started liking a song the more you hear it on the radio? Or warmed up to a face you keep running into on campus? Well, this is actually a well-studied phenomenon in psychology called the mere-exposure effect. The basic idea is simple. Just being exposed to something repeatedly, with no other positive event attached, can make you like it more. The classic study was done by Robert Zajonc in 1968. He showed participants a series of unfamiliar Chinese characters. Some characters appeared just once, others up to twenty-five times. Then he asked participants to guess what each character meant. The characters they had seen more often were consistently rated as having more positive meanings, even though no one had told them anything about the symbols. The effect has been replicated with photos, words, melodies, and even shapes. Now here's what's interesting. The effect tends to be strongest when you don't consciously notice the exposure. So an ad you barely registered while scrolling can still shift your preferences later. Marketing teams have known about this for decades. But the effect also has limits. If you really dislike something at first, more exposure can actually deepen the dislike rather than soften it. So the mere-exposure effect isn't a guarantee. It's a tendency that depends on starting from a roughly neutral position. That's a nuance that the simplified version often misses.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why some advertising campaigns fail in modern markets
options.B: A psychological tendency in which familiarity tends to breed liking
options.C: How Robert Zajonc became famous in social psychology
options.D: Why people remember Chinese characters better than letters

questions.explanation: 讲座主要讲解曝光效应——熟悉度通常会增加好感。其他选项要么过窄要么偏离主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Zajonc's 1968 study find?

options.A: Participants disliked characters they had seen frequently
options.B: Participants memorized the meanings of all characters quickly
options.C: Participants rated repeatedly seen characters more positively
options.D: Participants ignored characters they did not understand

questions.explanation: 实验显示出现次数多的字符被评价为含义更积极。A 是反向；B 和 D 与文本不符。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention modern advertising?

options.A: To suggest that unnoticed exposure can shift preferences
options.B: To argue that marketers should reduce ad frequency
options.C: To compare visual ads with audio ads in effectiveness
options.D: To recommend a specific advertising strategy

questions.explanation: 教授提及广告是为了说明即使没意识到，曝光也能影响偏好。其他选项不是真正目的。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about repeated exposure?

options.A: It guarantees that people will like any product more
options.B: Its effect depends on the starting attitude toward the object
options.C: It works equally well for both liked and disliked items
options.D: It rarely produces measurable changes in preference

questions.explanation: 教授强调起始态度重要——若一开始就反感，曝光反而加深反感。A、C 是反向；D 与文本相悖。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: How sedimentary rocks form
items.transcript: Sedimentary rocks are one of the three main rock types on Earth, and they tell us more about the planet's past than any other type. Let me explain why. Sedimentary rocks form from sediment, which is just particles of older rocks and biological material that have been broken down by wind, water, ice, or chemical action. These particles get transported, sometimes for hundreds of kilometers, and eventually settle in low-energy environments like the floor of a lake, a river delta, or the deep sea. Now, here's where it gets interesting. As more and more sediment piles up on top, the lower layers get compressed and cemented together. Compaction squeezes water out of the spaces between grains. Then dissolved minerals like calcium carbonate or silica precipitate into those gaps and glue the grains into solid rock. The whole process can take thousands or millions of years. Why do these rocks matter so much for understanding the past? Because they often preserve evidence of the environments where they formed. A layer of fine mud full of plant fossils suggests a quiet swamp. A layer of well-sorted sand suggests a beach. Ripple marks indicate flowing water. Fossils of marine creatures locked inside a sandstone tell us that part of a continent was once underwater. So a thick sequence of sedimentary layers is essentially a tape recording of past environments, stacked oldest at the bottom and youngest on top. Geologists read that tape to reconstruct how Earth has changed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How sedimentary rocks form and what they reveal about the past
options.B: The chemical composition of common minerals on Earth
options.C: Why mountains form along the edges of tectonic plates
options.D: How fossils are extracted from solid rock by geologists

questions.explanation: 讲座主要讲沉积岩的形成过程及其揭示的环境信息。其他选项偏离主题。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens during compaction?

options.A: Sediment is carried by rivers to a delta region
options.B: Water is squeezed out of spaces between grains
options.C: Fossils dissolve into the surrounding minerals
options.D: Layers are folded into mountains by tectonic forces

questions.explanation: 教授明确指出压实过程会挤出颗粒间的水。其他选项与压实概念无关。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe ripple marks in sedimentary layers?

options.A: To show that rocks contain dangerous minerals
options.B: To illustrate evidence of past flowing water
options.C: To explain why some fossils are crushed
options.D: To suggest that sediment never moves once deposited

questions.explanation: 教授举波痕例子来说明沉积岩能保留过去流水的证据。其他选项不是真正目的。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a thick sequence of sedimentary layers?

options.A: It is too damaged to study reliably with modern tools
options.B: It tends to form only in regions with active volcanoes
options.C: It records a long history of changing environments
options.D: It contains no fossils older than a few million years

questions.explanation: 教授将其比喻为录音带，记录过去环境变迁。其他选项与文本相悖或无据。

items.subject: history
items.topic: The spread of paper in medieval Europe
items.transcript: Paper transformed medieval Europe more than almost any other technology of its era, but its arrival was surprisingly slow. Paper itself was invented in China around the second century, and it reached the Islamic world by the eighth century. By the eleventh century, papermills were operating in Spain, but it took another two hundred years for paper to be widely produced in northern and central Europe. Why such a delay? A big part of the answer is parchment. Parchment is animal skin, prepared and stretched until it can be written on. It's incredibly durable, and a well-made parchment manuscript can survive for a thousand years. For centuries, Europe's monasteries had built their entire system of book production around parchment, and they didn't see much reason to switch. Paper, by comparison, looked fragile and cheap. Now, what changed? Two things, mostly. First, demand for written documents exploded in the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries — universities were expanding, trade networks were generating contracts, and city governments needed records. Parchment simply couldn't be produced fast enough or cheaply enough. Second, the technology of papermaking improved. European mills developed better fiber preparation and stronger sizing agents, which made paper much more durable. By the time Gutenberg started printing in the 1450s, paper had become the obvious choice. Without it, the printing revolution would have been impossible. Parchment would have been too expensive to print in large runs. So the slow rise of paper in Europe is really a story about how a technology needs the right context before it can change the world.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why Gutenberg invented the printing press in the 1450s
options.B: How parchment manuscripts were repaired in monasteries
options.C: Why paper spread slowly through medieval Europe and then transformed it
options.D: How Chinese inventors first developed the technology of papermaking

questions.explanation: 讲座讨论造纸为何在欧洲传播缓慢及其影响。A、B 太狭隘，D 与教授重点不符。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why were European monasteries slow to adopt paper?

options.A: They thought parchment was too durable to abandon
options.B: They believed paper was banned by Church authorities
options.C: They lacked access to the necessary fiber materials
options.D: They preferred the cheaper price of imported parchment

questions.explanation: 教授解释修道院满意现有羊皮纸系统，认为没必要更换。B、C、D 与文本不符。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention universities and trade networks?

options.A: To compare medieval education with modern systems
options.B: To show how demand for documents grew rapidly in the era
options.C: To suggest that trade declined during the period
options.D: To explain how monasteries lost political influence

questions.explanation: 教授提及大学和贸易是为了说明文献需求暴涨。其他选项不是真正目的。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the printing revolution?

options.A: It would have happened earlier without paper technology
options.B: It depended on a cheaper writing surface being available
options.C: It made parchment more valuable across Europe
options.D: It was rejected by most universities until the 1500s

questions.explanation: 教授暗示没有便宜的纸张，印刷革命就不可能发生。其他选项与文本相悖。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: the influence of climate on traditional vernacular housing
items.transcript: Architecture isn't always about famous architects and signature buildings. In many parts of the world, the most interesting structures are houses built by ordinary people using nothing more than the materials and techniques their grandparents used. We call this vernacular architecture, and what's striking is how precisely it responds to local climate. So today, let's look at three quick examples. First, think about a traditional house in the hot, dry regions of the Middle East. The walls are extraordinarily thick, often packed earth or stone, and the windows are small and placed high on the wall. Why? Because thick walls absorb heat slowly during the day and release it during the cool night, so the inside temperature stays remarkably stable. Now, compare that with a wooden stilt house in a tropical rainforest. There, the problem isn't heat alone, it's humidity and constant rain. Stilts lift the floor above damp ground and let air circulate underneath, and a steep roof sheds heavy rainfall before it can rot the structure. The shape is light, open, and almost the opposite of the desert house. Finally, look at a Nordic farmhouse. Insulated turf roofs, small windows, and a central hearth concentrate warmth in a tight, well-sealed envelope. You can almost read the local weather just by examining one of these houses. The deeper point is that traditional builders ran centuries of climate experiments that we sometimes overlook. As architects search for low-energy designs today, they're going back and studying these old houses very carefully, because the wisdom embedded in them is honestly hard to beat.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How traditional houses across regions were shaped by their local climates
options.B: Why modern architects ignore the lessons of vernacular building styles
options.C: How desert houses keep their interior temperatures stable
options.D: Why famous architects designed signature buildings around weather

questions.explanation: 讲座以三个不同气候区的房屋为例，论证传统民居如何响应当地气候，故 A 正确。B 与讲座结尾的态度相反；C 只是其中一个例子，过窄；D 与讲座对'非著名建筑师'的关注相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why are the walls of a desert house extremely thick?

options.A: Because thick walls allow rainwater to drain quickly outside
options.B: Because they store daytime heat and release it slowly at night
options.C: Because thick walls reduce the need for very high small windows
options.D: Because they support the heavy turf roofs found in the region

questions.explanation: 教授说厚墙'absorb heat slowly during the day and release it during the cool night'，故 B 对。A 与雨林房屋有关；C 关系颠倒，与小窗的关系不是支持；D 是北欧农舍的特征。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is one purpose of the stilts on a tropical rainforest house?

options.A: They let the structure lean toward the prevailing wind
options.B: They reduce the steepness needed in the roof above
options.C: They keep the floor above damp ground and let air circulate
options.D: They allow the central hearth to be installed safely below

questions.explanation: 讲座明确说明'Stilts lift the floor above damp ground and let air circulate underneath'，故 C 对。A 凭空发挥；B 与文意相反，屋顶仍需陡峭；D 是北欧炉灶的位置错配到雨林。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about modern low-energy architectural design?

options.A: It has rejected vernacular wisdom in favor of new materials
options.B: It performs poorly compared with desert-region houses
options.C: It now dominates construction in tropical rainforests
options.D: It can benefit by carefully studying traditional house designs

questions.explanation: 讲座结尾说现代建筑师'going back and studying these old houses very carefully'并赞美其智慧，可推出 D。A 与该陈述相反；B 没有提及性能对比；C 与文意无关。

items.subject: neuroscience
items.topic: how mirror neurons relate to empathy and imitation
items.transcript: Mirror neurons are some of the most discussed cells in cognitive neuroscience, and there's still real debate about how much they explain. Let's start with what we actually know. In the early 1990s, researchers at the University of Parma were recording from neurons in the premotor cortex of macaque monkeys. They noticed something unexpected. Certain neurons fired when the monkey reached for a peanut, which was expected. But the same neurons also fired when the monkey simply watched a researcher reach for the peanut. The brain seemed to mirror the action. Now, why does that matter? Well, one influential idea is that mirror neurons provide a basic neural mechanism for imitation. If watching an action activates the same circuit you would use to perform it, then learning by watching becomes far less mysterious. A second hypothesis goes further. Some researchers argue that mirror neurons help us understand other people's intentions, and that this understanding scaffolds empathy. When you see someone reach for a cup of coffee, you don't just see the motion, you sort of feel the goal. That is the bold claim. So here's where it gets complicated. The mirror neuron evidence in humans is mostly indirect, because we rarely record from single cells in healthy people. Brain imaging shows overlapping regions for action and observation, which is suggestive but not conclusive. And recent reviews caution that empathy involves many systems besides mirror neurons, including emotional and cognitive ones. So when you read a popular article calling mirror neurons the basis of empathy, it's worth treating that claim with a bit of caution. Real life empathy is layered, and mirror neurons are probably one ingredient, not the whole story.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How researchers in Parma first trained monkeys to imitate humans
options.B: What mirror neurons may explain about imitation and empathy, and the limits of those claims
options.C: Why brain imaging is the only reliable way to study empathy in humans
options.D: Why popular accounts of mirror neurons are completely wrong

questions.explanation: 讲座既介绍了镜像神经元在模仿和共情上的假设，又强调了证据的局限，B 最准确。A 与讲座事实矛盾；C 过度概括；D 把'谨慎对待'夸大为'完全错误'。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the macaque monkeys reaching for peanuts?

options.A: To suggest that mirror neurons are only present in non-human primates
options.B: To prove that mirror neurons in humans are now fully understood
options.C: To illustrate the original observation that defined mirror neurons
options.D: To compare the diet of macaques with that of laboratory researchers

questions.explanation: 教授提到这一例子是为了介绍镜像神经元最初被发现的过程，故 C 对。A 与讲座中人类讨论相反；B 与讲座对'间接证据'的强调相反；D 与讲座目的无关。

questions.stem: According to the professor, why is human evidence for mirror neurons mostly indirect?

options.A: Because brain imaging is too slow to detect neural activity
options.B: Because the premotor cortex is inaccessible during surgery
options.C: Because empathy and action share no overlapping regions
options.D: Because researchers rarely record from single cells in healthy people

questions.explanation: 讲座原话指出'we rarely record from single cells in healthy people'，故 D 对。A 与脑成像速度无关；B 凭空发挥；C 与讲座事实相反。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what does recent research suggest about empathy?

options.A: Empathy involves many systems beyond mirror neurons alone
options.B: Empathy can be measured only in macaques, not in humans
options.C: Empathy disappears when mirror neurons are damaged
options.D: Empathy is determined entirely by visual observation of actions

questions.explanation: 教授提到'empathy involves many systems besides mirror neurons, including emotional and cognitive ones'，故 A 对。B、C、D 都与讲座事实相反或缺乏依据。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: how the Doppler effect changes the pitch of a passing sound
items.transcript: You've probably noticed the way an ambulance siren seems to change pitch as it speeds past you. The sound gets higher as it approaches, then suddenly drops as it moves away. That shift isn't your imagination. It's a real physical effect called the Doppler effect, first described by Austrian physicist Christian Doppler in 1842. Let me explain why it happens. Sound travels as a series of pressure waves rippling outward from the source. Imagine a buoy floating in the ocean. If the buoy stays still, the waves leaving it spread outward in even, concentric circles. But now imagine the buoy starts moving forward through the water. The waves in front of it get bunched up, while the waves behind it spread out. Sound works the same way. When the ambulance moves toward you, the sound waves arriving at your ear get compressed, meaning more waves reach you each second. More waves per second means a higher frequency, which your ear perceives as higher pitch. As the ambulance passes and moves away, the waves stretch out, fewer reach you per second, and the pitch drops. Now, the Doppler effect isn't just a curiosity for sirens. Astronomers use the same principle with light to measure how fast distant galaxies are moving. When a galaxy moves away from Earth, its light gets stretched toward the red end of the spectrum, which we call redshift. Most galaxies show redshift, and that's a major piece of evidence that the universe is expanding. So the next time you hear a siren change pitch, remember that the same effect is helping astronomers map the cosmos.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why astronomers prefer to measure light over sound waves
options.B: How sirens have been improved by modern engineering teams
options.C: Why a passing sound source shifts in pitch and how the same principle aids astronomy
options.D: Why the ocean buoy analogy fully captures the behavior of light

questions.explanation: 讲座先解释多普勒效应导致音高变化，再引申到天文学的红移，故 C 涵盖全文。A 是片段；B 与讲座无关；D 把比喻当结论，且夸大。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to sound waves in front of an approaching source?

options.A: They stretch out into a wider concentric circle
options.B: They lose all of their energy before reaching the ear
options.C: They reflect back toward the moving source instead
options.D: They get compressed so more reach the ear per second

questions.explanation: 讲座说前方波被压缩，每秒到达耳朵更多，故 D 对。A 与拉伸后方波相反；B 凭空发挥；C 与多普勒效应无关。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe a buoy floating in the ocean?

options.A: To provide a clear visual analogy for how sound waves bunch up
options.B: To argue that ocean physics and sound physics are unrelated
options.C: To suggest that buoys are used to measure distant galaxies
options.D: To demonstrate how Christian Doppler conducted his early experiments

questions.explanation: 浮标比喻是为了直观说明运动声源前方波被压缩。B 与讲座目的相反；C 凭空发挥；D 是历史事实的虚构。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about a galaxy whose light shows redshift?

options.A: It is rapidly approaching Earth at high speed
options.B: It is moving away from Earth in the expanding universe
options.C: It does not emit any light of its own anymore
options.D: It is older than every other galaxy in the cosmos

questions.explanation: 讲座说远离地球的星系出现红移，且大多数星系都红移，证明宇宙膨胀，故 B 对。A 与红移含义相反；C、D 凭空发挥。

items.subject: social_psychology
items.topic: the diffusion of responsibility in large groups
items.transcript: Today's topic is something every social psychologist eventually teaches, and that's diffusion of responsibility. The idea is simple but the consequences are significant. When many people are present in an emergency, each one feels less personally responsible for stepping in. So let's start with the classic case. In the late 1960s, two researchers named Darley and Latané ran a study where students sat in cubicles and talked through intercoms. During the conversation, one student pretended to have a seizure. When the student believed only one other person was listening, eighty-five percent rushed out to help. But when the student believed four others were listening, only thirty-one percent helped. The bigger the group, the less each individual acted. Why does that happen? Two mechanisms seem to be at work. First, responsibility is divided. If twenty people are present, you assume someone else will probably act. Second, there's social influence. You glance around to see how others are reacting, and if everyone else looks calm, you decide the situation must not be that serious. The result is a kind of frozen collective politeness. Now, this effect isn't just a textbook curiosity. It shapes everything from workplace safety to online behavior. On a busy street corner, a person collapsing may be ignored for surprising stretches of time. In a group chat with two hundred members, a clear question can sit unanswered for hours. The practical takeaway, and you'll see this in safety training, is simple. If you're the one in trouble, don't shout to the crowd. Point at one specific person and ask that individual directly for help.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why Darley and Latané designed cubicle-based intercom experiments
options.B: How online group chats damage real-world workplace safety
options.C: How social influence convinces calm bystanders to panic
options.D: Why bystanders are less likely to help as group size grows, and what to do about it

questions.explanation: 讲座阐述了大群体中个体不行动的原因并给出实用建议，D 最贴合。A、B 仅为细节；C 与讲座中描述相反，社会影响让人保持冷静而非恐慌。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what percentage of students helped when they believed only one other person was listening?

options.A: About eighty-five percent of the students helped immediately
options.B: About thirty-one percent of the students helped immediately
options.C: About twenty percent of the students helped immediately
options.D: About fifty percent of the students helped immediately

questions.explanation: 讲座中明确说'eighty-five percent rushed out to help'，故 A 对。B 是四人在场的比例；C、D 是凭空发挥。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention a group chat of two hundred members?

options.A: To prove that small chat groups always answer questions quickly
options.B: To illustrate how diffusion of responsibility happens online as well
options.C: To recommend leaving any chat that has more than fifty members
options.D: To demonstrate that online emergencies are more dangerous than physical ones

questions.explanation: 群聊例子是为了说明责任分散同样发生在线上场景，故 B 对。A 与讲座中'sit unanswered for hours'相反；C 与文意无关；D 凭空发挥。

questions.stem: Why does the professor advise pointing at one specific person when asking for help?

options.A: To demonstrate that crowds always respond faster to formal language
options.B: To argue that shouting at a crowd is the most polite approach available
options.C: To make a single bystander feel directly responsible for acting
options.D: To prove that emergencies are caused by frozen collective politeness

questions.explanation: 讲座建议直接指向一人是为了让该个体觉得自己被点名负责，故 C 对。A、B 与建议相反；D 把现象误作原因。

items.subject: architecture
items.topic: how the skyscraper became possible with steel-frame construction
items.transcript: Think about how tall a building can be if you stack heavy stone walls. Not very tall at all. In a traditional masonry building, the walls themselves carry the entire weight of the structure. As you add floors, the lower walls have to bear more and more load, so they must get thicker and thicker. By the time you reach ten or twelve stories, the ground-floor walls would be so massive they'd swallow most of the usable space. That's the wall they had to break through, and the answer came from steel. In the eighteen-eighties, engineers in Chicago began constructing buildings around an internal skeleton of steel beams and columns. This is called the steel frame. The crucial idea is that the frame, not the walls, carries the load. The exterior walls become a thin curtain, hung onto the frame like a coat on a rack. They keep out the weather, but they hold up nothing. The Home Insurance Building, completed in Chicago in eighteen eighty-five, is usually credited as the first true skyscraper using this method. Once the frame carried the weight, the limiting factor was no longer the strength of the walls but practical concerns like wind and elevators. So height became a question of engineering, not of masonry. This shift didn't just make taller buildings possible. It transformed the look of the modern city, allowing the glass-and-steel towers we now take for granted. The wall stopped being a support and became a skin.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How steel-frame construction made the skyscraper possible
options.B: The history of the city of Chicago in the eighteen-eighties
options.C: Why stone walls must get thicker on lower floors
options.D: How elevators allowed buildings to become much taller

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。整段讲座的核心是钢框架结构如何让承重从墙体转移到内部钢骨架，从而使摩天大楼成为可能。B选项过于宽泛，芝加哥只是事件发生地，并非主题。C选项是讲座前半段用来说明传统砌体局限的一个细节，过于狭窄。D选项利用了讲座中提到的电梯一词，但电梯只是被顺带提及，不是主旨。干扰项类型：B是wrong_topic，C是too_narrow，D是surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what role do the exterior walls play in a steel-frame building?

options.A: They carry most of the weight of the upper floors
options.B: They form a thin curtain and hold up nothing
options.C: They must grow thicker toward the bottom of the building
options.D: They replace the steel columns on the ground floor

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授明确说在钢框架建筑中，外墙变成一层薄薄的幕墙，像挂在架子上的外套，挡风雨但不承重。A选项与讲座相反，承重的是框架而非墙。C选项描述的是传统砌体建筑墙体的特点，被讲座用作对比，并非钢框架外墙。D选项是讲座未提及且与框架承重逻辑相悖的说法。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，C是wrong_detail，D是topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe stacking heavy stone walls at the start of the lecture?

options.A: To praise the durability of traditional masonry buildings
options.B: To claim that stone is stronger than steel for tall towers
options.C: To set up the problem that steel-frame construction solved
options.D: To describe the appearance of the modern glass-and-steel city

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授开头描述堆叠石墙是为了引出传统建筑高度受限这一难题，进而说明钢框架如何解决它。A选项错误，教授并非赞美砌体的耐久性，而是指出其局限。B选项与讲座相反，讲座说明钢框架优于厚墙。D选项是讲座结尾才出现的内容，与开头石墙描述的目的无关。干扰项类型：A是wrong_purpose，B是over_inference，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the height of skyscrapers after the steel frame was adopted?

options.A: It was still strictly limited by the thickness of the walls
options.B: It could no longer be increased beyond about twelve stories
options.C: It depended mainly on the quality of the stone available
options.D: It became chiefly a matter of engineering rather than masonry

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授说一旦框架承重，限制因素就不再是墙的强度，而是风和电梯等工程问题，因此高度成为工程问题而非砌体问题。A选项与讲座相反，墙的厚度不再是限制。B选项把传统砌体的十到十二层上限错误地套到了钢框架时代。C选项重提石材质量，但钢框架时代石材已不再是关键。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，C是over_generalization。

items.subject: music
items.topic: how recording technology changed the way music was composed
items.transcript: For most of history, a piece of music existed only as a performance. It happened once, in a room, and then it was gone. Composers wrote scores so that performers could recreate the work, but the score was just a set of instructions. The actual sound vanished the moment the players stopped. Recording technology changed that completely. Once sound could be captured and replayed, music became a fixed object that could be studied, edited, and perfected. And this didn't just preserve performances. It began to change how composers and artists thought about the work itself. Consider the recording studio in the nineteen-sixties. Artists started using the studio not as a place to document a live performance, but as an instrument in its own right. They layered tracks recorded at different times, spliced together the best moments from many takes, and added effects that no live ensemble could produce. A finished recording might be a performance that never actually happened in one continuous moment. The Beatles, for instance, created songs in the studio that they could not reproduce on stage at all. This marks a real shift. Composition was no longer only about writing notes for live players. It became about shaping recorded sound directly. The recording stopped being a copy of the music and became the music itself. And that idea still shapes how popular music is made today, where the recording is usually the original work, and any live version is the imitation.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why live performances sound better than studio recordings
options.B: How recording technology changed the way music was created
options.C: The career of the Beatles during the nineteen-sixties
options.D: How musical scores are written down for performers

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。讲座主旨是录音技术如何改变了音乐创作本身——从为现场演奏写谱转向直接塑造录制的声音。A选项与讲座相反，讲座并未说现场演奏更好。C选项中Beatles只是一个例证，过于狭窄。D选项利用了讲座中提到的乐谱一词，但乐谱只是背景对比，不是主题。干扰项类型：A是wrong_topic，C是too_narrow，D是surface_word_trap。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how did artists in the nineteen-sixties begin to use the recording studio?

options.A: As a way to write down scores for live performers
options.B: As a place to document a single live performance faithfully
options.C: As an instrument in its own right for shaping sound
options.D: As a method to make recordings sound exactly like concerts

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授明确说六十年代的艺术家开始把录音棚当作一种独立的乐器来使用，分层录音、拼接片段、加入现场无法实现的效果。A选项与录音棚的用途无关，把录谱混入。B选项恰恰是讲座说他们不再这样做的旧方式。D选项与讲座相反，他们制作的是现场无法重现的声音。干扰项类型：A是topic_adjacent，B是reversed_logic，D是wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the Beatles?

options.A: To argue that the Beatles invented recording technology
options.B: To compare the Beatles with classical composers of the past
options.C: To describe the layering of tracks done in the studio
options.D: To give an example of music that could not be performed live

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授举Beatles为例，是要说明他们在录音棚里创作出根本无法在舞台上重现的歌曲，从而印证创作重心转向录制声音。A选项过度推断，讲座并未说他们发明了录音技术。B选项错误，此处并非与古典作曲家作比较。C选项只是复述了studio中做了什么，而非解释提到Beatles的目的。干扰项类型：A是over_inference，B是wrong_purpose，C是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a live version of a modern pop song?

options.A: It is usually treated as an imitation of the recording
options.B: It is always superior to the studio recording
options.C: It is the original work that the recording copies
options.D: It can never be performed by the original artists

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授在结尾说如今录音通常是原作，而现场版本是模仿，由此可推断现代流行歌曲的现场版被视为录音的模仿。B选项过度夸大，讲座并未说现场总是更好。C选项与讲座相反，原作是录音而非现场。D选项太绝对，讲座只说某些作品现场难以重现，并非永远无法演出。干扰项类型：B是over_generalization，C是reversed_logic，D是topic_adjacent。

items.subject: art_history
items.topic: why Impressionists painted outdoors and what that meant for color theory
items.transcript: Impressionism emerged in France in the eighteen-seventies, and one of its boldest moves was simply leaving the studio. Earlier painters worked indoors, building up carefully finished pictures under steady, controlled light. The Impressionists wanted something else. They wanted to capture a fleeting moment, the way light actually looks on a particular afternoon. So they carried their easels outside and painted directly from nature, a practice known by the French phrase 'en plein air.' This wasn't just a change of location. It forced a change in how they understood color. Outdoors, light shifts constantly, and colors are never pure or isolated. A white wall in shadow isn't simply gray. It picks up blue from the sky and warm tones from nearby surfaces. Painters working in a studio, mixing colors slowly on a palette, would smooth all of that away. But out in the open, the Impressionists noticed that shadows are full of color. To capture this, they applied small strokes of bright, often unmixed paint side by side. Stand close, and the strokes look like a confusing patchwork. Step back, and your eye blends them into a shimmering whole. Two new paint tubes and portable easels, recently manufactured, made this outdoor work practical for the first time. The result was a fresh, luminous way of seeing. By painting outdoors, the Impressionists weren't just choosing new subjects. They were rethinking what color is and how the eye perceives it.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: The invention of portable easels and ready-made paint tubes
options.B: Why earlier painters preferred to work inside a studio
options.C: How painting outdoors changed the Impressionists' view of color
options.D: The history of art movements in France in the eighteen-seventies

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。讲座主旨是印象派走出画室到户外作画，以及这一做法如何改变了他们对色彩的理解。A选项是讲座中支持户外作画的一个技术细节，过于狭窄。B选项是用来对比的背景，并非主题。D选项过于宽泛，讲座只聚焦印象派而非整个法国艺术史。干扰项类型：A是too_narrow，B是too_narrow，D是wrong_topic。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did the Impressionists notice about shadows when painting outdoors?

options.A: They were always a pure, neutral gray
options.B: They disappeared entirely in bright afternoon light
options.C: They were easiest to paint slowly on a palette indoors
options.D: They were full of color reflected from the surroundings

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。教授明确说在户外，印象派发现阴影里充满色彩，比如阴影中的白墙会反射天空的蓝色和附近物体的暖色。A选项与讲座相反，讲座特意指出阴影不是单纯的灰色。B选项是讲座未提及的说法。C选项把室内调色板的做法错误地安到了户外观察上。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，C是wrong_detail。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe stepping close to and then back from the painting?

options.A: To explain how separate strokes of color blend in the viewer's eye
options.B: To argue that Impressionist paintings should only be viewed up close
options.C: To prove that the Impressionists could not mix colors well
options.D: To describe how studio painters finished their pictures

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授描述凑近看是杂乱笔触、退后看则融为一体，是为了说明观众的眼睛会把并置的纯色笔触在视觉上混合起来。B选项过度推断，讲座并未说只能近看。C选项错误，分开上色是有意的技法，而非能力不足。D选项与此处描述的目的无关，画室画家是对比对象。干扰项类型：B是over_inference，C是wrong_purpose，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studio painting compared with painting outdoors?

options.A: It captured fleeting changes in light more accurately
options.B: It tended to smooth away the subtle colors found in shadows
options.C: It relied on small strokes of bright, unmixed paint
options.D: It was the main subject the Impressionists wanted to depict

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授说在画室里慢慢调色会把阴影中那些微妙的色彩抹平，由此可推断画室作画倾向于消除这些色彩。A选项与讲座相反，户外作画才更能捕捉瞬息的光线变化。C选项把印象派户外技法错误地归给了画室。D选项错误，画室作画是被对比的对象，而非印象派想要描绘的题材。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，C是topic_adjacent，D是over_generalization。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how tardigrades survive extreme environments
items.transcript: Tardigrades are tiny animals, barely half a millimeter long, often called water bears because of the way they lumber along under a microscope. They live almost everywhere, in moss, in soil, in the deep sea. But what makes them famous is their astonishing ability to survive conditions that would kill almost anything else. Tardigrades have been frozen near absolute zero, heated past the boiling point of water, exposed to enormous doses of radiation, and even taken into the vacuum of outer space, and they have come back to life. So how do they do it? The secret is a process called cryptobiosis. When their environment dries out, a tardigrade doesn't just slow down. It essentially shuts off. It pulls in its legs, curls into a tiny ball called a tun, and expels almost all the water from its body. Its metabolism drops to a fraction of one percent of normal. In this state, it isn't really alive in the usual sense, but it isn't dead either. It can stay this way for years, even decades. Then, when water returns, the tardigrade rehydrates and walks away as if nothing happened. Researchers think a special sugar replaces the lost water and protects the animal's cells from damage. Studying how tardigrades pull this off could teach us how to preserve cells, tissues, and maybe one day even organs for medicine. These tiny survivors have a lot to teach us.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why tardigrades are commonly known as water bears
options.B: How researchers send tiny animals into outer space
options.C: The many different habitats where tardigrades can be found
options.D: How tardigrades survive extreme environments through cryptobiosis

questions.explanation: 正确答案是D。讲座主旨是缓步动物如何通过隐生这一过程在极端环境中存活。A选项只是开头解释俗名的一个细节，过于狭窄。B选项利用了讲座中提到的外太空，但研究方法不是主题。C选项是讲座顺带提到的栖息地，并非核心。干扰项类型：A是too_narrow，B是surface_word_trap，C是too_narrow。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a tardigrade's body during cryptobiosis?

options.A: It curls into a ball and expels almost all of its water
options.B: It grows larger to store extra water for later
options.C: It speeds up its metabolism to fight the dry conditions
options.D: It splits into several smaller animals to survive

questions.explanation: 正确答案是A。教授明确说在隐生状态下，缓步动物收起腿、蜷成一个叫tun的小球，并排出体内几乎所有的水分。B选项与讲座相反，它是排水而非储水变大。C选项与讲座相反，它的代谢降到正常的极小一部分，而非加快。D选项是讲座未提及的说法。干扰项类型：B是reversed_logic，C是reversed_logic，D是topic_adjacent。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention that tardigrades have been taken into the vacuum of outer space?

options.A: To suggest that tardigrades originally came from space
options.B: To give a striking example of the extreme conditions they survive
options.C: To explain how researchers first discovered cryptobiosis
options.D: To describe the typical habitats where tardigrades live

questions.explanation: 正确答案是B。教授列举外太空真空，是为了用一个极端的例子来突出缓步动物惊人的生存能力。A选项过度推断，讲座从未暗示它们来自太空。C选项错误，外太空的例子与隐生如何被发现无关。D选项错误，太空不是它们的栖息地，讲座说的栖息地是苔藓、土壤、深海。干扰项类型：A是over_inference，C是wrong_purpose，D是literal_restatement。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about studying tardigrades?

options.A: It has already failed to produce any useful results
options.B: It is mainly useful for exploring distant planets
options.C: It could help develop ways to preserve cells and tissues
options.D: It shows that tardigrades cannot survive on Earth

questions.explanation: 正确答案是C。教授说研究缓步动物如何做到这一点，可能教会我们如何保存细胞、组织乃至器官，由此可推断这类研究有助于发展细胞和组织的保存方法。A选项与讲座相反，讲座对前景持乐观态度。B选项把外太空的细节夸大成研究的主要用途。D选项与讲座相反，缓步动物本就生活在地球各处。干扰项类型：A是reversed_logic，B是topic_adjacent，D是over_generalization。

items.subject: history
items.topic: the unexpected origins of the modern fire department
items.transcript: The modern fire department did not begin as a public service. In fact, the earliest organized firefighting in many cities grew directly out of the insurance business. So picture London after the Great Fire of 1666. Thousands of buildings burned, and a young entrepreneur named Nicholas Barbon saw an opportunity. He founded one of the first fire insurance companies, and to protect the policies he sold, he assembled his own private brigade of firefighters. Now, here's the part that surprises students. To know which buildings to save, the insurer issued each customer a metal plaque, called a fire mark, which was nailed to the front of the house. When a fire broke out, the brigade would race to the scene, look for their company's mark, and only then put out the blaze. If the building carried a rival firm's mark, or no mark at all, the brigade might simply stand aside and watch it burn. As you can imagine, this created chaos. Competing brigades sometimes arrived at the same fire and argued over whose customer it was while the flames spread. Eventually, city officials recognized that a patchwork of private brigades serving paying clients was dangerous for everyone. By the nineteenth century, many cities folded these competing companies into a single, taxpayer-funded municipal force that protected every building regardless of who insured it. So the fire department we think of as a basic civic institution actually traces its roots to private profit, and it became a public good only because the private model failed so visibly.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How the fire department developed from private insurance into a public service
options.B: The destruction caused by the Great Fire of London in 1666
options.C: How modern insurance companies calculate fire-related risk
options.D: The design and placement of decorative plaques on old houses

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：讲座的核心是消防部门如何从私人保险公司的产物演变为公共服务，开头和结尾都明确点出这条主线。B错（too_narrow）：1666年大火只是引出话题的背景例子，不是主旨。C错（wrong_topic）：讲座没有讨论现代保险公司如何计算火灾风险。D错（surface_word_trap）：fire mark这个牌子被提到，但它只是细节，不是讲座主旨。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was the purpose of a fire mark?

options.A: To warn nearby residents that a building was on fire
options.B: To show a brigade which buildings its company insured
options.C: To record the date when a house was first built
options.D: To advertise the price of an insurance policy to passersby

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：教授说保险公司给客户发放金属牌（fire mark）钉在房子上，消防队据此识别哪些建筑是自家客户。A错（topic_adjacent）：它不是用来警告火情的。C错（wrong_detail）：牌子与房屋建造日期无关。D错（wrong_detail）：牌子标明客户身份，并非广告保单价格。

questions.stem: What does the professor say sometimes happened when a fire broke out?

options.A: Brigades shared their water supplies to put out large fires faster
options.B: Insurance companies refused to sell any new fire policies
options.C: Competing brigades argued over whose customer the building was
options.D: City officials immediately took control of every private brigade

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：教授说相互竞争的消防队有时会同时赶到同一处火场，为这是谁的客户而争吵，火却继续蔓延。A错（reversed_logic）：实际情况相反，他们不合作，甚至会袖手旁观。B错（topic_adjacent）：讲座没说保险公司拒绝出售保单。D错（wrong_detail）：官员接管是后来发生的整体变革，不是火灾现场立刻发生的事。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about the shift to municipal fire departments?

options.A: It was opposed by most city residents who preferred private brigades
options.B: It made fire insurance companies far more profitable than before
options.C: It happened mainly because new firefighting technology was invented
options.D: It occurred because the private, profit-driven model had clearly failed

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：教授结尾说消防成为公共服务，正是因为私人盈利模式如此明显地失败了，可合理推断这是改革动因。A错（topic_adjacent）：讲座没说居民反对，反而暗示私人模式造成混乱。B错（topic_adjacent）：没有暗示保险公司因此更赚钱。C错（over_inference）：讲座归因于私人模式的失败，而非新技术的发明。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: how dual-process theory explains fast and slow thinking
items.transcript: Think about how you answer two different questions. If I ask you what two plus two equals, the answer just pops into your head. But if I ask you to multiply seventeen by twenty-four, you have to stop and work it out. That contrast is the heart of dual-process theory, one of the most influential ideas in cognitive psychology. The theory proposes that our minds use two different systems for thinking. The first system, often called System One, is fast, automatic, and effortless. It handles things like recognizing a friend's face or reacting to a sudden noise. The second system, System Two, is slow, deliberate, and demands real mental effort. It's the one you use for that multiplication problem or for planning a budget. Now, the psychologist Daniel Kahneman popularized this framework, and he stressed that the two systems are not equally lazy. System One is always running in the background, jumping to quick conclusions, while System Two would rather conserve energy and often just accepts whatever System One suggests. Here's a classic example. A bat and a ball cost a dollar ten together. The bat costs a dollar more than the ball. How much is the ball? Most people instantly answer ten cents, but that's wrong. The correct answer is five cents. System One blurts out the easy response, and System Two, feeling lazy, fails to check it. So dual-process theory doesn't just describe two modes of thought. It also helps explain why intelligent people make predictable, avoidable mistakes when they trust their gut instead of pausing to reason carefully.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How people can train themselves to solve math problems faster
options.B: A theory describing two different systems the mind uses to think
options.C: The life and major awards of the psychologist Daniel Kahneman
options.D: Why some people are naturally more intelligent than others

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：讲座主旨是双过程理论，即大脑用两套不同系统思考。A错（too_narrow）：算术只是引出话题的例子，不是主旨。C错（wrong_topic）：Kahneman只是被提到推广了该理论，他的生平和奖项不是内容。D错（surface_word_trap）：讲座末尾提到聪明人也会犯错，但主题不是智力差异的来源。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is System One responsible for?

options.A: Carefully planning a personal budget over several weeks
options.B: Multiplying large numbers that require effort to solve
options.C: Fast, automatic tasks like recognizing a friend's face
options.D: Deliberately checking whether a quick answer is correct

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：教授说System One快速、自动、不费力，例子是认出朋友的脸或对突然的声音作出反应。A错（wrong_detail）：做预算属于费力的System Two。B错（wrong_detail）：大数相乘也是System Two。D错（reversed_logic）：核对快速答案是System Two的职责，而System Two恰恰常常偷懒不核对。

questions.stem: In the bat-and-ball example, why do most people give the wrong answer?

options.A: They misremember the total price of the bat and the ball
options.B: They have never been taught how to do basic subtraction
options.C: They deliberately choose a wrong answer in order to save time
options.D: System One blurts an easy answer System Two fails to check

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：教授解释System One脱口而出简单答案（十美分），而偷懒的System Two没有去核对它。A错（wrong_detail）：错误不是因为记错总价，而是没核对。B错（topic_adjacent）：讲座没说人们不会减法。C错（reversed_logic）：人们不是故意选错，而是没意识到要核对。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about intelligent people?

options.A: They can still make avoidable mistakes when they trust their gut
options.B: They almost never rely on System One in their daily lives
options.C: They are unable to use System Two as effectively as others
options.D: They solve the bat-and-ball problem correctly on their first try

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授结尾说该理论解释了为何聪明人在依赖直觉而非仔细推理时会犯可预测、可避免的错误，故可推断聪明人也会犯这类错。B错（over_generalization）：讲座说System One人人都在后台持续运行，不能说聪明人几乎不用它。C错（reversed_logic）：讲座没说聪明人用System Two更差。D错（topic_adjacent）：讲座说多数人答错，并未暗示聪明人能一次答对。

items.subject: ecology
items.topic: rewilding efforts to restore degraded habitats
items.transcript: Rewilding represents a major shift in how ecologists approach conservation. For most of the twentieth century, restoring a damaged habitat meant carefully managing it, planting specific trees, controlling which animals lived there, basically gardening on a large scale. Rewilding takes the opposite approach. The goal is to step back and let natural processes rebuild the ecosystem on their own, often by reintroducing key species that were lost. The most famous case is the return of gray wolves to Yellowstone National Park in 1995. Now, you might expect the wolves' main effect to be reducing the elk population, and they did. But the more striking change was behavioral. With predators around, elk stopped lingering in open river valleys where they were easy to ambush. The willows and aspens along those rivers, which elk had been overgrazing for decades, finally recovered. Beavers returned to use the willows, songbirds nested in the new growth, and the riverbanks themselves grew more stable. This chain of effects is why rewilding can be so powerful, yet it also reveals a tension. Letting nature take over means giving up a great deal of human control, and the outcomes are not always predictable. A reintroduced predator might thrive, or it might collapse, or it might reshape the landscape in ways no one intended. For ecologists, that uncertainty is precisely the point. Rewilding accepts that healthy ecosystems are dynamic and self-organizing, not static gardens that humans must constantly tend. The harder challenge is convincing the public, and policymakers, to tolerate that loss of control in exchange for ecosystems that can ultimately sustain themselves.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How gray wolves hunt elk in the river valleys of Yellowstone
options.B: Why beaver populations have declined across North America
options.C: Rewilding as an approach that lets natural processes restore ecosystems
options.D: Techniques for planting willows and aspens along damaged riverbanks

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：讲座主旨是rewilding，一种放手让自然过程自行恢复生态系统的保护方法，开头明确对比传统做法。A错（too_narrow）：狼捕食麋鹿只是说明性例子。B错（wrong_topic）：讲座说海狸回归，并未讨论海狸数量下降。D错（surface_word_trap）：种植柳树和白杨正是rewilding要避免的人为管理方式，被用作反例。

questions.stem: Why does the professor describe traditional conservation as 'gardening on a large scale'?

options.A: To suggest that ecologists should grow more plants in habitats
options.B: To explain how beavers physically reshape their environment
options.C: To restate that elk overgrazed the willows for many decades
options.D: To contrast hands-on management with rewilding's hands-off approach

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：教授用大规模园艺这个比喻来形容传统保护的精细人为管理，从而与rewilding放手的做法形成对比。A错（wrong_purpose）：他不是在建议多种植物。B错（wrong_purpose）：这个比喻与海狸改造环境无关。C错（literal_restatement）：这只是复述麋鹿过度啃食的事实，而非说明比喻的目的。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what was the more striking effect of the wolves' return?

options.A: Elk changed their behavior and avoided open river valleys
options.B: The total elk population in Yellowstone remained unchanged
options.C: Park managers began planting willows to feed the elk
options.D: Beavers were driven out of the rivers by the returning wolves

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授说比起减少麋鹿数量，更显著的变化是行为上的——麋鹿不再在开阔河谷逗留。B错（reversed_logic）：讲座说狼确实减少了麋鹿数量，并非不变。C错（wrong_detail）：柳树是自然恢复的，不是管理员种的。D错（reversed_logic）：海狸是回归利用柳树，而不是被狼赶走。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the harder challenge for rewilding?

options.A: Finding enough predator species available to reintroduce
options.B: Convincing the public and policymakers to accept a loss of control
options.C: Measuring exactly how many elk a habitat can support
options.D: Preventing reintroduced predators from leaving the protected area

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：教授结尾明确说更难的挑战是说服公众和决策者接受这种控制权的丧失，以换取能自我维持的生态系统。A错（topic_adjacent）：讲座没说找捕食者物种困难。C错（topic_adjacent）：精确测算承载量不是讲座提到的难点。D错（topic_adjacent）：阻止捕食者离开保护区未被提及。

items.subject: anthropology
items.topic: how gift-giving rituals reinforce social bonds across cultures
items.transcript: Gift-giving might look like a simple act of generosity, but anthropologists see it as one of the most powerful tools for building and maintaining social relationships. The key idea here is reciprocity. When someone gives you a gift, you feel a quiet obligation to give something back, and that ongoing exchange ties people together over time. The anthropologist Marcel Mauss explored this in his study of societies in the Pacific and beyond. He argued that a gift is never truly free. It carries an unspoken expectation of return, and that expectation is exactly what keeps relationships alive. Consider the kula ring, a famous example from the Trobriand Islands. Islanders there travel long distances by canoe to exchange shell necklaces and armbands. These objects have no practical use. You can't eat them or wear them as protection. Their entire value lies in being given away and received. A man who receives a necklace is expected to pass it onward later, not keep it. So the items circulate endlessly among the islands, and each exchange renews a web of trust and alliance across great distances. What's striking is how universal this pattern is. From holiday presents in one culture to elaborate feasts in another, the same logic appears. The specific objects differ, but the underlying function stays constant. Gift-giving is rarely just about the object itself. It's about creating an obligation, and through that obligation, a lasting bond between giver and receiver. So when we exchange gifts, we are doing something far older and more strategic than we usually realize.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How islanders build and navigate canoes for long voyages
options.B: The economic value of shell necklaces in modern markets
options.C: Why some cultures hold elaborate feasts during the holidays
options.D: How gift-giving creates obligations that strengthen social bonds

questions.explanation: 正确答案D：讲座主旨是礼物如何通过制造回报义务来建立和巩固社会关系，结尾明确点题。A错（surface_word_trap）：独木舟被提到，但造船航行不是主题。B错（wrong_topic）：贝壳项链没有实际或市场经济价值正是讲座的论点之一，主题不是其市场价值。C错（too_narrow）：节日盛宴只是说明普遍性的一个例子。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Marcel Mauss argue about gifts?

options.A: A gift carries an unspoken expectation of something in return
options.B: A gift is most meaningful when it has high practical value
options.C: A gift should always be kept rather than passed onward
options.D: A gift is given freely with no expectation attached to it

questions.explanation: 正确答案A：教授说Mauss认为礼物从不是真正免费的，它带有不言而喻的回报期待，正是这种期待维系着关系。B错（topic_adjacent）：讲座强调kula物品恰恰没有实用价值。C错（wrong_detail）：kula里收到项链的人被期望传递下去，而不是留着。D错（reversed_logic）：这与Mauss的核心观点正好相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor mention the kula ring?

options.A: To show that island travel was dangerous before modern boats
options.B: To illustrate how objects with no practical use can sustain social ties
options.C: To restate that islanders exchange necklaces and armbands by canoe
options.D: To prove that shell ornaments were a form of early currency

questions.explanation: 正确答案B：教授用kula ring作为具体例子，说明毫无实用价值的物品也能通过持续交换维系跨距离的信任与联盟。A错（wrong_purpose）：提及它不是为了讲航行危险。C错（literal_restatement）：这只是复述交换的内容，而非解释举例的目的。D错（over_inference）：教授强调这些物品的价值在于被赠予和接收，并未称其为早期货币。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is a man expected to do after receiving a kula necklace?

options.A: Trade it for food or other practical goods on his island
options.B: Keep it permanently as a sign of his personal status
options.C: Pass it onward to someone else later rather than keep it
options.D: Destroy it during a feast to demonstrate his generosity

questions.explanation: 正确答案C：教授说收到项链的人被期望日后把它传递下去，而不是留着，物品因此在岛屿间不断流转。A错（wrong_detail）：这些物品没有实用价值，不能换食物。B错（reversed_logic）：他恰恰不应永久保留它。D错（topic_adjacent）：讲座提到别的文化有盛宴，但没说要在盛宴中销毁kula物品。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: the principle behind how a lever multiplies force
items.transcript: Let's look at one of the oldest and simplest machines humans ever invented: the lever. A lever is just a rigid bar that pivots around a fixed point we call the fulcrum. What makes it so useful is that it lets you multiply the force you apply. Think about trying to lift a heavy rock by hand. Nearly impossible. But slide a sturdy bar under that rock, rest the bar on a small stone near it, and push down on the far end, and suddenly you can raise the rock with ease. So how does this actually work? The secret is distance from the fulcrum. The force you apply and the load you are lifting sit on opposite sides of the pivot. If your end of the bar is much farther from the fulcrum than the rock is, your push gets amplified. A force applied three times farther out produces roughly three times the lifting force at the load. This relationship is what we call mechanical advantage. Now, here's the catch, and it's an important one. You don't get this extra force for free. To lift the rock just a few centimeters, your end of the bar has to travel a much longer distance. The lever trades distance for force. You move farther but push less hard, while the load moves only a little, yet with great force. Energy, in other words, is conserved. Levers don't create energy; they simply let us rearrange how effort is applied, which is exactly why they show up everywhere, from scissors to wheelbarrows.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How a lever multiplies the force a person applies
options.B: The history of simple machines used in building
options.C: Why heavy rocks are so difficult to lift by hand
options.D: How mechanical devices create new energy

questions.explanation: 讲座核心是杠杆如何放大施加的力，A 正确。B 讲历史属另一话题；C 只是引入例子，过窄；D 与讲座结论相反，杠杆并不创造能量。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what determines how much a lever amplifies force?

options.A: The total weight of the bar itself
options.B: The distance of each side from the fulcrum
options.C: The material the fulcrum is made of
options.D: The height of the load being lifted

questions.explanation: 教授说放大倍数取决于离支点的距离，B 正确。A 杆的重量未提及且非关键；C 支点材料未提；D 负载高度与放大无关，属混淆。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is the trade-off when using a lever?

options.A: The bar gradually becomes weaker with use
options.B: The fulcrum must be replaced frequently
options.C: Your end of the bar must travel a longer distance
options.D: The load ends up heavier than it was before

questions.explanation: 杠杆以距离换力，你这端要走更长的距离，C 正确。A、B 未提及；D 负载不会变重，是对原理的颠倒。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about levers and energy?

options.A: Levers slowly lose energy to friction over time
options.B: Levers can produce more energy than is put in
options.C: Levers work only with extremely heavy objects
options.D: Levers redistribute effort rather than creating energy

questions.explanation: 教授强调能量守恒、杠杆只是重新分配用力，D 正确。B 与守恒相反；C 以偏概全；A 摩擦损耗未涉及。

items.subject: biology
items.topic: how tardigrades survive extreme environments
items.transcript: Today I want to introduce you to one of the toughest animals on Earth, and it's one you've almost certainly never seen: the tardigrade, often nicknamed the water bear. These creatures are tiny, less than a millimeter long, and they live in moss, soil, and sediment all over the planet.
