What makes them remarkable is their ability to survive conditions that would kill almost any other animal. When their environment dries out, tardigrades do something extraordinary. They pull in their legs, expel most of the water from their bodies, and curl into a shriveled, barrel-shaped form called a tun. In this state, their metabolism nearly stops. We call this cryptobiosis, essentially a pause button on life. A tardigrade can remain like this for years, then revive within minutes once water returns. How do they avoid the damage that usually comes with drying out? As they lose water, tardigrades produce special proteins that turn the inside of their cells into a kind of glass. This glassy state holds delicate cell structures in place so they don't collapse or break apart. Think of it like packing fragile dishes in foam instead of an empty box. This same protection helps tardigrades endure extreme cold, intense radiation, and even the vacuum of space, where some have famously survived. Now, I should be careful here: this does not mean tardigrades are indestructible. In their active, hydrated state, they are actually quite fragile. Their extraordinary toughness depends entirely on first entering that dormant tun form.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How tardigrades reproduce in moss and soil
options.B: How tardigrades survive extremely harsh conditions
options.C: Why most animals cannot live without water
options.D: How scientists first discovered tardigrades in space

questions.explanation: 全篇讲水熊虫如何在极端环境中存活，B 正确。A 繁殖未涉及；C 过窄且非主旨；D 太空是举例，借词设陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happens to a tardigrade when its environment dries out?

options.A: It quickly lays eggs before it dies
options.B: It crawls to a wetter location nearby
options.C: It expels most of its water and forms a tun
options.D: It speeds up its metabolism to stay active

questions.explanation: 干燥时它排出水分、缩成 tun，C 正确。A 产卵未提；B 移动到湿处未提；D 与代谢几乎停止相反。

questions.stem: Why does the professor compare the proteins to packing dishes in foam?

options.A: To explain how tardigrades find their food
options.B: To prove that tardigrades are truly indestructible
options.C: To describe the exact shape of the tun form
options.D: To illustrate how the proteins protect cell structures

questions.explanation: 这个比喻是为了说明蛋白质如何固定保护脆弱细胞结构，D 正确。A 觅食无关；B 夸大为坚不可摧；C 只是复述形态而非解释用意。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about tardigrades?

options.A: Their survival depends on entering the dormant state
options.B: They are equally tough whether active or dormant
options.C: They can live permanently in outer space
options.D: They lose their special proteins as they age

questions.explanation: 教授指出其坚韧完全依赖先进入休眠 tun，A 正确。B 与活跃时其实脆弱相反；C 以偏概全；D 蛋白质随龄丧失未提及。

items.subject: psychology
items.topic: the peak-end rule in how people remember experiences
items.transcript: Have you ever noticed that the way you remember an experience can be quite different from how it actually felt moment to moment? Psychologists have a name for this: the peak-end rule. The idea is that when we look back on an event, we don't average all our feelings across the whole thing. Instead, our memory is shaped mostly by two moments: the most intense point, the peak, and how the experience ended. A famous study by the psychologist Daniel Kahneman showed this clearly. Patients undergoing an uncomfortable medical procedure rated their pain in real time. Afterward, what shaped their overall memory wasn't how long the discomfort lasted. Surprisingly, a longer procedure that ended with milder discomfort was remembered as less unpleasant than a shorter one that finished at a painful peak. The ending carried enormous weight. Notice what this means. Duration barely registers in our memory, a quirk researchers call duration neglect. Two hours of mild annoyance followed by a pleasant finish can be recalled more fondly than twenty minutes that ended badly. This has real practical value. A vacation that ends on a wonderful final day will be remembered warmly, even if the middle was ordinary. A great meal ruined by a rude farewell at the door may leave a sour memory. So if you are designing any kind of experience, pay special attention to the high point and especially to the ending. Next time, I want to look at how businesses deliberately use this principle to shape customer satisfaction.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How medical procedures cause lasting pain
options.B: Why longer experiences always feel more tiring
options.C: How the peak and the ending shape our memories
options.D: How Daniel Kahneman became a famous researcher

questions.explanation: 主旨是峰值和结尾如何塑造记忆，C 正确。A 把例子当主题；B 与时长被忽略相反；D 借人名设陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what mainly determined patients' memory of the procedure?

options.A: The total length of the procedure
options.B: The skill of the attending doctor
options.C: How the procedure first began
options.D: How intense the pain was and how it ended

questions.explanation: 记忆主要由疼痛峰值和结尾决定，D 正确。A 与时长被忽略相反；B 医生水平未提；C 开头并非关键。

questions.stem: What is 'duration neglect,' according to the professor?

options.A: The tendency for length to barely affect memory
options.B: The habit of forgetting how an event ended
options.C: The failure to notice pain during a procedure
options.D: The tendency to exaggerate how long events last

questions.explanation: 时长几乎不影响记忆即 duration neglect，A 正确。B 与峰末规则相反；C 偷换为没注意疼痛；D 把忽略说成夸大，方向颠倒。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: Other studies on different kinds of physical pain
options.B: How businesses use the peak-end rule with customers
options.C: Ways to shorten unpleasant medical procedures
options.D: The early childhood of Daniel Kahneman

questions.explanation: 结尾预告将讲商家如何运用峰末规则影响顾客满意度，B 正确。A 过窄；C 与主旨方向不符；D 偏题。

items.subject: geology
items.topic: how geologists use ice cores to reconstruct ancient climates
items.transcript: Imagine being able to sample the air that animals breathed eight hundred thousand years ago. Believe it or not, geologists can do something very close to that, using ice cores. An ice core is a long cylinder of ice drilled out from a glacier or a polar ice sheet, and it is one of our most valuable records of ancient climate. Here's the basic idea. Each year, snow falls on places like Antarctica and Greenland, and it doesn't fully melt. New snow buries the old, compressing it into layers of ice. Like tree rings, these layers stack up year after year, so the deeper you drill, the further back in time you go. A single core can span hundreds of thousands of years. But the real treasure is what's trapped inside. As snow compacts into ice, it seals tiny bubbles of air. Each bubble is a genuine sample of the ancient atmosphere, telling us how much carbon dioxide and methane it contained. That is how we know greenhouse gas levels today are far higher than at any point in those records. The ice itself holds another clue. The water molecules contain different forms, or isotopes, of oxygen, and the ratio between them depends on how cold it was when the snow fell. By measuring that ratio, scientists can estimate past temperatures with surprising precision. Together, the trapped air and the isotopes let researchers reconstruct both the temperature and the atmosphere of the distant past, layer by frozen layer.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why glaciers form in cold polar regions
options.B: How carbon dioxide warms the atmosphere
options.C: How tree rings record a tree's age
options.D: How ice cores reveal ancient climate conditions

questions.explanation: 主旨是冰芯如何还原古气候，D 正确。A 冰川成因过窄；B 温室效应是另一话题；C 树轮只是类比，借词陷阱。

questions.stem: According to the professor, how do the layers in an ice core form?

options.A: New snow buries and compresses older snow each year
options.B: Meltwater refreezes into one solid block
options.C: Wind deposits dust that hardens into bands
options.D: Air bubbles push older ice toward the surface

questions.explanation: 新雪掩埋压实旧雪形成层，A 正确。B 与不完全融化相反；C 风积尘未提；D 气泡推动是臆造、方向颠倒。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what do the trapped air bubbles reveal?

options.A: The amount of snowfall in a given year
options.B: The composition of the ancient atmosphere
options.C: The depth at which the ice was drilled
options.D: The speed at which the glacier moved

questions.explanation: 气泡是古大气样本，揭示其成分，B 正确。A 降雪量并非气泡所示；C、D 钻探深度与冰川流速均无关。

questions.stem: What can be inferred about the oxygen isotopes in the ice?

options.A: They are added to the ice by drilling equipment
options.B: They show how much air pollution once existed
options.C: Their ratio serves as a record of past temperature
options.D: They make the ice melt faster than usual

questions.explanation: 同位素比值取决于降雪时的冷暖，可作古温度记录，C 正确。A 由设备加入是臆造；B 偷换为污染；D 加速融化方向颠倒。
